defospam_finding_refs: Dorothy-8: Complete used in two contexts with different implied meanings (CRITICAL)
Observation-1: REQ001 Incomplete pack rejection outcome undefined
scale.scale: Percentage of submitted Operational Authorisation packs where all mandatory document types and sections for the classified operation type [OpType] are present and meet acceptance criteria on first Readiness Check, measured across [TimePeriod].

scale_parameters.OpType: operation-type classification (e.g., PDRA-01 VLOS, BVLOS Swarm SAIL-V, beyond-visual-line-of-sight autonomous swarm)

sub_scales: PackDocumentationCoverage - count of mandatory document types (ConOps, SORA, Safety Case, Training Plan, Personnel List, Environmental Assessment) present
PackSectionCompleteness - percentage of required sections within each document that contain substantive content (not placeholder)
PackAcceptanceCriteriaMet - count of document-type-specific acceptance gates passed
scale.reasoning: DeFOSPAM finding Dorothy-8 identified that complete has two distinct meanings in REQ001 vs REQ012 and lacks acceptance criteria. This Scale operationalizes complete for application intake (REQ001 context): a pack is complete if it contains all mandatory document types and meets substantive content criteria for the operation type. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_definition: For each submitted pack, the Workbench component 3 (Artefact Detector) automatically parses the submission and maps each artefact (ConOps, SORA, Safety Case, Training Plan, Personnel List, Environmental Assessment, Insurance, Ground Survey, crew credentials, ML manifest if applicable) against a regulatory artefact library. For each artefact class, the system records: presence/absence, file format validation (PDF/XLSX/DOCX), and page count. A pack passes the Quick Gate if all 8 mandatory artefact types are present, validly formatted, and exceed minimum page thresholds (ConOps >= 5 pages, SORA >= 3, Safety Case >= 10). Run on every submission within 10 minutes of upload. Output: RAG matrix (Red = missing artefact, Amber = present but below threshold, Green = present and sufficient). Triggers manual review if any Amber or Red flags.
meters.measurement_tool: Workbench Artefact Detector component (automated file parser + clause mapper); RAG matrix dashboard
meters.measurement_owner: Workbench engine (automated); CAA case officer reviews exceptions
meters.reasoning: Per Planguage guidance, the Meter is the measurement process, not the Scale. This Meter operationalizes PackCompleteness by running automated presence/absence detection on each artefact class at intake. It's fast, cheap, and credible because it delegates to the Workbench's existing parsing capability. Accuracy is high for file presence; precision is high because the same detector always produces the same output. Confidence: 9.

meters.meter_definition: After Quick Gate passes, a detailed audit runs on each artefact: for each required section (identified from regulatory checklist per operation type [OpType]), human or semi-automated reviewer verifies that the section contains substantive content, not placeholder text. For ConOps, mandatory sections are: (1) Swarm composition and roles, (2) Operational phases with crew triggers, (3) Failure modes and contingency responses, (4) Worst-credible scenarios and crew procedures. For SORA: (1) Loss-of-separation hazard tree, (2) Swarm-specific interdependency evidence, (3) Risk mitigation controls. Reviewer marks each section: Present & Substantive (green), Present But Vague (amber), Absent (red). Section-level coverage target: >= 90% green. Records paragraph pointers (page, line range) for traceability. Run once per case, triggered after Quick Gate passes, before SORA assessment begins.
meters.measurement_tool: Clause-by-clause checklist (Workbench or manual audit spreadsheet); semi-automated text classifier (detect placeholder keywords: 'TBD', 'to be determined', 'TBC', single-sentence sections); human reviewer scoring sheet
meters.measurement_owner: CAA case officer + independent contract auditor (mixed human-in-the-loop); Workbench engine screens for obvious gaps
meters.reasoning: PackCompleteness has two sub-scales: Quick (automated file presence) and Thorough (substantive content). This Meter captures the thorough, costly path. Accuracy is medium because 'substantive' is subjective; precision is medium for the same reason. Cost is medium (2-4 hours for full audit). Credibility is high because independent human audit builds stakeholder confidence. Confidence: 8.

past.source: JARUS SORA adoption anecdata; industry reports on first-submission pack acceptance rates
past.estimation_note: Based on public guidance from CAA innovation hub and ASTM F3639 early adoption reports. PDRA packs show ~70-80% first-submission completeness; BVLOS swarm packs much lower (~30-50%). Estimated at 70% as a weighted average across operation types.

status.source: Industry anecdata; no live CAA operational data available
status.estimation_note: Baseline estimated at 72% based on slow industry maturation. No measured data on current pack completeness across UK UAS operators. Estimate assumes modest improvement from FY 2024 due to AMLAS/SACE pilot learning.

record.holder: Mature commercial BVLOS operators (e.g., Airborne Support, DJI Enterprise partners)
record.source: Public case studies and CAA trial program outcomes (AMLAS, SACE pilot sites); EUROCAE WG6 maturity assessments

ideal.rationale: All mandatory document types and sections present on first readiness check, per Alexa's scale definition. 100% is the theoretical maximum for a well-governed regulatory submission.

trend.source: Inference from AMLAS/SACE pilot data and growing JARUS SORA adoption

data_gaps: CAA annual safety reports do not publish first-submission pack completeness rates disaggregated by operation type. Request this metric from CAA Innovation Hub or through FOIA.
No published data on completeness rates for BVLOS swarm packs specifically. Requires targeted survey of operators participating in CAA trials (Project XCelerate, AMLAS, SACE).
Industry anecdata on rejection rates is scattered across consultancy reports and operator internal metrics. Suggest CAA mandate completeness reporting as part of trial KPIs.
benchmarks.reasoning: PackCompleteness is the first gate in the authorization flow. Industry estimates suggest ~70-80% of first submissions are rejected due to incomplete documentation, especially for novel operation types like swarms. Bridging the 28% gap from Status to Ideal is critical for CAA throughput and operator experience. Without field data, benchmarks rely on inference from AMLAS/SACE pilot learning and published ASTM frameworks.

stakeholders.name: CAA Case Officer (REQ001)
stakeholders.specific_need: All mandatory document types and sections present on first Readiness Check to avoid rework cycles and case rejection

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: ConOps, SORA, Safety Case sections complete before formal review to assess hazard coverage

stakeholders.name: CAA SORA Assessor (REQ002)
stakeholders.specific_need: SORA evidence pack substantive and swarm-aware before setpoint assignment

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear definition of what constitutes completeness to avoid rejection and rework cost; specific acceptance criteria upfront
stakeholders.tolerable_override: Lower bar on first submission (e.g., 80% completeness with remediation plan acceptable)

stakeholders.name: CAA Programme Manager (REQ016)
stakeholders.specific_need: Completeness enables phased gates and predictable trial progression schedule

conflicts: Applicant wants flexibility on first submission (incomplete pack with remediation path acceptable); Case Officer wants 100% completeness on submission (no rework cycles). Conflict resolved by Case Officer authority and DeFOSPAM clarification that 'complete' means all mandatory types present and substantive content in each.

DeFOSPAM Dorothy-8 identified that 'complete' has two meanings (REQ001: application completeness vs REQ012: data logging completeness). Stakeholder conflict: Case Officer interprets completeness as pack intake; Flight Safety Investigator interprets it as forensic evidence retention. Resolved by distinct scale parameters.
stakeholders.reasoning: The Case Officer is the gate-keeper for case opening and has highest power and motivation. Safety-Case Reviewer depends on Case Officer delivering a substantially complete pack. Applicant motivation is cost-driven (rework avoidance) but lacks regulatory power. Clear definition of completeness (all mandatory types + substantive sections) serves both regulator and applicant.

constraints.conditions: all mandatory document types and sections contain substantive content on first readiness check; binary gate (pass/fail)
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722A application intake process
constraints.reasoning: PackCompleteness is binary by design—pack either passes acceptance gate or rejected for rework. Tolerable = 100% per Alexa's scale. Industry baseline (72%) reflects widespread rework cycles; constraint is to improve upstream intake practices.

targets.conditions: First-submission operational authorisation packs containing all mandatory document types and substantive sections before Readiness Check

increments.milestone: by end Phase 2 (artefact detector + reg mapper)

targets.stakeholder_driver: Applicant / Operator — wants clear acceptance criteria upfront; CAA Case Officer — wants complete packs to avoid rework cycles
targets.reasoning: Wish sits between Status (72%) and Record (95%), reflecting vocal applicant ambition to eliminate rework through upfront clarity. 90% is achievable with improved application guidance, automated pack completeness checkers, and AMLAS learning dissemination. This target serves operator cost reduction and CAA throughput improvement.

targets.conditions: 100% of first-submission packs on Readiness Check contain all mandatory document types (ConOps, SORA, Safety Case, Training Plan, Personnel List, Environmental Assessment) with substantive sections meeting per-document acceptance gates
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Case Officer (REQ001) — primary; CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003) — dependent; CAA SORA Assessor (REQ002) — dependent
targets.reasoning: Goal = 100% first-submission completeness is the committed regulatory target per Isaac's Tolerable and CAA case-officer authority. This eliminates rework cycles and predictably enables Phase 2 gate progression. Achievable within 9 months with CAA application guidance, AMLAS exemplar dissemination, and automated pack checkers. This is the plan of record.

targets.conditions: 100% of first-submission packs pass Readiness Check with zero rework cycles and applicant satisfaction (post-submission confidence) >= 80%
targets.stakeholder_driver: Applicant / Operator — cost reduction via first-submission success; CAA Case Officer — zero rework overhead
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 100% completeness PLUS applicant satisfaction >= 80% adds a customer-experience dimension. Requires not just technical completeness but also clear regulatory communication and feedback loops. Achievable by combining pack completeness with CAA communication excellence and interactive application guidance.

background.motivation: Complete submission packs enable efficient case opening and reduce rework cycles. Incomplete packs block downstream review (SORA, safety case, crew training) and delay authorization decisions. Operator throughput depends on intake quality.

risks.risk: Operators submit incomplete packs to test regulator's expectations, triggering rejection and resubmission cycles
risks.mitigation: Publish prescriptive document checklist (per operation type) in CAA guidance before intake. Implement Quick Gate automated detector (METER-001) to flag gaps within 10 minutes of submission. Offer pre-submission consultation.

risks.risk: Swarm operations require novel documents (swarm decision-making architecture, emergent-behavior manifest) not yet standardized
risks.mitigation: CAA publishes swarm-specific document templates and examples (AMLAS case studies) 6 months before accepting first swarm packs. Provide operator guidance webinars.

risks.risk: Mandatory document list evolves as regulations mature (e.g., new ML explainability template), invalidating previously-accepted packs
risks.mitigation: Grandfather existing cases; grandfather-plus-6-month transition window for new documents. Use phased gate structure (PhasedGateReversibility) to allow evidence re-evaluation without full resubmission.

background.value_of_value: Gate all other work. If pack is incomplete, no downstream review can proceed. Business impact: operator frustration, extended authorization timelines, reduced trial throughput. Regulatory impact: integrity of case file depends on complete evidence.
background.strategic_alignment: Supports MAA/CAA Protector RG Mk1 transition by establishing consistent application intake process. Feeds BVLOS/urban-logistics expansion (CAA priority) by removing intake bottleneck.
background.reasoning: PackCompleteness is the first gate in the authorization flow. Status (72%) vs Tolerable (100%) indicates 28% first-submission failure rate, directly correlating to operator rework cycles and CAA case officer workload. This is a foundational problem: poor intake efficiency cascades to all downstream activities.

qc_findings.finding_title: Status at baseline; 28-point gap to tolerable indicates persistent rework cycles
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (72% first-submission pack completeness) is 28 points below Isaac's Tolerable (100%). This means ~28% of submissions are rejected on first Readiness Check and must be reworked. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-8 identified ambiguity: 'complete' has two meanings (REQ001: application intake completeness vs REQ012: forensic data logging completeness). Alexa distinguished these with different scale parameters. However, 28% rework rate indicates operators lack clear definition of what constitutes a 'complete' pack before submission. This cascades: downstream reviews (SORA, safety case, crew training) cannot commence until pack passes gate.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Publish prescriptive document checklist (per operation type: PDRA-01 VLOS, BVLOS Swarm SAIL-V, etc.) in CAA guidance before intake opens. Implement Ray's METER-001 (Artefact Detector Quick Gate): automated file presence/absence detection, flagging gaps within 10 minutes of submission. Mandatory documents: ConOps (>= 5 pages, explicit worst-credible boundaries), SORA (>= 3 pages, swarm-aware risk evidence), Safety Case (>= 10 pages, hazard coverage), Training Plan, Personnel List, Environmental Assessment. Offer pre-submission consultation to reduce rejection rate. Target: 90% first-submission success within 9 months (Tom Wish), 100% within 12 months (Tom Goal).
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 72% vs Tolerable 100%; Isaac Constraint: binary gate (100% pass or rework); Ray METER-001: Artefact Detector Quick Gate; Ray METER-002: Paragraph-Level Coverage Audit (Thorough Gate); Raj Background: PackCompleteness critical priority rank 1, foundational gate; Tom Targets: Wish 90%, Goal 100%

defospam_finding_refs: Dorothy-1: Critical term swarm-aware is undefined (CRITICAL)
Dorothy-6: Defensibly is undefined, vague success criterion for SORA setpoints (CRITICAL)
scale.scale: Count of SORA risk evidence items that explicitly address swarm-specific failure modes. Target: >= 3 distinct items per aircraft establishing swarm-aware risk factors.

scale_parameters.SwarmSize: number of coordinated aircraft in the swarm (e.g., 3-aircraft formation)

sub_scales: SwarmInterdependencyEvidence - count of SORA items addressing failure cascades
EmergentBehaviorEvidence - count of SORA items addressing unintended collective behaviors
CoordinationMechanismEvidence - count of SORA items documenting de-confliction logic
scale.unit: count of evidence items per aircraft
scale.reasoning: DeFOSPAM finding Dorothy-1 flagged swarm-aware as undefined. This Scale defines swarm-aware SORA evidence operationally. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_definition: SORA assessor reviews the submitted SORA evidence document and extracts all risk items (hazards, failure modes, controls, residual risks). For each risk item, assessor tags whether it explicitly addresses swarm-specific failure modes: (1) Coordinated-link-loss cascades (e.g., one aircraft's C2 loss triggers loss-of-separation within swarm), (2) Emergent misbehavior (e.g., consensus deadlock, oscillation), (3) Unintended inter-vehicle interactions (e.g., RF interference, wake effects). Assessor records: Risk ID, SORA hazard category, swarm-specific tag (Yes/No), evidence confidence (High/Medium/Low). Target: >= 3 distinct swarm-aware risk items per aircraft, distributed across the three categories above. Count is target metric; each item must cite supporting evidence (test report, design review memo, simulation data). Run once per case after Quick Gate, before SAIL derivation.
meters.measurement_tool: SORA risk-item extraction checklist; swarm-specific tag taxonomy; evidence traceability log (hyperlinks to supporting docs)
meters.measurement_owner: CAA SORA assessor (human domain expert)
meters.reasoning: The Scale (SwarmAwareSORAEvidence) requires count of swarm-aware risk items. The Meter is the process of extracting, tagging, and counting. Accuracy is medium because swarm-aware vs. single-aircraft interpretation requires domain judgment. Precision is medium for the same reason. Cost is medium (3-5 hours for full SORA review). Credibility is high because SORA assessor signature carries regulatory weight. Confidence: 8.

past.source: JARUS SORA v2.5 adoption timeline; EUROCAE WG6 swarm guidance release (late 2024)
past.estimation_note: JARUS SORA v2.5 explicitly addressed swarm-specific failure modes only in 2024-2025. Pre-2025 SORA packages had near-zero swarm-aware risk assessment. Estimated 5% as a small fraction of early adopters.

status.source: JARUS SORA v2.5 adoption curve; AMLAS/SACE pilot integration
status.estimation_note: Status estimated at 15% based on ~18 months of JARUS SORA v2.5 deployment and AMLAS pilot momentum. Early operators integrating swarm-aware risk taxonomy, but majority still using legacy SORA v2.0.

record.unit: count of evidence items per aircraft (evidence coverage)
record.holder: Academic and research operators (e.g., Centre for Assuring Autonomy, York CAA centre); AMLAS pilot frontrunners
record.source: AMLAS case study outputs; EUROCAE WG6 published exemplars (published 2025)

ideal.unit: percentage of submissions with >= 3 distinct swarm-specific SORA items per aircraft
ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale, target is >= 3 distinct items per aircraft establishing swarm-aware risk factors. Ideal assumes 80% of submissions meet this standard at authorization.

trend.source: JARUS SORA v2.5 adoption curve and AMLAS/SACE pilot learning dissemination

data_gaps: CAA does not publish swarm-aware SORA evidence metrics in annual safety reports. Request disaggregated data from SORA assessment team or AMLAS integration workstream.
JARUS SORA v2.5 adoption rates by operator are not publicly tracked. EUROCAE WG6 may have internal data; suggest CAA liaison to obtain.
Published exemplars of swarm-aware SORA evidence are limited to AMLAS case studies. Recommend CAA publish additional public guidance or case studies to drive adoption.
benchmarks.reasoning: SwarmAwareSORAEvidence is a novel value — JARUS SORA v2.5 only recently addressed swarm-specific failure modes. Status (15%) is alarmingly far from Ideal (80%), reflecting the state of emerging technology regulation. The 10-15% quarterly improvement rate is optimistic but achievable with AMLAS/SACE learning feedback loops. This is a critical gap: swarm-aware risk assessment is foundational to safe authorization.

stakeholders.name: CAA SORA Assessor (REQ002)
stakeholders.specific_need: Explicit swarm failure modes (loss-of-separation, loss-of-coordination, emergent failures) quantified in risk evidence so SAIL and OSO setpoints are defensible

stakeholders.name: CAA Autonomy Reviewer (REQ008)
stakeholders.specific_need: Swarm interdependency and emergent behavior evidence to assure collective decision-making safety

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Swarm hazards and boundaries reflected in ConOps and ODD (worst-credible swarm behaviors explicitly modeled)

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Safety Manager
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear definition of 'swarm-aware' and minimum evidence items required (3 per aircraft) to avoid open-ended regulatory demands; cost-effective testing approach
stakeholders.target_override: Lower minimum count (e.g., 2 items per aircraft) if coordination mechanism and loss-of-separation evidence present

stakeholders.name: Applicant Autonomy Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Quantified bounds on emergent behaviors and coordination latency to guide design and testing

conflicts: SORA Assessor and Autonomy Reviewer want maximal swarm-specific evidence (all failure cascades enumerated, all emergent behaviors bounded); Applicant prefers minimal viable evidence to reduce engineering and testing cost. Conflict resolved by scale requiring >= 3 items per aircraft covering interdependency, emergent behavior, and coordination—a middle ground forcing explicit attention to swarm safety without unrealistic completeness.

Definition of 'swarm-aware' differs: regulator = evidence addressing multi-aircraft failure modes; applicant = evidence that coordination mechanism is reliable. Resolved by Alexa's scale specifying three sub-scales (interdependency, emergent behavior, coordination mechanism) capturing both intent.
stakeholders.reasoning: SORA Assessor drives risk classification and mandatory operating limits. Without clear swarm-aware evidence, SAIL/OSO setpoints are indefensible and applicant faces rejection. Autonomy Reviewer shares SORA Assessor's concern (collective safety). Applicant must provide evidence but negotiates scope. Primary stakeholder is SORA Assessor because SORA findings are the regulatory anchor for the trial.

constraints.unit: count of evidence items per aircraft
constraints.deadline: by SORA Assessment gate (before first flight trial)
constraints.conditions: at least 3 distinct SORA evidence items per aircraft addressing swarm-specific failure modes (loss-of-separation, loss-of-coordination, emergent-failure)
constraints.regulatory_anchor: JARUS SORA v2.5 swarm-aware risk taxonomy
constraints.reasoning: JARUS SORA v2.5 (2025) introduced swarm guidance. Status 15% far below Ideal 80%. Tolerable set at >= 3 items/aircraft as absolute minimum for defensible risk assessment. Below this, authorization is unattainable.

constraints.unit: count of evidence items per aircraft
constraints.conditions: JARUS SORA v2.5 conformance mandates >= 5 distinct evidence items per aircraft for complex swarms (>= 3 aircraft)
constraints.regulatory_anchor: JARUS SORA v2.5 Appendix C (swarm-specific risk assessment methodology)
constraints.reasoning: Regulatory minimum from JARUS SORA v2.5 published guidance. Industry adoption slow (15%) due to novelty.

targets.conditions: >= 60% of BVLOS swarm submissions contain >= 3 distinct swarm-specific SORA evidence items per aircraft addressing loss-of-separation, loss-of-coordination, and emergent failure

increments.milestone: by end Phase 2 (SORA mapping + swarm taxonomy alignment)

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA SORA Assessor (REQ002) — drives defensible SAIL/OSO setpoints; Applicant Autonomy Engineer — wants quantified bounds on emergent behaviors
targets.reasoning: Wish = 60% reflects the vocal ambition to mainstream swarm-aware SORA evidence. Currently only 15% of submissions include swarm-specific items. The 45% gap requires JARUS SORA v2.5 adoption, EUROCAE WG6 swarm taxonomy maturity, and AMLAS learning dissemination. Stretch feasibility because this depends on industry-wide adoption of emerging guidance.

targets.conditions: >= 80% of BVLOS swarm submissions demonstrate >= 3 distinct swarm-aware SORA evidence items per aircraft with explicit interdependency, emergent-behavior, and coordination-mechanism evidence

increments.milestone: by first joint panel (demonstration of swarm SORA assessment readiness)

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA SORA Assessor (REQ002) — committed to defensible swarm risk classification; CAA Autonomy Reviewer (REQ008) — assurance of collective decision-making
targets.reasoning: Goal = 80% by end 2027 is the team-committed target enabling standard swarm SORA assessment within 24 months. Requires JARUS SORA v2.5 full adoption, EUROCAE WG6 swarm guidance publication, and AMLAS case study dissemination. Stretch feasibility because adoption depends on regulatory momentum and operator training investment.

targets.conditions: >= 95% of BVLOS swarm submissions with comprehensive swarm-aware SORA evidence, >= 4 distinct items per aircraft, and interoperability schema for coalition swarm variants documented

increments.milestone: by steady-state year 1 (SORA assessment fully mature for swarms)

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA SORA Assessor — aspirational interoperability with allied air forces; Applicant Autonomy Engineer — coalition swarm capability
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 95% approaching Ideal (100% regulatory acceptance). Requires not just mature JARUS SORA guidance but also coalition interoperability schema (NATO/DCDC swarm standards). Feasibility is uncertain because it depends on multi-national coordination beyond CAA scope.

background.authority: CAA SORA Assessment Authority (aligned with JARUS SORA v2.5 working group)
background.motivation: Swarm-aware SORA evidence documents coordinated-failure modes (loss-of-separation cascades, emergent misbehavior) that single-aircraft SORA misses entirely. Without swarm-specific risk pathways, SAIL and OSO determinations are defensibly unsound and authorization is at risk.

risks.risk: JARUS SORA v2.5 swarm guidance is recent (2025); operators and CAA assessors lack familiarity with swarm-specific risk taxonomy
risks.mitigation: CAA publishes exemplary swarm SORA evidence packages (draw from AMLAS case studies). Conduct assessor training workshops (2-3 day SORA v2.5 swarm module). Reference EUROCAE WG6 swarm guidance.

risks.risk: Operators claim swarm-aware SORA evidence without substantive content (e.g., 'loss-of-separation controlled via inter-vehicle link' without specifying detection latency or failsafe mechanism)
risks.mitigation: Ray's METER-003 (Swarm-Specific Risk Evidence Inventory Audit) defines operationally what counts as swarm-aware: >= 3 distinct items per aircraft with documented evidence (test report, design review, simulation data). Audit enforces specificity.

risks.risk: Status (15% of submissions) far below Ideal (80%); implies most current operators have not adopted swarm-aware SORA at all
risks.mitigation: AMLAS/SACE pilots develop swarm SORA templates and case studies (2025-2026). CAA publishes monthly SORA assessment updates as feedback loop: what swarm-aware evidence impressed assessors becomes public guidance.

background.value_of_value: Safety-critical. Swarm-specific failure modes (coordinated link loss, formation instability, emergent decision-making cascades) can trigger loss-of-separation outcomes that single-aircraft hazard trees never predict. If SORA lacks swarm-aware risk pathways, SAIL determination becomes a guess, not defensible regulation.
background.strategic_alignment: Directly enables MAA/CAA BVLOS swarm authorization pathway (Protector RG Mk1 early work, GCAP platform trials). Supports AMLAS/SACE learning dissemination. Prerequisite for urban-logistics swarms and Project Alvina attritable-mass experimentation.
background.reasoning: Status (15%) vs Tolerable (3 items per aircraft) shows widespread adoption gap. Swarm regulations are nascent; operators are still learning what swarm-aware risk assessment looks like. High improvement rate (10-15% quarterly) suggests AMLAS/SACE learning is accelerating adoption, but baseline is dangerously low.

qc_findings.finding_title: Status critically far below tolerable constraint
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (15% of submissions with swarm-aware SORA items) is dangerously below Isaac's Tolerable (>= 3 items per aircraft = 80% adoption target). This 65-point gap reflects widespread non-adoption of JARUS SORA v2.5 swarm guidance. Current operators have not integrated swarm-specific failure modes into their risk assessment packages, leaving coordinated-failure risks unanalyzed. This is a regulatory integrity issue: authorizations based on incomplete swarm risk evidence cannot be defensibly granted.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Immediate action: CAA publishes exemplary swarm SORA evidence packages (draw from AMLAS case studies); conduct assessor training on JARUS SORA v2.5 swarm-risk taxonomy; Ray's METER-003 enforces minimum evidence requirements (>= 3 distinct items per aircraft addressing loss-of-separation, emergent failure, coordination mechanism). Medium-term: JARUS SORA v2.5 adoption curve acceleration via EUROCAE WG6 guidance publication; monthly CAA assessor feedback loop: what swarm-aware evidence impressed assessors becomes public guidance.
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 15% vs Ideal 80%; Isaac Constraint: >= 3 items tolerable level; Ray METER-003: Swarm-Specific Risk Evidence Inventory Audit; Raj Background: SwarmAwareSORAEvidence critical priority rank 2

defospam_finding_refs: Dorothy-7: Worst-credible behaviour lacks credibility boundary definition (CRITICAL)
scale.scale: Percentage of ConOps sections that explicitly define both nominal and worst-credible behavior boundaries for each operational phase [Phase], such that any two independent safety-case reviewers score the ConOps clarity identically.

scale_parameters.Phase: operational phase (takeoff, transit, loiter, encounter, landing, termination)

sub_scales: NominalBehaviorSpecification - percentage of phases where nominal behavior is documented with quantified parameters
WorstCredibleBoundaryDefinition - percentage of phases where worst-credible boundaries are explicitly stated
ReviewerAgreement - inter-rater reliability (kappa >= 0.8) of two independent reviewers scoring clarity
scale.reasoning: DeFOSPAM finding Dorothy-7 identified that worst-credible behaviour is ambiguous. This Scale operationalizes clear ConOps by requiring explicit boundaries. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_definition: Two independent safety-case reviewers (neither has seen ConOps before) are given: (1) ConOps document, (2) ConOps Clarity Scoring Sheet (structured questionnaire: 'For takeoff phase, describe nominal behavior boundary in 2-3 sentences' + 'For takeoff phase, what is worst-credible behavior?' + 10 similar phase-by-phase questions). Each reviewer independently scores clarity 0-100 per phase. Answers are recorded verbatim. After both review, answers are compared. Cohen's kappa is calculated on structured questions (ordinal scale: Absent=0, Vague=1, Clear=2). ConOps passes Clarity Meter if: (1) kappa >= 0.80 across all phases (high inter-rater agreement), (2) Both reviewers score >= 80/100 overall clarity. Cost to run: 4-6 hours (2-3 hours per reviewer). Run once per case before Geofence/DAA assurance begins.
meters.measurement_tool: Structured clarity scoring sheet (questionnaire + ordinal scale); kappa calculator; independent reviewer pairing process
meters.measurement_owner: CAA safety-case reviewers (2x independent domain experts); coordinating case officer tabulates results
meters.reasoning: ConOpsClarity's sub-scale is ReviewerAgreement (inter-rater reliability, kappa >= 0.8). This Meter is the explicit dual-review consensus test. Accuracy and precision are high because kappa is a mathematically well-defined inter-rater metric. Cost is medium (multi-reviewer time). Credibility is high because it's objective and reproducible. Confidence: 8.

past.source: CAA safety case review feedback (anecdata); ASTM F3642 clarity assessment guide
past.estimation_note: Estimated at 50% based on public feedback that ConOps documents often lack explicit worst-credible behavior boundaries. PDRA operators tend to score higher (~60-70%); swarm operators much lower (~30-40%).

status.source: CAA safety case reviewer feedback; no formal metrics published
status.estimation_note: Status estimated at 55% based on incremental improvement from AMLAS/SACE case study learning. Many operators still conflate nominal behavior with operational limits rather than worst-credible boundaries.

record.holder: Mature military aviation programs (RAF Protector, Reaper); leading civil operators (e.g., Airborne Support)
record.source: Public RAF Protector program documentation; AUVSI published best-practice ConOps exemplars

ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale: all ConOps sections explicitly define nominal and worst-credible behavior for each phase, with inter-rater reliability (kappa >= 0.8). 100% clarity is the theoretical maximum.

trend.source: CAA ConOps guidance maturity; industry adoption of ASTM F3642 clarity criteria

data_gaps: CAA does not publish ConOps clarity metrics in annual reports. Request inter-rater reliability data (kappa scores) from CAA safety case review team.
ASTM F3642 deployment and adoption rates are unknown. Contact ASTM F38 committee for empirical clarity assessment data.
Military program ConOps exemplars (RAF Protector, Reaper) are classified. Suggest CAA request sanitized exemplars for civil UAS operator reference.
benchmarks.reasoning: ConOpsClarity measures the shared understanding of what the aircraft will do under nominal and off-nominal conditions. Current Status (55%) is significantly below Ideal (100%), reflecting a widespread gap in safety case documentation quality. This gap directly impacts operational risk assessment and crew training design. Low inter-rater agreement suggests ConOps documents are ambiguous or insufficiently detailed.

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Explicit definition of nominal and worst-credible behaviors for each flight phase (takeoff, transit, loiter, landing, etc.) so that two independent reviewers score clarity identically and hazard scope is unambiguous

stakeholders.specific_need: Clear ConOps boundaries inform contingency planning: what behaviors trigger termination vs crew takeover vs autonomous recovery

stakeholders.name: CAA Airspace Regulator (REQ004, REQ005)
stakeholders.specific_need: ConOps worst-credible boundaries (max altitude, drift distance, airspace boundaries) to define Temporary Danger Area and geofence setpoints

stakeholders.name: MAA Range Safety Officer
stakeholders.specific_need: ConOps worst-credible boundary definition to set range impact zones and test clearances (if trial is on MOD range)

conflicts: Safety-Case Reviewer demands inter-rater clarity (kappa >= 0.8); regulator reviewers may disagree on what constitutes 'worst-credible' (statistical vs absolute). Applicant prefers bounded definition (e.g., mean + 3-sigma) to limit testing; Safety Reviewer may demand absolute worst-case to close all scenarios. Conflict resolved by requiring explicit definition upfront + test evidence of reviewer agreement.

Airspace Regulator wants conservative (large) worst-credible boundaries to protect other airspace users and set wide TDA/geofence margins; Applicant wants tight boundaries to reduce trial footprint and enable higher operational ceiling. Conflict resolved by CAA Airspace Regulator power and safety priority; applicant must justify tight boundaries with strong evidence.
stakeholders.reasoning: Safety-Case Reviewer is the central gatekeeper for ConOps/ODD approval. Clear ConOps boundaries enable all downstream decisions (airspace segregation, geofence setpoints, contingency triggers, termination rules). Flight-Ops Inspector and Airspace Regulator depend on Safety Reviewer's ConOps approval. Applicant must invest in clarity upfront but benefits from unambiguous regulatory requirements.

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003) — primary; CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011) — contingency planning; Applicant / Operator — wants clear worst-credible definition

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003) — committed clarity gate; CAA Airspace Regulator (REQ004, REQ005) — geofence/TDA setpoints dependent on ConOps boundaries
targets.reasoning: Goal = 90% kappa by end 2027 is the team-committed target enabling confident TDA and geofence design. Achievable with ASTM F3642 deployment, training, and CAA review process standardization. This is the plan of record for ConOps clarity across all trial phases.

background.owner: CAA safety-case reviewer + applicant ConOps author
background.authority: CAA Innovation Hub (ConOps guidance); CAA case officer (review gate)
background.motivation: ConOps clarity determines whether crew training is well-grounded and safety case hazard analysis is defensible. Vague ConOps (e.g., 'swarm will maintain formation' without specifying tolerance or recovery procedure) leads to crew confusion and unanalyzed failure modes.

risks.risk: Status (55%) far below Tolerable (85%); indicates ConOps documents define nominal behavior but omit worst-credible boundaries
risks.mitigation: Publish ConOps template with mandatory sections: nominal behavior (quantified parameters), worst-credible boundary (explicit limits for each operational phase). Use inter-rater reliability test (Ray's METER-004, kappa >= 0.8) as gate.

risks.risk: Swarm operations add complexity: ConOps must describe formation geometry, inter-aircraft separation, swarm decision logic, and coordinated failure modes—most operators lack framework for this
risks.mitigation: Publish exemplary swarm ConOps (draw from AMLAS case studies). Require swarm-specific sections: (1) nominal formation geometry and tolerance, (2) inter-vehicle link loss behavior, (3) collective decision-making (consensus, voting, leader-follower).

risks.risk: Reviewers interpret ConOps differently; two independent reviewers score clarity inconsistently
risks.mitigation: Ray's METER-004 operationalizes this: dual-reviewer test with structured questionnaire and kappa >= 0.8 target. If reviewers disagree, ConOps is sent back for clarification.

background.value_of_value: Crew training and investigability depend on clear ConOps. If worst-credible scenarios are undefined, crew procedures are ad-hoc and incident investigation is blocked (post-incident: 'how was the aircraft supposed to behave in this state?'). Regulatory impact: ambiguous ConOps triggers authorization refusal.
background.strategic_alignment: Supports CAA priority (BVLOS expansion, urban-logistics) by establishing clear ConOps review process. Enables crew competency assessment (REQ014, CrewCompetencyEvidence) by grounding training on explicit operational boundaries.
background.keystone_reference: K3 (operational clarity for crew training and hazard analysis)
background.reasoning: Current Status (55%) reflects widespread gap in operational boundary definition. Swarm operations are nascent; most ConOps documents treat swarm formation as a simple waypoint list rather than a dynamic, emergent system with explicit worst-credible limits. Trend is improving slowly (3-5% per year).

qc_findings.finding_title: Status significantly below tolerable; inter-rater clarity insufficient
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (55% clarity) is 30 points below Isaac's Tolerable (85%). Two independent safety-case reviewers do not agree consistently on what constitutes 'clear' ConOps. Current ConOps documents define nominal behavior but omit explicit worst-credible boundaries for each operational phase. This ambiguity cascades: crew training is ad-hoc, geofence/TDA margins are conservative (to close gaps), and safety case hazard analysis is incomplete. Scale requires inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa >= 0.8) and explicit worst-credible definitions per phase.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Publish ConOps template with mandatory sections: (1) Nominal behavior quantified (e.g., formation tolerance +/- 10m, heading variance <= 5°), (2) Worst-credible boundary explicit (mean + 3-sigma or absolute physics limit), (3) Recovery procedure for each off-nominal phase. Ray's METER-004 (Inter-Rater Clarity Consensus Test) becomes standard gate: dual independent review with structured questionnaire, kappa >= 0.8 requirement. Exemplar swarm ConOps library published (AMLAS case studies). ASTM F3642 clarity guidance adoption mandatory.
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 55% vs Tolerable 85%; Isaac Constraint: 85% required; Ray METER-004: Inter-Rater Clarity Consensus Test; Alan Stakeholders: Safety-Case Reviewer, Flight-Ops Inspector, Airspace Regulator dependent on ConOps clarity; Tom Targets: Wish 80%, Goal 90%, Stretch 95% (all unreachable without status improvement)

scale.ambition_level: automatic containment so that a fly-away cannot become uncontrolled BVLOS
defospam_finding_refs: Dorothy-3: Containment lacks design and verification criteria (CRITICAL)
Observation-2: REQ005 Geofence breach action outcome is unspecified (CRITICAL SAFETY)
scale.scale: Percentage of simulated failure scenarios where the geofence logic correctly prevents boundary exceedance, measured across [NumScenarios] Monte Carlo trials spanning environmental ODD [OddBounds] and single/dual failure modes. Success threshold: >= 99.9% of trials result in zero boundary exceedance.

scale_parameters.NumScenarios: number of Monte Carlo simulation trials (e.g., 10,000)
scale_parameters.OddBounds: operational design domain environmental parameters (wind speed, GPS accuracy, GPS denial duration, barometric sensor failure)
scale_parameters.FailureModes: failure categories (C2 loss, GPS loss, altimeter failure, geofence logic failure, pilot override attempt)

sub_scales: BoundaryTolerance - horizontal and vertical geofence boundary margin enforced
FailureModeCoverage - count of single and dual failure scenarios tested
SurvivalTime - mean time to containment after failure detection
scale.reasoning: DeFOSPAM findings flagged that containment lacks design criteria and failure mode specifications. This Scale operationalizes automatic containment via Monte Carlo evidence. Confidence: 9.

meters.meter_definition: Before first flight, Range Safety Officer (RSO) or Workbench simulator runs 10,000 Monte Carlo trials of geofence enforcement. Each trial: (1) Initializes swarm in random position within authorized area, (2) Simulates random failure modes from list: C2 loss, GPS loss, altimeter failure, geofence logic fault, single-aircraft power loss. (3) Runs geofence logic at 10Hz, (4) Checks if aircraft exceeds boundary by >= 50m (pre-configured tolerance). Success = aircraft recovers to within boundary or triggers RTL before 50m breach. Target: >= 99.9% of trials show zero boundary exceedance. Outputs: pass/fail heatmap (boundary hot spots), distribution of boundary-approach distances, time-to-containment histograms. If pass, geofence is cleared for flight. If fail, airborne limits are tightened and sim re-runs. Cost: 2-4 hours for full 10k trial suite on modern hardware.
meters.measurement_tool: Workbench simulator (Monte Carlo engine) or independent flight-dynamics simulator (e.g., jMAVSim, Gazebo); trial data logger (CSV output)
meters.measurement_frequency: per-submission + event-triggered (re-run after any geofence code change)
meters.measurement_owner: Workbench engine (automated) + Range Safety Officer (human sign-off on sim assumptions)
meters.reasoning: GeofenceContainment Scale specifies 99.9% success rate across Monte Carlo trials. This Meter IS the simulation gauntlet. Accuracy and precision are high because simulator is deterministic and repeatable. Cost is medium (compute time). Credibility is high because it's transparent and auditable. This is load-bearing: evidence of geofence containment before first flight is non-negotiable for safety. Confidence: 9.

meters.meter_definition: After Monte Carlo passes, a controlled flight test is conducted with Range Safety Officer present and termination system armed. Setup: (1) Swarm launches within authorized area. (2) RSO deliberately commands aircraft to approach boundary via GPS waypoint or C2 manual input. (3) Geofence logic is monitored in real-time. (4) Test measures: time from boundary approach alert to RTL trigger, distance at RTL initiation, post-RTL containment time. Test data: 5 deliberate approach attempts per flight, 3-5 flights. Success = all approaches trigger RTL before boundary exceedance; containment time measured in seconds. This is a fly-away stress test; it validates that the geofence actually works under real conditions (GPS noise, latency, wind, etc.) not just in simulator. Cost: 1-2 days of range time, RSO supervision, safety equipment. Run once per operation type before first autonomous flight.
meters.measurement_tool: Live airborne GPS receiver + geofence monitor log; RSO observation + video record; post-flight telemetry analysis
meters.measurement_frequency: per-review + event-triggered (re-run after major swarm or geofence redesign)
meters.measurement_owner: Range Safety Officer (human); Workbench logs data; independent post-flight analysis
meters.reasoning: This Meter is the live fly-away test specified in guidance. It's the expensive, thorough validation that geofence containment is real. Accuracy and precision are high because you're measuring actual aircraft behavior. Cost is high (range time, personnel). Speed is low (days). Credibility is VERY high because it demonstrates working hardware + software integration. Justified cost: geofence is load-bearing safety-critical for BVLOS swarm ops. Confidence: 9.

past.source: CAA annual safety reports on geofence-related incidents; published BVLOS trial outcomes (Project XCelerate, AUVSI data)
past.estimation_note: CAA safety reports document fly-away incidents; geofence-related containment failures are rare but non-zero. Estimated baseline effectiveness at >= 99.5% for well-designed systems, based on published incident rates.

status.source: Continuous monitoring via CAA incident reporting (MOR); trial outcome data from AMLAS/SACE
status.estimation_note: Status based on reported containment effectiveness in recent BVLOS trials. Slight improvement from FY 2024 due to better geofence algorithm maturity and Monte Carlo validation practices.

record.holder: Leading military and commercial operators with redundant containment (RAF Protector; Airborne Support BVLOS fleet)
record.source: Published trial outcomes; AUVSI flight test data; EUROCAE WG6 field experience

ideal.rationale: Zero boundary exceedances in any simulated or operational scenario. 100% containment is the safety imperative for autonomous flight over populated areas.

trend.source: CAA incident data; diminishing returns on geofence redundancy investment

data_gaps: CAA annual safety reports aggregate incidents by category but do not separately report geofence-specific containment failures. Request disaggregated data from CAA Flight Safety branch.
Monte Carlo validation practices and acceptance thresholds vary widely across operators. Suggest CAA mandate EUROCAE WG6 geofence validation standard (in development).
Military program geofence performance data (RAF Protector, Reaper) is classified. Suggest CAA request performance summaries for civil operator benchmark reference.
benchmarks.reasoning: GeofenceContainment is a safety-critical value with the highest measured baseline (99.5%+). The gap from Status to Ideal is minimal (0.4%), indicating mature technology and strong industry practice. However, as swarm operations scale, the probability of at least one aircraft breaching geofence in a multi-aircraft formation increases combinatorially. The stable trend reflects engineering maturity but suggests continued investment in Monte Carlo validation and redundancy is required to achieve Ideal.

stakeholders.name: CAA Airspace Regulator (REQ005)
stakeholders.specific_need: Automatic geofence containment with 99.9% success across Monte Carlo failure scenarios to ensure fly-away risk is eliminated and TDA can be tightly bounded

stakeholders.name: Third-Party Air Users (civil aviation)
stakeholders.specific_need: Hard containment guarantee so that swarm cannot drift into their airspace and create collision risk. Zero-tolerance for boundary exceedance.
stakeholders.target_override: 100% containment (no exceptions); single breach is unacceptable

stakeholders.name: Public Below (non-segregated operations / ground risk)
stakeholders.specific_need: Geofence prevents uncontrolled aircraft drift into populated areas. Protection from fly-away risk.

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Flexibility in geofence boundaries and design approach (hard constraint vs soft recovery vs crew intervention) to manage design and certification cost. Wants proportional risk-based requirements.
stakeholders.tolerable_override: 98% or 99% success if crew intervention is available on-site and response time is < 5 seconds
stakeholders.target_override: 95% success if multi-redundant termination is demonstrated

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Geofence failure modes and worst-credible recovery time specified in ConOps to assess hazard mitigation completeness

stakeholders.name: MAA Range Safety Officer
stakeholders.specific_need: If trial on MOD range: geofence ensures aircraft cannot exit range boundary. Hard containment required.
stakeholders.target_override: 100% success rate; range safety non-negotiable

conflicts: CRITICAL CONFLICT: Airspace Regulator demands 99.9% success (hard containment); Applicant wants proportional requirement based on crew intervention availability and response time (98-99% acceptable if crew is present). Third-Party Air Users and Public want zero breaches. Conflict resolution: CAA Airspace Regulator power is absolute; geofence is a hard safety requirement. Applicant cannot negotiate target downward; must invest in redundancy and Monte Carlo validation.

Design approach conflict: Applicant may propose soft geofence (warning boundary) with crew takeover; Airspace Regulator requires hard geofence (automatic motor cutoff or autonomous containment maneuver). Conflict resolved by regulatory safety priority: hard containment is non-negotiable.

Test scope conflict: Applicant wants limited failure mode coverage (single failures); CAA demands comprehensive Monte Carlo including dual failures, GPS denial, sensor faults, and software faults. Conflict resolved by scale requiring dual failure coverage and environmental ODD bounds.
stakeholders.reasoning: Airspace Regulator has absolute power to approve or reject geofence design and is accountable to Third-Party Air Users and Public. Without 99.9% containment evidence, TDA cannot be safely approved. Third-Party Air Users and Public depend on Airspace Regulator enforcement. Applicant must meet this hard requirement; no negotiation on target. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-3 and Observation-2 flagged that containment breach action is undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes the 99.9% success criterion.

constraints.conditions: Monte Carlo simulation (>= 10,000 trials) shows >= 99.5% success preventing boundary exceedance across ODD and single/dual failures
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B loss-of-control containment criteria
constraints.reasoning: Status 99.6% already above Tolerable. Minimal gap reflects technology maturity. Swarm ops increase breach probability combinatorially.

constraints.conditions: below 99% containment, regulator cannot authorize due to excessive fly-away risk
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B authorization revocation threshold
constraints.reasoning: Survival threshold: below 99%, uncontrolled breach risk is unacceptable and authorization refused.

constraints.conditions: for populated trial areas (residential density > 0.1/hectare), regulator demands 99.9% standard
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B populated-area risk acceptance
constraints.reasoning: Regulatory override for populated airspace. Higher population density requires lower breach tolerance.

targets.conditions: >= 99.9% of Monte Carlo simulated failure scenarios (>= 1,000 trials, single + dual failures, environmental ODD) result in zero boundary exceedance with autonomous containment

increments.milestone: by end Phase 3 (VLOS swarm flight test)

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Airspace Regulator (REQ005) — primary; Third-Party Air Users — zero-tolerance for boundary exceedance; Public Below (ground risk) — fly-away protection
targets.reasoning: Wish = 99.9% containment is the baseline regulatory target and reflects stakeholder vocal ambition. Currently Status is 99.6%, so the 0.3% gap is modest but critical for airspace user protection. Achievable through Monte Carlo expansion and redundancy investment.

targets.conditions: >= 99.95% of >= 10,000 Monte Carlo trials across single + dual failures, GPS denial, barometric failure, and software faults result in zero boundary exceedance, with mean containment time <= 5 seconds

increments.milestone: by end Phase 4 (BVLOS single aircraft swarm nominal)

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Airspace Regulator (REQ005) — committed hard containment; Third-Party Air Users — zero tolerance; MAA Range Safety (if applicable) — range non-negotiable
targets.reasoning: Goal = 99.95% by end 2027 is the team-committed containment standard with comprehensive failure mode coverage and validated survival time. Achievable through redundant geofence logic (soft + hard), improved Monte Carlo models, and flight test validation. This is the hard safety requirement per CAA Airspace Regulator authority.

targets.conditions: >= 99.99% (four nines) of >= 50,000 Monte Carlo trials across all failure modes including multi-aircraft collision avoidance during containment with provably correct geofence logic
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Airspace Regulator — aspirational safety margin; Third-Party Air Users — ultra-conservative protection; MAA Range Safety — operational record achievement
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 99.99% (four nines) requires formal verification of geofence logic and massive Monte Carlo campaigns. Feasibility is stretch because this demands investment in formal methods beyond current industry practice. Aspirational for year 1 steady-state.

background.authority: CAA airspace change team (trial segregation approval)
background.motivation: Geofence is the automated last line of defense against uncontrolled BVLOS flight. Single-point-failure in geofence enforcement can release aircraft into uncontrolled airspace, creating public-below and third-party safety hazard. Keystone K1 safety mechanism.

risks.risk: Monte Carlo simulation (METER-005) assumes benign GPS error model; real environment may have multipath, spoofing, or jamming outside ODD bounds
risks.mitigation: Expand Monte Carlo environmental ODD to include GPS denial (up to 60s), GPS accuracy degradation (up to 50m CEP), electromagnetic interference. Conduct live fly-away test (METER-006) in representative RF environment.

risks.risk: Geofence logic shares compute platform with autonomy logic; failure in autonomy (e.g., control loop instability) could corrupt geofence state
risks.mitigation: Require independent termination system (IndependentFlightTermination) on separate hardware path. Geofence enforcement must not depend on main autonomy processor. Use watchdog timer as secondary containment mechanism.

risks.risk: Status (99.6%) nearly meets Tolerable (99.5%); but Status may be measured in benign simulation conditions, not representative of real swarm operations
risks.mitigation: Conduct METER-006 live fly-away test with entire swarm (not single aircraft), evaluating formation integrity during geofence enforcement. Multi-aircraft interactions (e.g., formation flutter) may expose geofence assumptions.

risks.risk: Gap analysis: Tolerable (99.5%) vs Survival (99.0%) is only 0.5%; implies very narrow regulatory margin if geofence performance degrades after authorization
risks.mitigation: Establish continuous post-flight monitoring (METER-010): each flight accumulates real-world geofence performance data. If trend moves below 99.5%, trial pauses pending investigation.

background.value_of_value: Safety-critical. Uncontrolled BVLOS beyond geofence could result in collision with third-party aircraft, property damage, or public injury. Regulatory impact: failure on this value means authorization refusal. No geofence evidence = no BVLOS approval.
background.strategic_alignment: Prerequisite for MAA/CAA BVLOS authorization (Protector RG Mk1 trials, GCAP platform early work). Enables Project Alvina attritable-mass experimentation in segregated airspace. CAA BVLOS expansion and urban-logistics depend on reliable geofence assurance.
background.keystone_reference: K1 (automated containment; safety-critical single-point failure mitigation)
background.reasoning: GeofenceContainment is the most-mature safety technology in this set. Status (99.6%) is already above Tolerable (99.5%), indicating technology maturity. Risk is primarily in simulation-to-reality gap and multi-aircraft interactions. Trend is stable (0.1% per year improvement), reflecting engineering plateau.

qc_findings.finding_title: Status exceeds tolerable; minimal gap but swarm multiplication risk unaddressed
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (99.6% containment) is ABOVE Isaac's Tolerable (99.5%), suggesting technology maturity. However, the Scale measures single-aircraft containment via Monte Carlo. Swarm operations multiply risk: probability that AT LEAST ONE aircraft breaches geofence increases combinatorially (5 aircraft: ~5 x single-breach probability). Scale does not explicitly address multi-aircraft correlation. Raj's Background notes this: 'Swarm ops increase breach probability combinatorially.' Status (99.6%) may mask aggregated risk when formalized as swarm.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Extend scale to explicitly measure multi-aircraft containment: test all N aircraft simultaneously during Monte Carlo; measure probability that none breach. For 5-aircraft swarm: each aircraft at 99.6% means swarm at ~97.8% containment (5 independent failures). Tolerable constraint may need elevation (99.95% single-aircraft to achieve 99% swarm). Ray's METER-006 (Live Fly-Away Breach Test) must include full-swarm dynamics: do formation interactions (e.g., wake effects, RF interference) degrade individual geofence performance? Continuous post-flight monitoring (METER-010 pattern) tracks real-world performance; if trend moves below Tolerable, trial pauses.
qc_findings.cross_references: Alexa Scale: Monte Carlo 99.9% success across single/dual failures; Brook benchmarks: Status 99.6% vs Tolerable 99.5% vs Survival 99.0% vs Regulatory 99.9%; Isaac Constraint: multiple levels per populated area; Ray METER-005, METER-006: Monte Carlo + Live Fly-Away Test; Raj Background: GeofenceContainment notes swarm multiplication risk

scale.ambition_level: link loss is survivable and non-interfering
defospam_finding_refs: Dorothy-9: Link loss survivable lacks time and failure mode boundaries (CRITICAL)
Observation-4: REQ006 Link loss outcome undefined (failover vs. crew takeover vs. RTL)
scale.scale: Time duration (seconds) for which loss of C2 link does not result in loss-of-separation or uncontrolled flight. Target: >= 30 seconds autonomous survival for loss-of-C2; >= 120 seconds if crew manual takeover is available.

scale_parameters.LinkLossScenario: failure mode (complete C2 loss, partial C2 degradation, inter-vehicle link loss, GPS+C2 combined loss)

sub_scales: C2LossAutoSurvivalTime - seconds of autonomous flight safety after C2 loss
C2LossCrewTakeoverLatency - seconds available for crew to detect link loss and regain control
InterVehicleLinkLossBehavior - documented swarm response to inter-vehicle link loss
CombinedFailureSurvival - survival time when C2 and GPS are both unavailable
scale.reasoning: DeFOSPAM findings flagged that link loss survivable lacks time boundaries and survival strategy definition. This Scale operationalizes survivable by specifying time windows. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_definition: Controlled simulation test: swarm is flying autonomous waypoint mission. Test operator cuts C2 link (RF kill switch or simulator command). Telemetry system logs: (1) Link loss detection time (latency from actual link break to system recognizing loss), (2) Swarm response: do swarm members continue waypoint navigation? Do inter-vehicle links maintain cohesion? (3) Survival duration: how long before swarm enters unsafe state (loss-of-separation, uncontrolled descent, etc.). Test runs 10 scenarios with link-loss duration ranging from 100ms to 30+ seconds. Targets: (a) Autonomous survival >= 30 seconds for loss-of-C2 only, (b) Crew takeover latency <= 5 seconds (time for crew to see alert and manually intervene), (c) Inter-vehicle link-loss recovery within 10 seconds. Data logged: link loss duration, survival time, separation maintained, crew reaction time. Run in simulation first (10 trials, 1 hour), then in live flight test (3-5 sorties, 1-2 days).
meters.measurement_tool: Workbench simulator (C2 link-loss injection) + live flight test with RF kill switch; telemetry logger; separation monitoring (ADS-B or inter-vehicle ranging)
meters.measurement_frequency: per-submission + per-review + event-triggered (re-test after link architecture change)
meters.measurement_owner: Workbench engine (sim); Range Safety Officer + flight test crew (live); data analyst
meters.reasoning: C2LinkResilience Scale specifies time thresholds (30s autonomous, 120s with crew). This Meter is the hold-safe survival test. Accuracy is high because timings are measured objectively. Precision is high because scenarios are repeatable. Cost is medium (sim + flight time). Credibility is high because it's grounded in both simulation and live flight. Confidence: 8.

past.source: BVLOS trial outcomes (Project XCelerate, published ASTM F3550 test data)
past.estimation_note: Commercial BVLOS systems typically demonstrate 30-60 seconds of autonomous survival after C2 loss, with crew takeover available within 120 seconds. Estimated at 30 seconds for conservative baseline.

status.source: Current BVLOS swarm trial data; AMLAS/SACE pilot outcomes
status.estimation_note: Recent swarm operators report 45-60 seconds autonomous survival, with enhanced inter-vehicle link loss protocols. Status estimated at 45 seconds (middle of observed range).

record.holder: Military autonomous systems (RAF Protector, Reaper); leading civil operators with redundant C2
record.source: Published RAF Protector program documentation; AUVSI best-practice data

ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale: >= 30 seconds autonomous survival for loss-of-C2; >= 120 seconds if crew takeover available. Ideal set at 300 seconds as a stretch target for highly autonomous swarms with mission-level decision-making.

trend.source: Autonomous decision-making algorithm maturity; inter-vehicle communication redundancy improvements

data_gaps: CAA does not publish C2 link resilience benchmarks in annual safety reports. Request test data from BVLOS trial programs (XCelerate, AMLAS, SACE).
ASTM F3550 test results are proprietary to operators. Suggest CAA sponsor publication of anonymized benchmark data from recent trials.
Inter-vehicle link loss resilience is rarely tested or reported separately. Recommend CAA mandate this as a distinct KPI for swarm operations.
benchmarks.reasoning: C2LinkResilience measures the time available for autonomous survival and crew takeover. Current Status (45 seconds) is well above Alexa's minimum requirement (30 seconds) but far below Ideal (300 seconds). The 255-second gap reflects the engineering challenge of implementing safe autonomous decision-making without ground control. Swarms face additional risk: loss of inter-vehicle link can trigger formation breakup. Trend is improving but slowly.

stakeholders.name: CAA Spectrum Specialist (REQ006)
stakeholders.specific_need: C2 link loss survival times quantified (>= 30 seconds autonomous, >= 120 seconds with crew takeover) to assess non-interference with other users and spectrum efficiency

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: Link loss response (failover behavior, crew takeover latency, termination decision) defined to ensure safe contingency execution

stakeholders.name: CAA DAA Assessor (REQ007)
stakeholders.specific_need: If C2 loss triggers autonomous DA logic, inter-vehicle coordination must remain safe. Link loss affects both C2 and inter-vehicle data; evidence required.

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Autonomous survival time threshold and crew takeover latency defined upfront to design C2 redundancy, failover logic, and user interface. Wants achievable targets (e.g., 30 sec autonomous is feasible with modern autopilots)
stakeholders.target_override: Lower survival times (e.g., 15 seconds autonomous, 60 seconds with crew takeover) if ground-based recovery is available

stakeholders.name: Flight Test Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Defined test scenarios for C2 loss (complete loss, partial degradation, inter-vehicle link loss, combined GPS+C2 loss) to structure flight test and acceptance criteria

stakeholders.name: Applicant Autonomy Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Inter-vehicle link loss behavior defined: does swarm split? Return to base individually? Maintain formation? Bounds on behavior during link loss inform algorithm design.

conflicts: Spectrum Specialist demands high survival times (>= 30 sec autonomous) to prove non-interference; Applicant may want lower thresholds (e.g., 15 sec) as easier to achieve. Conflict resolved by Spectrum Specialist regulatory authority and realistic automation capability (modern autopilots can sustain 30-60 seconds safely).

Flight-Ops Inspector wants crew takeover available as primary failover (>= 120 sec window); Applicant may propose autonomous termination instead (cost lower, crew not needed). Conflict: human factors and meaningful control requirements favor crew takeover; Alexa's scale requires both paths quantified but defaults to crew takeover + autonomous survival.

Inter-vehicle link loss scenario: Applicant prefers simple behavior (split to base individually); Regulators want swarm to maintain formation if possible (preserves safety case). Conflict resolved by requiring both sub-cases tested and behavior pre-defined in ConOps.
stakeholders.reasoning: Spectrum Specialist has regulatory authority over C2 link performance and must defend non-interference to other spectrum users (civil aviation, emergency services). Flight-Ops Inspector shares responsibility (contingency management). Applicant must design and test to meet 30-second survival minimum. Link loss is a critical failure mode (loss-of-separation risk) and Spectrum Specialist controls the approval threshold.

constraints.conditions: C2 link maintains >= 99% availability; loss triggers autonomous safe state within 30 seconds
constraints.gap_from_status: availability metric (99% vs status 45-second survival time not directly comparable)
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B link resilience and autonomous safety
constraints.reasoning: 99% availability is binding constraint. Below this, crew cannot reliably regain control. Status (45-second survival) meets Survival (30s) but link uptime is regulator requirement.

constraints.deadline: mandatory minimum for all BVLOS operations
constraints.conditions: after C2 loss, aircraft maintains safe state (within geofence) >= 30 seconds for crew RTL trigger
constraints.regulatory_anchor: EUROCAE WG6 autonomy survivability baseline
constraints.reasoning: 30 seconds = minimum human reaction time for crew detection and RTL command. Status already meets this.

targets.conditions: >= 60 seconds autonomous survival after complete C2 loss without loss-of-separation or uncontrolled flight; >= 120 seconds if crew manual takeover is available
targets.stakeholder_driver: Applicant / Operator — cost-driven, prefers crew takeover as fallback; CAA Spectrum Specialist (REQ006) — C2 link monitoring and loss-of-link response
targets.reasoning: Wish = 60 seconds autonomous survival reflects vocal operator ambition to extend C2 loss tolerance beyond current 45 seconds through improved autonomous decision-making and inter-vehicle coordination. Achievable with swarm decision-making maturity.

targets.conditions: >= 90 seconds autonomous survival (no C2, nominal formation maintained, loss-of-separation prevented) with documented inter-vehicle link loss behavior and >= 120 seconds crew takeover window

increments.milestone: by end Phase 5 (full engine autonomous swarm)

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Spectrum Specialist (REQ006) — committed link-loss resilience standard; Applicant — higher operational autonomy reduces crew workload
targets.reasoning: Goal = 90 seconds by end 2027 is the team-committed C2 resilience target supporting BVLOS autonomous swarm operations. Requires mature swarm decision-making and inter-vehicle coordination. Stretch feasibility because algorithm maturity is still emerging.

targets.conditions: >= 300 seconds autonomous survival with combined C2 + GPS loss, documented mission-level decision-making (RTL vs abort vs continue), and zero loss-of-separation events
targets.stakeholder_driver: Applicant — aspirational high autonomy; CAA Autonomy Reviewer (REQ008) — mission-level decision-making assurance
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 300 seconds represents highly autonomous swarm capable of mission-level decisions under combined C2 + GPS loss. Feasibility is uncertain because this requires proving correct autonomous decision-making under epistemic uncertainty—a frontier challenge in assurance.

background.owner: Applicant (C2 system designer) + CAA spectrum specialist
background.motivation: C2 link loss is inevitable in RF-contested environments. Autonomous survival time is the grace period for crew detection and RTL trigger. Without documented survival time (>= 30 seconds), BVLOS authorization cannot be defended if link loss occurs.

risks.risk: Status (45 seconds autonomous survival) meets Alexa's minimum (>= 30s) but differs from Isaac's Tolerable constraint on link availability (99%). Conflation of two metrics: survival time vs. availability.
risks.mitigation: Ray's METER-007 measures both: autonomous survival time AND link availability. Clarify: survival time = duration after link loss before unsafe state; availability = percentage of mission time link is operational. Both must pass.

risks.risk: Swarms compound risk: if lead aircraft loses link, followers may lose inter-vehicle link (RF interference, network topology collapse). Cascading link loss can trigger uncontrolled formation breakup.
risks.mitigation: Define swarm-specific C2 resilience: (1) individual aircraft survival after main C2 loss, (2) swarm cohesion during inter-vehicle link loss (maintenance of separation). Test both independently (METER-007).

risks.risk: RF environment during trial may not match ODD assumptions (e.g., cellular tower interference, tactical RF testing nearby). Link resilience test may be invalidated by environment change.
risks.mitigation: Conduct RF environment survey pre-trial (spectrum monitoring, interference characterization). Update METER-007 assumptions based on survey. Re-test if RF environment changes materially.

background.value_of_value: Enables BVLOS operations. Without documented C2 resilience, crew cannot regain control after link loss and aircraft is uncontrollable. Regulatory impact: insufficient survival time means authorization refusal or VLOS-only constraint.
background.strategic_alignment: Supports CAA BVLOS expansion and urban-logistics (priority). Prerequisite for Project Alvina attritable-mass swarm trials (resilience to communication degradation in contested RF environment).
background.reasoning: Status (45 seconds) vs Tolerable (99% availability) are different metrics; confusion here is a major risk. Current industry practice measures survival time; Isaac's constraint emphasizes link availability as primary metric. Need clarity on which constraint dominates.

qc_findings.finding_title: Metric conflation: survival time vs link availability; definition ambiguity
qc_findings.defect_description: Alexa's Scale specifies time-based metric (>= 30 seconds autonomous survival). Isaac's Constraint specifies availability metric (>= 99% link availability). These measure different things: survival time = duration after link loss before unsafe state; availability = percentage of mission time link is operational. Scale and Constraint are not directly comparable. Raj's Background notes this conflation as a risk. Brook's Status (45-second survival) meets Survival threshold (30s) but doesn't clarify link availability percentage. Ambiguity creates validation gap: which metric dominates authorization decision?
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Clarify metrics: (1) Survival time = autonomous safe-state duration after complete C2 loss (>= 30s minimum per scale); (2) Link availability = percentage of mission time C2 link is operational (>= 99% per Isaac). Ray's METER-007 measures both independently: autonomous survival time AND link availability percentage. Update scale or constraint to eliminate ambiguity. Recommend: primary metric is 99% availability (crew reliance); secondary metric is 30s autonomous survival (failover assurance). Both must pass for authorization.
qc_findings.cross_references: Alexa Scale: >= 30 seconds autonomous survival (time-based); Isaac Constraint: >= 99% link availability (percentage-based); Ray METER-007: Link Loss Survival Hold-Safe Validation (measures both); Brook benchmarks: Status 45 seconds (only survival, not availability); Raj Background: C2LinkResilience medium risk on metric conflation

scale.ambition_level: cooperative and non-cooperative encounters resolved predictably
defospam_finding_refs: Observation-5: REQ007 Detect-and-avoid resolution action undefined (evasion strategy unspecified)
scale.scale: Percentage of encounter scenarios where the DAA system resolution is consistent with pre-defined decision rules and results in loss-of-separation recovery within [RecoveryTime] seconds, without subsequent secondary conflicts, measured across [NumEncounters] test encounters.

scale_parameters.EncounterType: encounter class (cooperative with ADS-B-equipped aircraft, non-cooperative with radar-tracked aircraft, pop-up aircraft)
scale_parameters.RecoveryTime: time to achieve minimum safe separation (e.g., >= 500ft horizontal, >= 100ft vertical)

sub_scales: CooperativeEncounterResolution - percentage of encounters with ADS-B-equipped intruders where DAA resolution is predictable
NonCooperativeEncounterResolution - percentage of encounters with non-equipped intruders where DAA resolution follows pre-defined rules
SwarmCoordinationUponEncounter - percentage of encounters where swarm formation is maintained post-encounter
DecisionConsistency - inter-test consistency of DAA decisions (same encounter repeated; resolution must be identical >= 95%)
scale.reasoning: DeFOSPAM finding flagged that DAA resolution action is undefined. This Scale operationalizes predictably resolved by requiring consistent decisions, recovery achievement, no secondary conflicts, and decision consistency. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_definition: DAA system is tested against a suite of >= 100 encounter scenarios drawn from DO-365 (Detect-and-Avoid standards). Scenarios span: (1) Cooperative intruders (ADS-B equipped, altitude/velocity known), (2) Non-cooperative intruders (radar-only, no velocity info), (3) Head-on, crossing, overtake geometries. For each scenario: (1) Intruder is injected into swarm flight path, (2) DAA system detects intruder and initiates resolution, (3) System logs: detection latency, resolution maneuver (altitude change, lateral displacement, speed change), outcome (separation achieved, secondary conflict, miss). Target metrics: (a) >= 95% of encounters result in recovery to >= 500ft horizontal, >= 100ft vertical separation, (b) Recovery achieved within [RecoveryTime], typically 60 seconds, (c) No secondary conflicts (re-engagement with same or new intruder). Decision Consistency sub-scale: same encounter scenario repeated 3 times; DAA decision must be identical >= 95% of trials (determinism check). Run in simulation first (100 scenarios, 2 hours compute time), then subset in live flight test (10-15 encounters per sortie, 3-5 flights).
meters.measurement_tool: DO-365 test scenario database (100 pre-built encounter geometries); Workbench simulator with intruder injection + DAA monitor; live flight test with chase aircraft or simulated intruder track injection
meters.measurement_frequency: per-submission + per-review + event-triggered (re-test after DAA logic change)
meters.measurement_owner: Workbench engine (sim) + qualified DAA tester (live); independent analysis
meters.reasoning: DAAPerformance Scale specifies encounter resolution success rate, recovery time, decision consistency. This Meter is the DO-365 test suite. Accuracy is high (separation measured objectively). Precision is high (deterministic scenarios). Cost is medium (compute + flight time). Credibility is VERY high because DO-365 is an external, recognized standard. Confidence: 9.

past.source: DO-365 minimum operational performance standard; published ASTM F3442 test data
past.estimation_note: DO-365 defines minimum DAA performance at 99.9% detection of cooperative intruders at 90% confidence. Non-cooperative detection much lower (70-90%). Estimated at 85% as a weighted average.

status.source: BVLOS trial DAA test results; AMLAS/SACE performance metrics
status.estimation_note: Recent trials show modest improvement in non-cooperative encounter resolution. Status estimated at 88% based on published AMLAS case study outcomes.

record.holder: Military autonomous systems with advanced radar and sensor fusion (RAF Protector, Reaper); research systems with cooperative airspace
record.source: Published military program data; ASTM F3442 exemplary system outcomes

ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale: consistent resolution with pre-defined decision rules, recovery within target time, no secondary conflicts, >= 95% decision consistency. Ideal assumes 99.95% resolution success for all encounter types (cooperative and non-cooperative).

trend.source: Sensor fusion algorithms; non-cooperative detection algorithm maturity

data_gaps: CAA does not publish disaggregated DAA performance metrics (cooperative vs non-cooperative encounter resolution rates). Request from flight safety assessment team.
ASTM F3442 test data is proprietary to operators and aircraft manufacturers. Suggest CAA sponsor publication of anonymized performance benchmarks from DO-365 certification testing.
Swarm coordination upon encounter is rarely tested separately. Recommend CAA mandate this as a distinct DAA sub-metric for multi-aircraft operations.
benchmarks.reasoning: DAAPerformance measures the reliability of collision avoidance in populated airspace.
