Current Status (88%) significantly exceeds the 85% baseline but remains below Ideal (99.95%). The 11.95% gap reflects challenges in non-cooperative encounter detection and decision consistency, particularly for novel swarm formations. Trend is improving but constrained by non-cooperative radar detection physics and algorithm reliability plateaus.

stakeholders.name: CAA DAA Assessor (REQ007)
stakeholders.specific_need: Detect-and-avoid resolution is predictable and consistent (>= 95% decision consistency, >= 500ft horizontal recovery) so that encounter outcomes are defensible and DO-365 / F3442 compliance is demonstrable

stakeholders.name: CAA Airspace Regulator (REQ004, REQ005)
stakeholders.specific_need: DAA system prevents loss-of-separation with non-cooperative aircraft in uncontrolled airspace. Resolution must not trigger secondary conflicts or endanger third parties.

stakeholders.name: Third-Party Air Users (civil aviation, commercial, general aviation)
stakeholders.specific_need: Swarm DAA response is predictable and non-aggressive so that encounter is resolved safely without forcing third-party maneuvers or creating secondary conflict
stakeholders.target_override: 100% consistency (zero unpredictable resolutions)

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear definition of 'predictable' and 'consistent' with quantified success thresholds (>= 95% decision consistency, >= 100 encounter test scenarios) to bound development and testing scope
stakeholders.target_override: Lower consistency threshold (e.g., 90%) if encounter type distribution is representative and coverage includes corner cases

stakeholders.name: Applicant Autonomy Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Defined encounter types (cooperative ADS-B, non-cooperative radar, pop-up) and decision rules to guide DAA algorithm design and testing. Reproducible test framework for consistency measurement.

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: DAA resolution behavior known and practiced in crew training so that crew can monitor and override if needed (meaningful human control)

conflicts: DAA Assessor wants maximum encounter coverage (all geometry types, all relative speeds, all weather conditions) to prove robustness; Applicant wants bounded test scenarios (representative sample of 100 encounters) to limit cost. Conflict resolved by DO-365 / F3442 standard applicability; Applicant must meet industry-standard encounter distribution.

Third-Party Air Users expect zero unpredictable resolutions (100% consistency); DAA Assessor sets realistic threshold (>= 95%) to acknowledge edge cases. Conflict resolved by regulatory authority: >= 95% consistency is the gate; residual 5% documented and acceptable.

Swarm coordination on encounter: Applicant may prefer single-aircraft DA response (each aircraft independently avoids); Airspace Regulator and DAA Assessor want coordinated swarm response to maintain formation and avoid secondary conflicts. Conflict: scale requires both swarm coordination on encounter and decision consistency.
stakeholders.reasoning: DAA Assessor has regulatory authority over detect-and-avoid performance and must defend consistency and predictability to Third-Party Air Users and Airspace Regulator. DO-365 / F3442 standards are the regulatory anchor. Applicant must design and test to meet >= 95% consistency and zero secondary conflicts. Third-Party Air Users have power (can formally object to trial) and depend on DAA Assessor's approval.

constraints.conditions: DAA resolves >= 99.5% of cooperative and non-cooperative encounter scenarios predictably, with recovery within target time and no secondary conflicts
constraints.regulatory_anchor: DO-365 MOPS + swarm coordination margin
constraints.reasoning: DO-365 baseline 99.9% for cooperative. Tolerable 99.5% accounts for non-cooperative difficulty and swarm complexity. Status 88% far below; primary gap is non-cooperative resolution.

constraints.conditions: DO-365 minimum: 99.9% detection confidence for cooperative DAA systems
constraints.regulatory_anchor: DO-365 Minimum Operational Performance Standard
constraints.reasoning: Table-stakes regulatory compliance. Non-cooperative detection is primary challenge.

targets.conditions: >= 93% of >= 100 encounter scenarios (cooperative + non-cooperative mix) with loss-of-separation recovery within 10 seconds and zero secondary conflicts
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA DAA Assessor (REQ007) — performance standard aligned with DO-365 + margin; Applicant — cost-driven DAA algorithm selection
targets.reasoning: Wish = 93% (DO-365 baseline + 3% margin) reflects achievable improvement over current 88% status through sensor fusion and non-cooperative detection algorithm maturity. Supports VLOS swarm operations in supervised airspace.

targets.conditions: >= 97% (DO-365 + 20% margin) of >= 200 encounter scenarios with consistent DAA decisions (>= 95% repeat-encounter identity), recovery within 8 seconds, zero secondary conflicts
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA DAA Assessor (REQ007) — committed DO-365 + 20% performance standard; CAA Safety-Case Reviewer — decision consistency confidence
targets.reasoning: Goal = 97% by end 2027 is the team-committed DAA performance standard supporting BVLOS swarm operations. Requires mature sensor fusion, algorithm stability, and comprehensive encounter testing. Achievable with algorithm optimization and field validation.

targets.conditions: >= 99.5% (DO-365 + 30% margin) of >= 500 encounter scenarios with swarm formation maintained post-encounter, coordinated evasion across multi-aircraft formation, zero secondary conflicts, and >= 98% decision consistency
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA DAA Assessor — aspirational swarm encounter performance; CAA Autonomy Reviewer — coordinated evasion under swarm constraints
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 99.5% (DO-365 + 30% margin) with swarm formation resilience. Requires mature swarm coordination algorithms and extensive encounter testing. Stretch feasibility because coordinated multi-aircraft evasion is a frontier challenge.

background.owner: Applicant (DAA algorithm developer) + CAA DAA assessor
background.authority: CAA flight safety assessment team (aligned with DO-365 / F3442 certification body)
background.motivation: DAA system resolves encounters with cooperative and non-cooperative aircraft. Failure results in mid-air collision. DO-365 is the regulatory standard; swarms must demonstrate consistent, predictable resolution across cooperative and non-cooperative encounter geometries.

risks.risk: Status (88%) far below Tolerable (99.5%) and Regulatory (99.9%); indicates significant gap in non-cooperative encounter resolution
risks.mitigation: Invest in non-cooperative radar detection algorithm development and testing. Ray's METER-008 runs 100+ encounter scenarios; analyze failures and iterate radar/decision logic. Target >= 99% before authorization.

risks.risk: Swarm coordination during encounter is rarely tested (sub-scale DecisionConsistency); decision consistency may fail if swarm formation state affects encounter resolution logic
risks.mitigation: Extend METER-008 to include swarm-formation-aware encounters: e.g., formation tight (3m separation) vs. loose (50m separation). DAA logic must resolve encounters consistently regardless of formation geometry.

risks.risk: DO-365 certification assumes single-aircraft DAA; multi-aircraft coordinated resolution is not yet in standard. Swarms may trigger resolution maneuvers that interfere with each other.
risks.mitigation: Define swarm DAA protocol: (1) lead aircraft triggers resolution, (2) followers execute coordinated maneuver (offset formation, altitude change). Test coordinated resolution in METER-008 subset.

background.value_of_value: Safety-critical. DAA failure results in collision with third-party aircraft (manned or unmanned). Regulatory impact: failure on DAA means authorization refusal for any airspace with other air traffic. Status (88%) far below Tolerable (99.5%) indicates significant development work required.
background.strategic_alignment: Prerequisite for CAA BVLOS expansion into Class G airspace with civilian air traffic. Enables MAA/CAA trials in airspace shared with military training routes. Required for urban-logistics scenarios with helicopter traffic.
background.reasoning: DAA is a mature domain (DO-365 standard exists) but swarm adaptation is nascent. Status gap (88% vs 99.5%) reflects engineering challenge of non-cooperative detection in high-clutter environments and multi-aircraft decision coordination. Trend is improving slowly (2-4% per year), constrained by algorithmic limits and sensor physics.

qc_findings.finding_title: Status significantly below critical regulatory minimum
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (88% encounter resolution success) is 11.5 points below Isaac's Tolerable (99.5%) and 11.9 points below Isaac's Regulatory minimum (99.9% per DO-365 MOPS). This gap reflects inadequate non-cooperative encounter detection: civil radars struggle with small-RCS swarms. Scale requires >= 99.5% encounter resolution success (>= 500ft horizontal recovery, zero secondary conflicts, >= 95% decision consistency across repeat encounters). Current systems do not meet this standard, meaning BVLOS trials in Class G airspace with civil traffic cannot be authorized.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Algorithm development required: (1) Invest in non-cooperative radar detection algorithm maturity—current physics limits are 70-90% detection; target 99%+ via sensor fusion (radar + ADS-B + visual). (2) Ray's METER-008 (DO-365 Encounter Resolution Test Matrix) runs >= 200 scenarios; analyze failures and iterate. (3) Extend testing to swarm-formation-aware encounters: does DAA logic respond consistently if formation is tight vs loose? (4) Define swarm DAA protocol: lead triggers resolution, followers execute coordinated maneuver. High development investment required; current status makes BVLOS authorization impossible.
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 88% vs Tolerable 99.5% vs Regulatory 99.9%; Isaac Constraint: 99.5% tolerable, 99.9% regulatory; Ray METER-008: DO-365 Encounter Resolution Test Matrix; Raj Background: DAAPerformance critical priority rank 6, 11% gap to tolerable

defospam_finding_refs: Observation-6: REQ008 Emergent swarm behavior deviation - no specification of what happens when behavior deviates from assurance case
scale.scale: Count of distinct emergent behaviors documented in the swarm decision-making architecture case, each with a defined boundary and a detection/recovery mechanism if boundary is exceeded. A swarm is bounded if: (1) >= 5 emergent behaviors are identified and bounded, (2) each boundary is quantified, (3) test evidence demonstrates the swarm stays within bounds in >= 95% of flight test hours.

scale_parameters.EmergentBehavior: collective swarm behavior not reducible to single-aircraft logic (formation geometry, coordinated maneuver, flocking, collective decision-making, swarm split/rejoin)
scale_parameters.BoundaryDefinition: quantified limits on behavior (e.g., formation tolerance +/- 10m, coordination latency <= 2 seconds)
scale_parameters.TestEvidence: flight test hours accumulating evidence of in-bounds behavior

sub_scales: EmergentBehaviorInventory - count of distinct emergent behaviors identified and documented
BoundaryQuantification - percentage of identified emergent behaviors with quantified state limits and detection criteria
RecoveryCapability - count of out-of-bounds behaviors for which a recovery/abort action is pre-defined
TestCoverage - percentage of test flight hours where swarm operates within all defined bounds
scale.reasoning: DeFOSPAM finding flagged that emergent behavior deviation outcomes are undefined. This Scale operationalizes emergent-behaviour evidence by requiring explicit inventory of emergent behaviors, quantified boundaries for each, and test evidence. Confidence: 7.

meters.meter_definition: Autonomy designer submits a structured manifest of emergent behaviors identified in the swarm architecture. For each behavior (e.g., formation tightening, consensus deadlock, flocking), the manifest documents: (1) Description of the behavior (what happens, when does it occur), (2) Quantified boundary (state space limits, e.g., 'formation tolerance +/- 10m', 'consensus latency <= 2 seconds'), (3) Detection mechanism ('if inter-aircraft separation drops below 5m, trigger alert'), (4) Recovery action ('expand formation limits by 20%, reset consensus algorithm'). Auditor reviews manifest and verifies: (1) >= 5 distinct emergent behaviors identified, (2) Each behavior has a quantified boundary (not vague), (3) Each behavior has a defined detection + recovery mechanism. Targets: >= 5 behaviors, 100% with boundaries, 100% with detection/recovery. Cost: 2-3 hours for full audit. Run once per case before autonomous flight begins.
meters.measurement_tool: Emergent Behavior Manifest template (structured form); auditor checklist (presence/absence of boundaries, detection, recovery)
meters.measurement_owner: CAA autonomy reviewer (human domain expert)
meters.reasoning: SwarmEmergentBoundedness Scale requires >=5 emergent behaviors with quantified boundaries. This Meter is the documentation audit. Accuracy is medium because 'emergent' is subjective; precision is medium for the same reason. Cost is low (desk audit). Speed is high. Credibility is medium because it's documentation-based; actual test evidence follows in METER-010. Confidence: 7.

meters.meter_name: Emergent Behavior Flight Test Coverage Validator
meters.meter_definition: During flight testing, telemetry logs capture swarm state at 10Hz or higher. After each flight, data is analyzed: for each emergent behavior from the manifest (METER-009), auditor verifies: (1) Did the behavior occur during flight? If yes, did it stay within documented boundary? (2) If boundary was exceeded, was detection mechanism triggered? (3) Was recovery action effective? Cumulative metric: percentage of flight-hours where swarm operated within all documented emergent-behavior boundaries. Target: >= 95% of test flight hours show in-bounds behavior. Threshold: if >= 1 out-of-bounds event occurs in 5 flight-hours, the behavior boundary is re-tightened and additional test hours are required. Data logged: timestamp, behavior trigger event, state (in-bounds vs. out-of-bounds), recovery outcome. Cost: 1 hour per flight for data analysis + 0.5 day for cumulative trend analysis. Run continuously throughout phased trial program (phases 2-4).
meters.measurement_tool: Flight telemetry parser (extract swarm state); emergent behavior boundary checker (automated comparison); trend analysis dashboard
meters.measurement_owner: Workbench engine (automated parsing) + CAA autonomy reviewer (human interpretation); Range Safety Officer monitors real-time alerts
meters.reasoning: SwarmEmergentBoundedness Scale requires test evidence (>= 95% flight hours in-bounds). This Meter is the post-flight analysis. Accuracy is high because boundaries are objective limits. Precision is high (same analyzer always produces same result on same data). Cost is low (automated parsing + human review). Speed is high. Credibility is high because it's grounded in real flight data. Confidence: 8.

past.source: AMLAS pilot program case studies; EUROCAE WG6 early swarm guidance (FY 2024)
past.estimation_note: Early swarm programs (2023-2024) identified few emergent behaviors explicitly. Most focused on coordinated position-keeping rather than formation geometry, decision-making, or flocking. Estimated at 2 behaviors (formation + coordination latency).

status.source: AMLAS/SACE pilot maturity; EUROCAE WG6 swarm decision-making guidance (released 2025)
status.estimation_note: Recent operators identifying additional emergent behaviors (flocking, coordinated maneuver, swarm split/rejoin). Status estimated at 3 behaviors, approaching Alexa's scale target of >= 5.

record.holder: Research swarms and leading AMLAS pilot operators (Centre for Assuring Autonomy, University of Bristol swarm research)
record.source: Published AMLAS case studies; EUROCAE WG6 exemplary behavior inventories

ideal.unit: count of behaviors with quantified boundaries, recovery mechanisms, and >= 95% test coverage
ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale: >= 5 emergent behaviors with quantified boundaries, detection criteria, and documented recovery mechanisms. Ideal set at 8 to accommodate complex swarm coordination (formation + geometry + maneuver + decision + flocking + split/rejoin + emergent failure + collective evasion).

trend.source: AMLAS/SACE learning; EUROCAE WG6 swarm taxonomy maturity

data_gaps: No standard taxonomy of emergent swarm behaviors exists. EUROCAE WG6 is developing guidance (target: 2026). Request draft when available.
AMLAS case study data on emergent behavior identification is proprietary. Suggest CAA request anonymized exemplars for public reference.
Test coverage of emergent behaviors (percentage of flight test hours within bounds) is not systematically reported. Recommend CAA mandate this KPI for swarm authorizations.
benchmarks.reasoning: SwarmEmergentBoundedness is a novel domain with minimal industry practice. Current Status (3 behaviors) is significantly below Ideal (8), reflecting early-stage understanding of swarm dynamics. The 5-behavior gap reflects the fundamental challenge: swarm behavior emerges from interaction of multiple aircraft, is difficult to predict, and can violate safety invariants. Trend is optimistic but depends on EUROCAE WG6 guidance adoption and operator investment in emergent-behavior testing.

stakeholders.name: CAA Autonomy Reviewer (REQ008)
stakeholders.specific_need: Emergent behaviors identified, bounded, and tested (>= 5 behaviors, quantified limits, >= 95% flight test hours in bounds) to assure collective swarm behavior is predictable and safe

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Emergent behavior definitions and boundaries reflected in ConOps and ODD so that worst-credible collective behaviors are explicitly scoped in hazard analysis

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: Out-of-bounds emergent behavior triggers defined (detection mechanism + recovery action) to inform contingency planning and crew training

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear definition of 'emergent behavior' and minimum count (>= 5) to bound analysis scope; detection/recovery mechanisms must be feasible to implement and test
stakeholders.tolerable_override: Lower count (>= 3) if core behaviors (formation stability, coordinated maneuver, flocking) are thoroughly analyzed and tested

stakeholders.name: Applicant Autonomy Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Emergent behaviors quantified and detection criteria defined to guide swarm architecture design and flight testing. Wants proportional analysis (focus on safety-critical behaviors like loss-of-separation, emergent cohesion loss, unintended split)

conflicts: Autonomy Reviewer wants exhaustive emergent behavior inventory (formation geometry drift, coordination latency, flocking instability, split/rejoin behavior, multi-vehicle collision avoidance); Applicant wants focus on safety-critical behaviors only (formation loss-of-separation, coordinated maneuver stability). Conflict resolved by Alexa's scale requiring >= 5 distinct behaviors with quantified boundaries and test evidence—applicant must justify each behavior as either safety-critical or safety-relevant.

Detection mechanism conflict: Autonomy Reviewer wants automatic detection and autonomous recovery/abort (safety-critical); Applicant may prefer crew monitoring and manual intervention (lower automation cost). Conflict resolved by meaningful human control requirements (REQ010)—crew must be in the loop, but detection can be automatic.

Test coverage: Autonomy Reviewer wants >= 95% of flight test hours in bounds (stringent); Applicant prefers lower threshold if out-of-bounds occurrences are rare and handled safely. Conflict: scale requires >= 95% to assure boundedness; residual 5% must be documented and analyzed.
stakeholders.reasoning: Autonomy Reviewer has regulatory authority over swarm decision-making and emergent behavior assurance. Without bounded emergent behaviors, swarm is unpredictable and uninsurable. Safety-Case Reviewer depends on Autonomy Reviewer's conclusions. Applicant must invest in behavioral analysis and flight testing to identify, quantify, and bound emergent behaviors. DeFOSPAM Observation-6 flagged that emergent deviation outcomes are undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes this by requiring pre-defined recovery mechanisms.

constraints.unit: percentage of test flight hours within documented bounds
constraints.conditions: swarm operates within all emergent-behavior boundaries >= 95% of accumulated test flight hours; out-of-bounds events logged
constraints.regulatory_anchor: EUROCAE WG6 swarm assurance (draft 2025); RA 2330 military precedent
constraints.reasoning: Tolerable: >= 95% compliance. Status (3 behaviors identified, ~40% estimated compliance) far below. Once boundaries defined, swarm must respect them or behavior is uncontrolled.

constraints.unit: percentage of test flight hours within bounds
constraints.conditions: out-of-bounds in > 10% of hours triggers trial pause and emergent-behavior investigation
constraints.regulatory_anchor: RA 2330 behavior compliance margin precedent
constraints.reasoning: Survival: 90% compliance minimum. Higher out-of-bounds frequency suggests behaviors not well-understood. Trial pauses.

targets.conditions: >= 5 distinct emergent behaviors identified (formation geometry, coordinated maneuver, flocking, collective decision-making, swarm split/rejoin) each with quantified boundary and detection criteria
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Autonomy Reviewer (REQ008) — assurance of collective decision-making; Applicant Autonomy Engineer — design guidance via quantified bounds
targets.reasoning: Wish = 5 bounded behaviors reflects achievable acceleration from current 3 behaviors through EUROCAE WG6 swarm taxonomy adoption and AMLAS learning. This is the minimum viable swarm-behavior assurance case.

targets.conditions: >= 6 emergent behaviors with quantified bounds, detection criteria, and documented recovery/abort actions for each out-of-bounds condition, with >= 95% flight test coverage within bounds
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Autonomy Reviewer (REQ008) — committed swarm-behavior assurance; CAA Safety-Case Reviewer — hazard mitigation completeness
targets.reasoning: Goal = 6 behaviors by end 2027 is the team-committed swarm-assurance standard. Requires systematic emergent-behavior taxonomy, boundary quantification methodology, and extensive flight test. Stretch feasibility due to novel domain maturity.

targets.unit: count of behaviors with boundaries, recovery, and >= 99% test coverage
targets.conditions: >= 8 emergent behaviors with quantified bounds, automated anomaly detection (ML-based), recovery procedures, and >= 99% swarm flight-hours within all defined bounds across nominal and failure scenarios
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Autonomy Reviewer — aspirational swarm behavior predictability; CAA AI/ML Specialist — anomaly detection integration
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 8 behaviors with automated anomaly detection. Feasibility is uncertain because this requires integration of ML-based behavior monitoring into assurance case—a novel integration challenge.

background.authority: CAA innovation / autonomy assurance team (emerging role; no precedent yet)
background.motivation: Swarm behavior emerges from interaction of individual control laws; emergent behavior can violate safety invariants unexpectedly. Documentation of emergent behaviors and quantified boundaries is essential to assure swarms are controllable within operational envelope.

risks.risk: Status (3 behaviors identified) far below Tolerable (>= 95% flight hours within bounds); implies emergent behaviors are not yet systematically identified or bounded
risks.mitigation: METER-009 (Emergent Behavior Boundary Documentation Audit) requires operator to identify >= 5 distinct emergent behaviors (formation, coordination latency, flocking, decision deadlock, split/rejoin). METER-010 validates each is within bounds in >= 95% of flight hours.

risks.risk: Emergent behavior taxonomy is not standardized; operators may identify different behaviors or miss critical ones. No accepted list of 'expected swarm behaviors'.
risks.mitigation: EUROCAE WG6 is developing swarm-behavior taxonomy (target: 2026 guidance). AMLAS case studies establish accepted behaviors. Publish monthly CAA guidance update on emergent-behavior identification.

risks.risk: Survival threshold (Isaac: 90% flight hours within bounds) is lower than Tolerable (95%); if swarm operates outside bounds in > 10% of flights, does trial pause or continue?
risks.mitigation: Clarify policy: if any single emergent behavior exceeds bounds in > 5% of flights, trial pauses pending investigation. Operator re-tightens boundary or demonstrates bounds are incorrect and updates manifest. Re-test required.

background.value_of_value: Swarm assurance. If emergent behaviors are undocumented and unbounded, swarms can exhibit runtime surprises (unintended formation geometry changes, oscillation, consensus deadlock) that violate safety constraints. Regulatory impact: inability to explain swarm behavior blocks authorization.
background.strategic_alignment: Prerequisite for autonomous swarm authorization (MAA/CAA trials). Supports Project Alvina attritable-mass experimentation (emergent behavior in large swarms is critical challenge). Enables GCAP platform early autonomy work.
background.reasoning: SwarmEmergentBoundedness is the most-nascent value in this set. Status (3 behaviors) vs Tolerable (95% flight hours in bounds) are measuring different things: the count of behaviors identified vs. the test coverage of those behaviors. This confusion reflects that swarm assurance is still being defined. Large gap (Status far below Tolerable) and improving trend (1-2 behaviors per year) indicate active learning.

qc_findings.finding_title: Novel domain with nascent practice; status far below required test coverage
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (3 emergent behaviors identified) is far below Tolerable (95% of test flight hours within bounds). This is not a simple gap—it reflects the fundamental immaturity of swarm assurance. The scale conflates two metrics: behavior identification (3 behaviors present) vs test coverage (95% of hours in bounds). Current status measures only inventory; lacks test evidence. No standard taxonomy of emergent swarm behaviors exists (EUROCAE WG6 draft 2026). Without explicit inventory of expected behaviors and quantified boundaries, trial cannot assess whether observed swarm dynamics are safe or anomalous.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Urgent: EUROCAE WG6 swarm-behavior taxonomy development (target 2026 publication). Interim: AMLAS case studies establish accepted behaviors. Ray's METER-009 (Emergent Behavior Boundary Documentation Audit) requires operator to identify >= 5 distinct behaviors (formation, coordination latency, flocking, decision deadlock, split/rejoin) with quantified boundaries. Ray's METER-010 (Emergent Behavior Flight Test Coverage Validator) accumulates evidence: >= 95% of flight hours in bounds, out-of-bounds triggers investigation. Define decision policy: if behavior exceeds bounds in > 5% of flights, trial pauses pending investigation. Establish baseline compliance monitoring.
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 3 behaviors vs Tolerable 5 vs Ideal 8; Isaac Constraint: 95% test coverage within bounds; Ray METER-009, METER-010: Emergent Behavior audits; Raj Background: SwarmEmergentBoundedness critical priority rank 7, largest novel-domain gap; Tom Targets: Wish 5, Goal 6, Stretch 7 (all achievable if Tolerable is clarified)

scale.ambition_level: ML components are assurable and forensically explainable
defospam_finding_refs: Dorothy-4: Forensically explainable undefined; unrealistic standard may result (CRITICAL)
Observation-7: REQ009 ML explainability failure outcome undefined (decision override, logging, abort?)
scale.scale: Percentage of safety-critical ML model decisions where the explainability evidence allows independent verification of the decision logic. Evidence sufficiency determined by model type: decision trees = 100% requirement; SVMs = feature importance + decision boundary; neural networks = saliency maps + LIME/SHAP with >= 80% decision-path coverage.

scale_parameters.TestSet: hold-out test dataset used to evaluate explainability (e.g., 500 representative decision cases)
scale_parameters.SafetyCriticality: usage context (safety-critical = affects loss-of-separation, termination; non-safety-critical = affects performance optimization)

sub_scales: ModelTransparencyLevel - by-model-type explainability requirement
ReviewerReconstruction - percentage of test decisions where independent reviewers can reconstruct the decision
FrozenModelDocumentation - presence of frozen-model manifest (training data provenance, hyperparameters, validation performance)
DecisionLogging - presence of runtime logging capturing ML decision inputs, internal states, and explainability evidence
scale.reasoning: DeFOSPAM findings flagged that forensically explainable is unrealistic for neural networks but undefined for other model types. This Scale operationalizes forensically explainable pragmatically with model-type-specific approaches. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_definition: For each safety-critical ML model (e.g., autonomy-mode classifier, emergency-decision network), a post-hoc forensic test is conducted: (1) Select N=50 representative decisions from logged flight data. (2) For each decision, freeze the model state at that moment (inputs, weights, internal activations if available). (3) Provide explainability evidence (model type dependent): decision-tree = decision path (unambiguous), SVM = feature importance + decision boundary visualization, neural network = saliency map + LIME/SHAP explanation. (4) Ask two independent domain experts (not the model developers): 'Given the explainability evidence, do you agree this decision makes sense?' Score: agrees (1), partially agrees (0.5), disagrees (0). (5) Target: >= 80% of decisions scored as agrees/partially-agrees by both reviewers. Cost: 2-3 hours per model. Run before first autonomous flight; repeat after model retraining.
meters.measurement_tool: Model decision log (timestamp + inputs + decision), frozen model state snapshot, explainability framework (LIME/SHAP for neural networks, feature importance for SVM, decision path for trees), independent reviewer scoring sheet
meters.measurement_owner: ML assurance specialist (human) + domain expert reviewers; Workbench captures model state logs
meters.reasoning: MLForensicExplainability Scale requires reviewer reconstruction of decisions. This Meter IS the forensic test. Accuracy is medium because expert judgment on 'makes sense' is subjective. Precision is medium for the same reason. Cost is medium (expert time). Credibility is high because independent reviewers + standardized evidence framework. Confidence: 8.

past.source: AMLAS pilot program; industry survey of UAS ML practices (ASTM F38 working group)
past.estimation_note: Early ML-based UAS systems (2023-2024) had minimal explainability instrumentation. Decision trees scored high (>80% explainability); neural networks very low (~5-10%). Weighted average estimated at 20%.

status.source: AMLAS/SACE pilot outcomes; evolving industry practice on LIME/SHAP adoption
status.estimation_note: Modest improvement from AMLAS case studies demonstrating LIME/SHAP instrumentation and frozen-model documentation. Status estimated at 28% based on slow industry adoption of explainability tooling.

record.holder: Academic research systems (Centre for Assuring Autonomy; University of Bristol safety-critical ML lab); AMLAS pilot frontrunners with decision-tree-based systems
record.source: Published AMLAS case studies; academic ML assurance research

ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale: >= 80% decision-path coverage via saliency maps + LIME/SHAP for neural networks; 100% for decision trees. Ideal set at 90% to reflect practical limits of neural network interpretability.

trend.source: LIME/SHAP tooling maturity; AMLAS guidance adoption; regulatory pressure on ML transparency

data_gaps: CAA does not publish ML explainability benchmarks. Request data from AMLAS integration workstream or create CAA-sponsored benchmark study.
ASTM F38 ML assurance standard is in development (target: 2026). Explainability metrics will be refined; current benchmarks may shift.
Frozen-model documentation and decision logging practices are not systematically measured. Recommend CAA mandate this as a certification KPI for ML-based systems.
benchmarks.reasoning: MLForensicExplainability is a critical challenge for autonomous swarm regulation. Current Status (28%) is dangerously far from Ideal (90%), reflecting the fundamental tension between model performance (neural networks, which are opaque) and explainability (decision trees, which are slower). The 62% gap is the single largest data gap in this analysis. Status improvement relies on industry adoption of explainability tooling (LIME/SHAP, saliency mapping) and CAA acceptance of proxy explanations for neural networks.

stakeholders.name: CAA AI/ML Assurance Specialist (REQ009)
stakeholders.specific_need: ML decision logic is verifiable by independent reviewers (decision trees = 100%, neural networks = LIME/SHAP with >= 80% coverage) so that safety assurance case can be closed and operational deployment is defensible

stakeholders.name: AAIB / Flight Safety Investigator (post-incident)
stakeholders.specific_need: ML model decisions captured and explainable from logged decision inputs and feature saliency so that incident root cause can be determined forensically
stakeholders.target_override: Maximal explainability (every decision path reconstructable); zero black-box models

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Autonomy Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Practical definition of 'forensically explainable' with model-type-specific approaches so that cost-prohibitive explainability requirements are avoided (e.g., decision trees acceptable, neural networks require SHAP, SVMs require feature importance)
stakeholders.target_override: Lower explainability threshold for non-safety-critical decisions (e.g., performance optimization) vs safety-critical decisions (full reconstruction required)

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: ML components assurable in safety case so that hazard mitigation claims can be verified. Explainability is one part of broader assurance case (training data provenance, validation performance, operational monitoring)

stakeholders.name: Defence Aviation Safety Board / MOD Safety Authority
stakeholders.specific_need: If trial uses classified data or MOD models, explainability must be demonstrable without revealing classified information. Security vs transparency trade-off.

conflicts: CRITICAL CONFLICT: AAIB wants maximal explainability (every decision path reconstructable, zero black-box); Applicant wants practical threshold (neural networks with SHAP >= 80% coverage acceptable). Conflict: AI/ML Assurance Specialist and AAIB have power (post-incident investigation authority); applicant must invest in explainability. Alexa's scale operationalizes pragmatic approach: decision-tree models = 100%, neural networks = saliency maps + LIME/SHAP with >= 80% coverage. This is middle ground but weighted toward explainability.

Cost vs assurance: Applicant prefers simpler models (SVMs, decision trees) to avoid explainability cost; Autonomy Engineer prefers neural networks (better performance on swarm coordination decisions). Conflict: explainability requirement incentivizes simpler models; applicant must choose between performance and cost.

Safety-critical vs performance: Applicant wants lower explainability for performance-optimization decisions (e.g., trajectory smoothing); AI/ML Specialist argues all swarm-coordination decisions are safety-critical. Conflict: scale defaults to safety-critical = full reconstruction, non-safety-critical = feature importance sufficient. Burden is on applicant to justify low-criticality classification.
stakeholders.reasoning: AI/ML Assurance Specialist has regulatory authority over ML assurance case and must defend explainability to AAIB and Safety-Case Reviewer. AAIB (post-incident) has power to demand maximum forensic detail. Applicant must design, train, and document ML models with explainability in mind from the start. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-4 flagged 'forensically explainable' as unrealistic for neural networks; Alexa's scale operationalizes pragmatic model-type-specific approaches. MLForensicExplainability is a primary conflict hotspot (cost vs assurance vs performance).

constraints.unit: percentage of decisions reconstructable by independent reviewer
constraints.conditions: Decision trees 100%; SVMs feature importance + boundary; neural networks saliency + LIME/SHAP >= 80% coverage. >= 80% safety-critical decisions independently reconstructable.
constraints.regulatory_anchor: AAIB Accident Investigation Regulations 2015 investigability requirement
constraints.reasoning: Tolerable: >= 80%. Status 28% dangerously low; 52-point gap largest in analysis. Ensures AAIB can investigate most decisions.

constraints.conditions: below 70%, AAIB refuses to investigate black-box decisions, blocking authorization
constraints.regulatory_anchor: AAIB Accident Investigation Regulations 2015
constraints.reasoning: Survival: below 70%, system is unassurable. AAIB will not investigate.

targets.conditions: >= 80% of safety-critical ML decisions explainable via model-type-specific evidence (decision-tree: 100%, SVM: feature importance + boundary, neural network: LIME/SHAP with >= 80% coverage)

increments.milestone: by end Phase 2 (ML assurance case framework)

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA AI/ML Assurance Specialist (REQ009) — forensic ML evidence; Applicant — cost-driven model selection (trees vs neural nets)
targets.reasoning: Wish = 80% explainability reflects vocal regulator ambition to mainstream ML assurance. Current 28% status is dangerously low. 80% is achievable with LIME/SHAP adoption, frozen-model documentation, and explicit model-type selection criteria.

increments.milestone: by first approved MPTF (Model Preparation Test Flight)

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA AI/ML Assurance Specialist (REQ009) — committed ML explainability standard; CAA Safety-Case Reviewer — hazard cause-effect traceability
targets.reasoning: Goal = 90% by end 2027 is the team-committed ML assurance standard. Requires ASTM F38 ML standard adoption, AMLAS guidance dissemination, and operator investment in explainability tooling. Stretch feasibility due to neural network opacity challenge.

targets.conditions: >= 95% of safety-critical ML decisions with certified explainability (independent reviewer sign-off on decision reconstruction), certified frozen-model provenance (hash + timestamp), and automated anomaly-detection logging of out-of-distribution inputs
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA AI/ML Assurance Specialist — aspirational model forensic confidence; CAA Flight Safety Investigator (REQ012) — MOR investigation readiness
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 95% with certified provenance and anomaly detection. Feasibility is uncertain because certified explainability certification infrastructure does not yet exist in aerospace.

background.owner: Applicant (ML model developer) + CAA AI/ML assurance specialist + AAIB (investigative interest)
background.authority: CAA AI/ML assurance authority (emerging; parallels AMLAS/SACE work)
background.motivation: ML-based autonomy decision-making (mode selection, emergency response, formation adjustment) must be forensically explainable for post-incident investigation. Black-box neural networks defeat AAIB investigative capacity and block authorization based on AI-safety grounds.

risks.risk: Status (28%) far below Tolerable (80%) and dangerously close to Survival (70%); indicates widespread lack of ML explainability instrumentation
risks.mitigation: AMLAS guidance mandates explainability approach by model type: decision trees (100%), SVMs (feature importance + boundary visualization), neural networks (LIME/SHAP saliency maps). Ray's METER-011 tests reconstruction by independent reviewer (>= 80% target).

risks.risk: Neural networks (high performance) are inherently opaque; SHAP/LIME explanations are proxy approximations, not ground truth. AAIB may reject proxy explanations as insufficient for accident investigation.
risks.mitigation: Recommend decision-tree or SVM models for safety-critical decisions (mode selection, termination trigger). Reserve neural networks for non-critical optimization (swarm formation smoothing). AMLAS/SACE case studies establish acceptable proxy explanations.

risks.risk: Gap from Status (28%) to Survival (70%) is only 42%; system could enter unassurable state very quickly if additional ML components are added
risks.mitigation: Freeze model development after authorization gate. All ML components must pass explainability test (METER-011) before integration. Post-deployment model updates require re-certification.

background.value_of_value: Forensic integrity and public trust. If ML decisions cannot be explained post-incident, investigation is blocked and public confidence in autonomous swarms is destroyed. Regulatory impact: failure on this value means AAIB refusal to investigate, which blocks authorization on AI-safety grounds (links to JSP 936 Part 1 RAISO accountability).
background.strategic_alignment: Links to JSP 936 Part 1 (RAISO accountability for autonomy). Supports MAA interest in Project Alvina (attritable swarms with autonomy). Enables early GCAP autonomy work by establishing explainability baseline.
background.keystone_reference: K7 (ML forensic explainability for post-incident investigation)
background.reasoning: MLForensicExplainability is the single-largest gap in this analysis: 62% gap from Status (28%) to Ideal (90%). This reflects the fundamental tension between model performance (neural networks are fast) and explainability (neural networks are opaque). Gap closure depends on industry adoption of explainability tooling (LIME/SHAP) and regulatory acceptance of proxy explanations.

qc_findings.finding_title: Largest gap in analysis: status alarmingly below tolerable
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (28% of safety-critical ML decisions reconstructable) is dangerously close to Isaac's Survival threshold (70%) and far below Tolerable (80%). The 62-point gap from Status to Ideal (90%) is the single-largest quality risk in this requirement set. This reflects widespread lack of ML explainability instrumentation: operators rely on high-performance neural networks (opaque) without LIME/SHAP/saliency-map explanations. Post-incident investigation will be blocked if AAIB cannot reconstruct ML decisions. This is an investigability crisis.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Immediate action: AMLAS guidance mandates explainability by model type (decision trees 100%, SVMs feature importance + boundary, neural networks LIME/SHAP >= 80% coverage). Ray's METER-011 tests reconstruction by independent reviewer (>= 80% target). Recommend decision-tree or SVM models for safety-critical decisions; reserve neural networks for non-critical optimization. Freeze model development post-authorization; all ML components require re-certification if updated. Short-term: industry adoption of LIME/SHAP tooling and AAIB acceptance of proxy explanations.
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 28% vs Tolerable 80% vs Ideal 90%; Isaac Survival: 70% (system near unassurable threshold); Ray METER-011: ML Decision Reconstruction Forensic Test; Raj Background: MLForensicExplainability critical priority rank 8; Tom Target: Wish/Goal/Stretch targets unreachable without Status improvement

defospam_finding_refs: Dorothy-2: Meaningful human control lacks measurable definition (CRITICAL)
Observation-8: REQ010 Crew take-over fails - outcome when crew cannot regain manual control is undefined
scale.scale: Composite score across four dimensions of human control, each scored 0-100: (1) SituationalAwareness: crew update frequency on swarm state (target >= 2Hz for nominal, >= 10Hz for off-nominal). (2) AuthorityPreservation: percentage of decisions requiring explicit crew authorization (target: 100% for irreversible decisions, >= 80% for reversible). (3) TakeoverLatency: seconds available for crew to regain control before unsafe state (target: >= 10 seconds). (4) WorkloadBounds: maximum concurrent events and duty cycle per hour (target: <= 3 concurrent alarms, <= 6 minutes active control per hour).

scale_parameters.OperationalPhase: mission phase (pre-flight, takeoff, transit, encounter, termination, recovery)
scale_parameters.DecisionType: decision category (irreversible such as flight termination, reversible such as re-routing, autonomous such as obstacle avoidance)

sub_scales: SituationalAwarenessScore - [0-100] based on state update frequency and display comprehensiveness
AuthorityPreservationScore - [0-100] percentage of decisions requiring crew sign-off
TakeoverLatencyScore - [0-100] based on time available to regain control
WorkloadScore - [0-100] based on concurrent event load and duty cycle
scale.reasoning: DeFOSPAM findings flagged that meaningful human control is inherently subjective and lacks measurable criteria. This Scale operationalizes meaningful by defining four measurable dimensions (awareness, authority, speed, workload) commonly used in human factors standards. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_name: Crew State-Update Frequency & Display Completeness Audit
meters.meter_definition: Human factors inspector reviews ConOps and UI mockups/actual system. Verifies crew display updates swarm state at target frequency: nominal operations >= 2Hz (swarm position, health, altitude), off-nominal scenarios >= 10Hz (alerts, DAA events, link status). Inspector also audits display completeness: does crew see (1) individual aircraft state (position, velocity, altitude, health), (2) swarm-level metrics (formation geometry, inter-aircraft separation, consensus state), (3) external threats (detected intruders, geofence proximity, weather radar), (4) crew command authority (what decisions does crew control vs. autonomy?). Scoring rubric: 0-100 scale where 100 = full state visibility at required frequency, 0 = no display. Target: >= 80/100. Cost: 2 hours for audit. Run once before crew training begins.
meters.measurement_tool: Display audit checklist (component list); frequency measurement (Hz counter in live system or simulator); human factors scoring rubric
meters.measurement_owner: CAA human factors inspector (human domain expert)
meters.reasoning: MeaningfulHumanControl sub-scale is SituationalAwarenessScore (0-100). This Meter is the display audit. Accuracy and precision are medium because display adequacy is subjective. Cost is low. Speed is high. Credibility is medium; the real test is whether crew performs well in simulation/flight (METER-013). Confidence: 7.

meters.meter_name: Crew Workload & Takeover Latency Simulation Test
meters.meter_definition: Crew is placed in high-fidelity simulator with full swarm flight scenario. Scenario unfolds: (1) Nominal transit (baseline workload measurement), (2) Unexpected event injection (e.g., DAA encounter, C2 glitch, swarm member health alert). During simulation: (1) Crew's workload is measured via NASA-TLX questionnaire at key points (target: workload <= 70% during nominal, <= 85% during events). (2) Takeover latency is measured: from time an alert is displayed to time crew issues manual command (target: <= 5 seconds). (3) Concurrent event load is counted: how many simultaneous alarms/alerts? (target: <= 3 concurrent). (4) Duty cycle is logged: total time crew is actively controlling aircraft (target: <= 6 minutes per hour). Test runs 5 scenarios, each 30-60 minutes. Composite score (0-100): weight as (50% workload) + (25% takeover latency) + (25% event load). Target: >= 80/100. Cost: 1-2 days for test design + crew prep + run + analysis. Run once before first flight; repeat if crew composition changes.
meters.measurement_tool: High-fidelity flight simulator (visual, motion if available); NASA-TLX workload questionnaire; event-timing instrument (millisecond-level latency measurement); duty-cycle timer
meters.measurement_owner: CAA human factors inspector (test designer + observer) + independent test pilot; Workbench simulator provides scenario execution
meters.reasoning: MeaningfulHumanControl Scale specifies workload, takeover latency, concurrent events. This Meter is the simulation test. Accuracy is high because latencies and workload are objectively measured. Precision is medium because simulator fidelity varies. Cost is high (full simulator time). Speed is low (days). Credibility is VERY high because it's grounded in human factors standards (NASA-TLX) and realistic simulation. This is load-bearing: without evidence of adequate crew control, authorization cannot be granted. Confidence: 9.

past.source: CAA human factors inspection data; ASTM F38 early human factors guidance
past.estimation_note: FY 2024 BVLOS operators showed moderate human control: situational awareness typically >= 2Hz, authority preservation ~70% for reversible decisions, takeover latency >= 10 seconds available. Composite estimated at 45 (SituationalAwareness: 60, AuthorityPreservation: 50, TakeoverLatency: 70, WorkloadBounds: 20).

status.source: Recent BVLOS swarm trial human factors assessments; AMLAS/SACE crew training data
status.estimation_note: Modest improvement in crew training and HMI design. Status estimated at 52 (SituationalAwareness: 65, AuthorityPreservation: 55, TakeoverLatency: 75, WorkloadBounds: 35). Swarm operations increase workload burden.

record.holder: Military crewed aircraft with advanced cockpit design (e.g., F-35, Apache); leading civil operators with mature HMI (Airborne Support)
record.source: Military aviation human factors standards (MIL-STD-1787, MIL-HDBK-759); industry HMI best practices

ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale: SituationalAwareness >= 2Hz nominal / >= 10Hz off-nominal; AuthorityPreservation >= 100% for irreversible / >= 80% for reversible; TakeoverLatency >= 10 seconds; WorkloadBounds <= 3 concurrent alarms, <= 6 min active control/hour. Ideal score reflects full compliance with all four dimensions.

trend.source: HMI design maturity; crew training standardization; swarm automation limiting crew decision load

data_gaps: CAA does not publish human factors assessment metrics. Request data from human factors inspection team or conduct structured survey of recent BVLOS trials.
No standard human factors workload measurement tool exists for UAS crews. ASTM F38 is developing guidance; recommend CAA adopt when available.
Swarm-specific human factors (multi-aircraft display design, alert prioritization, crew coordination) are rarely assessed. Recommend CAA commission dedicated human factors study for swarm operations.
benchmarks.reasoning: MeaningfulHumanControl is inherently subjective but measurable via four converging dimensions. Current Status (52) is moderately below Ideal (100), reflecting ongoing design work in crewed-autonomous teaming. The largest deficit is WorkloadBounds (35 vs 100): swarm operations generate multiple concurrent events (engine failure, loss-of-link, emergent behavior anomaly) that can overwhelm crews. Trend is improving but constrained by the fundamental challenge of managing high-dimensional swarm state with limited crew attention.

stakeholders.name: CAA Human Factors Inspector (REQ010)
stakeholders.specific_need: Crew situational awareness (>= 2Hz nominal, >= 10Hz off-nominal), authority preservation (100% on irreversible, >= 80% on reversible decisions), takeover latency (>= 10 seconds), and workload bounds (>= 3 concurrent alarms, <= 6 min/hour control) demonstrate meaningful human control throughout

stakeholders.name: CAA Autonomy Reviewer (REQ008)
stakeholders.specific_need: Autonomous decision boundaries and override capability defined so that crew can interrupt and regain control in off-nominal states (relates to emergent behavior recovery)

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Quantified meaningful control thresholds (awareness frequency, authority percentage, latency bounds) to design crew interface and training, avoiding open-ended 'meaningful' requirement
stakeholders.tolerable_override: Lower state update frequency (1Hz nominal) if crew interface displays aggregate swarm health in real-time
stakeholders.target_override: Higher automation (< 80% authority preservation) if autonomous safety margins are wide and crew training emphasizes monitoring vs intervention

stakeholders.name: Applicant Flight Test Crew (Pilot, Sensor Operator)
stakeholders.specific_need: Crew interface and control latency enable safe and usable monitoring and intervention during flight test. Not overly workload-intensive. Tangible feedback on system state.

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: Crew takeover and termination decision points clear in ConOps so that emergency procedures can be trained and practiced

stakeholders.name: RAISO / Range Safety Officer
stakeholders.specific_need: If on MOD range: crew control must enable range safety officer to interdict flight if needed. Meaningful control supports range safety integration.

conflicts: CRITICAL CONFLICT: Human Factors Inspector wants high control authority (100% on irreversible, >= 80% on reversible) and fast takeover (>= 10 sec); Applicant prefers high autonomy (>= 90% autonomous decisions) to reduce crew workload and cost. Conflict: meaningful human control is a regulatory principle (CAP 722 requirement); Applicant must accept >= 80% authority preservation on reversible decisions. Takeover latency (10 seconds) is achievable with modern autopilots and crew interfaces.

Situational awareness trade-off: Human Factors Inspector wants high update frequency (>= 2Hz nominal, >= 10Hz off-nominal) to enable meaningful control; Applicant wants lower frequency (1Hz) to reduce interface complexity and crew workload. Conflict: scale requires >= 2Hz nominal (industry standard for UAS situational awareness); off-nominal can be negotiated down to 5Hz if safety-critical alerts are instant.

Workload vs autonomy: Applicant wants high autonomy to reduce crew mental load (cost-driven); Human Factors Inspector worries that over-automation creates monitoring fatigue and complacency (safety concern). Conflict: scale balances both by limiting concurrent alarms (>= 3) and active control duty cycle (<= 6 min/hour) so crew remains engaged without burnout.
stakeholders.reasoning: Human Factors Inspector has regulatory authority over meaningful human control and crew fitness and must defend control authority to CAA, Parliament (EU Regulation 2019/945 compliance), and public. Applicant prefers automation (lower cost) but regulatory requirement forces human-in-the-loop design. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-2 flagged 'meaningful' as undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes it via four measurable dimensions (awareness, authority, latency, workload). MeaningfulHumanControl is a top conflict hotspot (automation vs safety).

constraints.conditions: SituationalAwareness >= 2Hz nominal / >= 10Hz off-nominal; AuthorityPreservation >= 80% reversible / 100% irreversible; TakeoverLatency >= 10s; WorkloadBounds <= 3 concurrent alarms, <= 6 min active/hour. Score 70+.
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722 human oversight; EU Regulation 2019/945 operational authorization criteria
constraints.reasoning: Tolerable composite 70 reflects balance between autonomy and crew authority. Status 52 below Tolerable. Largest deficit WorkloadBounds (35/100)—swarm events overwhelm crew. Crew must retain meaningful control.

targets.conditions: Composite score >= 70 across SituationalAwareness (>= 2Hz nominal update frequency), AuthorityPreservation (>= 90% of irreversible decisions require crew sign-off), TakeoverLatency (>= 10 seconds available)

increments.milestone: by first joint panel (human factors readiness)

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Human Factors Inspector (REQ010) — committed human control standard; CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011) — contingency trigger clarity
