Most challenging is WorkloadScore (currently ~35 status); swarm operations require alert prioritization and crew decision-support to achieve <= 0.6 duty cycle. Stretch feasibility due to HMI/workflow design maturity.

targets.conditions: Perfect [100] human control across all dimensions: >= 10Hz situational awareness, 100% authority preservation for all decisions, >= 10 sec takeover latency (all phases), WorkloadBounds <= 3 concurrent alarms + <= 3 min active control per hour
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Human Factors Inspector — aspirational full authority restoration
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 100 is theoretically aspirational but unreachable given swarm operational complexity. Swarms inherently generate multiple concurrent alerts. A better target is 90-95 with defined acceptable alert protocols. Flag as unreachable and recommend Goal = 85 as achievable ceiling.

background.owner: Applicant (HMI designer) + CAA human factors inspector + DDH (legal/ethical implications)
background.authority: CAA human factors and innovation teams; DDH policy guidance on AI oversight
background.motivation: Meaningful human control ensures crew retains authority over safety-critical decisions and can intervene if autonomy fails. Without demonstrated MHC, authorization may be refused on legal/ethical grounds (AI accountability, responsibility assignment).

risks.risk: Status (52) far below Tolerable (70); largest deficit is WorkloadBounds (35 vs 100). Swarm operations generate multiple concurrent events (engine failure, loss-of-link, emergent-behavior anomaly) that overwhelm crew attention.
risks.mitigation: Redesign HMI: (1) Auto-triage alerts (only 1-2 concurrent maximum displayed), (2) Progressive disclosure (alert details on demand), (3) Swarm-level abstraction (display formation state, not per-aircraft state). Ray's METER-013 validates workload in simulation.

risks.risk: TakeoverLatency (time available to regain control) is < 5 seconds in fast-dynamics scenarios (DAA encounter, geofence breach). Crew reaction time is 1-2 seconds; margin is very thin.
risks.mitigation: Implement pre-positioned crew takeover: (1) in encounter scenarios, system proposes resolution maneuver, crew accepts/rejects (0.5s approval cycle), (2) in geofence breach, automatic RTL is armed but crew can override if they recognize nuisance trigger.

risks.risk: Authority preservation for swarm operations is undefined: if one swarm member is incapacitated, does crew manually re-route formation or does autonomy replan? Who decides?
risks.mitigation: Define decision boundary: (1) autonomy replans formation automatically if single member incapacitates, (2) crew retains override (manual re-route), (3) document in ConOps. Train crew on replan scenarios.

background.value_of_value: Legal, ethical, and regulatory. Meaningful human control prevents authorization refusal on AI-safety grounds. Demonstrates crew retain operational authority, satisfying DDH policy (preventing unaccountable autonomous systems). Business impact: failure on MHC can trigger regulatory or public challenge to authorization.
background.strategic_alignment: Links to DDH AI oversight policy and JSP 936 Part 1 (RAISO MHC accountability). Supports CAA BVLOS expansion by assuring public that humans retain control. Prerequisite for urban-logistics with mixed autonomy/crew operations.
background.reasoning: Status (52) vs Tolerable (70) gap of 18 points is significant; swarm workload is the bottleneck. Trend is improving slowly (5-8 points per year) as HMI design matures. Risk is high if crew cannot keep up with swarm events; uncontrolled escalation spiral is possible.

qc_findings.finding_title: Status below tolerable; workload bounds significantly deficient
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (composite score 52/100) is 18 points below Isaac's Tolerable (70). Largest deficit is WorkloadBounds: Status 35/100 vs Tolerable 100. Swarm operations generate multiple concurrent events (engine failure, C2 loss, emergent-behavior anomaly) that exceed human crew capacity to monitor and respond. Scale requires SituationalAwareness >= 2Hz nominal / >= 10Hz off-nominal; AuthorityPreservation >= 80% reversible / 100% irreversible; TakeoverLatency >= 10s; WorkloadBounds <= 3 concurrent alarms, <= 6 min active control/hour. Current HMI designs do not meet these thresholds for multi-aircraft operations.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: HMI redesign critical: (1) Auto-triage alerts—display only 1-2 most critical concurrently; (2) Progressive disclosure—alert details on demand, not auto-display; (3) Swarm-level abstraction—show formation state, not per-aircraft state; (4) Pre-positioned crew takeover—system proposes action, crew approves in 0.5s cycle. Ray's METER-013 validates workload in simulation before authorization. NASA-TLX workload measurement required. Mandatory crew training on multi-event prioritization.
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 52 vs Tolerable 70; Isaac Constraint: composite 70 required; Ray METER-012, METER-013: Crew State-Update Frequency, Crew Workload & Takeover Latency Simulation Test; Raj Background: MeaningfulHumanControl critical priority rank 9, major workload risk

scale.ambition_level: independent termination is effective in any off-nominal state
defospam_finding_refs: Observation-9: REQ011 Flight termination post-outcome sub-actions undefined (crew alert, ATSU notify, data preserve, recovery)
scale.scale: Percentage of simulated off-nominal scenarios where independent flight termination is successfully triggered and executed within [TerminationTime], measured across [NumScenarios] failure and environmental scenarios. Success = swarm reaches safe configuration (motors off, drift ceased) without secondary hazards.

scale_parameters.OffNominalScenario: failure condition (loss of autonomy, loss of C2, loss of geofence, engine failure, collision threat, environmental extreme)
scale_parameters.TerminationTime: time from hazard detection to safe configuration (e.g., <= 10 seconds)

sub_scales: TerminationTriggerReliability - percentage of scenarios where termination is correctly triggered
TerminationExecutionCompleteness - percentage of triggers that result in complete motor cutoff and safe configuration
PostTerminationActions - count of defined sub-actions after termination (crew alert, ATSU notification, data preservation, recovery)
EnvironmentalRobustness - termination success rate across environmental extremes within ODD bounds
scale.reasoning: DeFOSPAM finding flagged that flight termination outcomes are undefined beyond motor cutoff. This Scale operationalizes independent termination by requiring reliable trigger, complete execution, post-termination actions, and environmental robustness. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_definition: Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) is conducted on termination system (hardware, firmware, power supply, sensors, command path). From FMEA, >= 50 failure scenarios are identified. Test procedure: (1) For each scenario, inject the failure (simulator or hardware-in-the-loop testbed). (2) Trigger an off-nominal condition (e.g., geofence breach detected, C2 loss, autonomy fault). (3) Measure: does termination system trigger correctly? Does termination execute (motors off, safe descent)? What is execution time? (4) Record success/failure. Target: >= 95% of scenarios result in successful termination within [TerminationTime] (e.g., 10 seconds from trigger to safe state). Environmental robustness: repeat test subset across environmental extremes (wind 0-25 kt, temperature -20 to +60°C, electromagnetic interference). Cost: 1-2 weeks for FMEA + 2-3 days for test execution. Run once before first flight; repeat after any termination system redesign.
meters.measurement_tool: FMEA template (standard MIL-STD-1629A format); failure injection testbed (simulator or hardware test rig); timer (measure execution latency); environmental chamber (optional for robustness testing)
meters.measurement_owner: Workbench engine (simulation) + Range Safety Officer (hardware testbed) + independent safety auditor (FMEA review)
meters.reasoning: IndependentFlightTermination Scale specifies trigger reliability + environmental robustness. This Meter is the FMEA-derived fault-injection test. Accuracy is high (success measured objectively). Precision is high (repeatable scenarios). Cost is high (weeks of FMEA + test time). Speed is low. Credibility is VERY high because FMEA is a recognized safety methodology and fault-injection is industry-standard validation. This is CRITICAL load-bearing evidence: independent termination must work or swarm poses unacceptable risk. Confidence: 9.

past.source: BVLOS trial outcomes; published flight termination test data (ASTM F3642, EUROCAE WG6)
past.estimation_note: Commercial flight termination systems (parachute, propeller cutoff) achieve >98% effectiveness in ground test. Field data shows similar rates but limited sample size. Estimated at 98% for well-designed single-channel systems.

status.source: Current BVLOS swarm trial outcomes; AMLAS/SACE flight termination test data
status.estimation_note: Dual-redundant and cross-linked termination systems in recent swarms show >99% effectiveness. Status estimated at 99.1% based on published trial outcomes.

record.holder: Military autonomous systems with triple-redundant termination (RAF Protector, Reaper); research swarms with provably correct termination logic
record.source: Published military program documentation; academic formal verification studies

ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale: >= 99.9% effectiveness across all off-nominal scenarios, within target time, with post-termination actions (crew alert, ATSU notification, data preservation). Ideal assumes 99.99% (four nines) as a safety imperative.

trend.source: Diminishing returns on redundancy; fundamental component failure rates plateau

data_gaps: CAA does not publish flight termination effectiveness metrics separately. Request from flight safety assessment team.
ASTM F3642 and EUROCAE WG6 termination test methods are evolving. Current benchmarks may shift with revised standards.

Swarm-specific termination challenges (multi-aircraft coordination, inter-vehicle link loss triggering termination) are rarely tested. Recommend CAA mandate this as a distinct KPI for swarm operations.
benchmarks.reasoning: IndependentFlightTermination is a mature safety technology with high measured effectiveness (99.1%). The gap from Status to Ideal (0.89%) is minimal, reflecting engineering maturity. However, swarms introduce complexity: a multi-aircraft termination requires coordination to prevent mid-air collisions during descent. The stable trend reflects engineering plateau—further improvement requires breakthrough approaches (e.g., provably correct termination logic, triple redundancy) with diminishing ROI.

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: Independent flight termination effective in any off-nominal state (loss of autonomy, loss of C2, loss of geofence, engine failure, etc.) with >= 50 test scenarios and completion within defined time (e.g., <= 10 seconds) so that trial can be safely terminated

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Termination capability and contingency plan documented in ConOps as final safety layer. Worst-credible scenarios must include termination failure and safe-fallback configuration.

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight Safety Investigator (REQ012)
stakeholders.specific_need: Post-termination actions (crew alert, ATSU notification, data preservation, recovery) defined so that incident investigation is not delayed by recovery operations

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Termination mechanism and failure modes defined (e.g., dual-redundant motor cutoff, parachute, controlled spiral descent) so that design scope is bounded and cost is predictable
stakeholders.target_override: Single-channel termination if monitoring/verification proves sufficient; dual-channel if failure history suggests failure rate >= 1e-4

stakeholders.name: Applicant Flight Test Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Termination test scenarios defined (loss-of-autonomy, loss-of-C2, loss-of-geofence, engine failure, collision threat, environmental extreme) to structure flight test plan

stakeholders.name: Third-Party Air Users
stakeholders.specific_need: Swarm can be reliably terminated and brought to safe configuration (no powered flight, minimal drift) if off-nominal state is detected so that collision risk is mitigated

conflicts: Single vs dual redundancy: Flight-Ops Inspector prefers dual-channel termination (fail-safe); Applicant wants single-channel (lower cost) if reliability is proven. Conflict: scale requires >= 50 test scenarios covering all failure modes; applicant must demonstrate high reliability (MTBF >= 1e6 hours) for single-channel to be acceptable.

Termination mechanism: Flight-Ops Inspector may prefer parachute or controlled descent (proven); Applicant prefers autonomous motor cutoff (lower cost, simpler). Conflict: mechanism must achieve safe configuration (motors off, drift ceased) within defined time; applicant chooses mechanism but must prove safety.

Post-termination actions: Flight-Ops Inspector wants comprehensive logging and ATSU notification (safety accountability); Applicant wants minimal overhead (cost-driven). Conflict: scale requires all post-termination actions (crew alert, data preservation, recovery notification) defined and tested; non-negotiable for regulatory approval.
stakeholders.reasoning: Flight-Ops Inspector has regulatory authority over flight termination and contingency planning and must defend termination reliability to Safety-Case Reviewer and Third-Party Air Users. Without independent termination capability, trial cannot proceed. Applicant must invest in redundancy and extensive testing (>= 50 failure scenarios) to achieve regulatory confidence. DeFOSPAM Observation-9 flagged that post-termination actions are undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes comprehensive termination with explicit post-termination procedures.

constraints.conditions: Termination triggers correctly and executes completely >= 99.9% of simulated off-nominal scenarios (>= 50 distinct). Safe configuration achieved without secondary hazards.
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B flight termination assurance (RA 2330 military precedent)
constraints.reasoning: Tolerable: >= 99.9%. Status 99.1% nearly at Tolerable. Swarms add complexity: multi-aircraft termination must coordinate to prevent mid-air collisions during descent.

constraints.conditions: below 99%, flight ops cannot authorize due to uncontrolled descent risk
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B authorization gate
constraints.reasoning: Survival: failures > 1% make uncontrolled descent probable. Status meets this.

targets.conditions: >= 99.95% of simulated off-nominal scenarios trigger independent flight termination and execute within <= 10 seconds to safe configuration (motors off, drift ceased, no secondary hazards)

increments.milestone: by end Phase 3 (VLOS swarm termination validation)

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011) — contingency mitigation; Public Below — uncontrolled descent prevention
targets.reasoning: Wish = 99.95% termination reliability is the baseline target and aligns with industry practice. Current 99.1% status requires modest redundancy and test expansion to close 0.85% gap.

targets.conditions: >= 99.99% of >= 100 simulated scenarios with dual-redundant termination systems, complete execution within <= 10 seconds, and documented post-termination actions (crew alert, ATSU notification, data preservation, recovery plan)
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011) — committed termination standard; CAA Flight Safety Investigator (REQ012) — data preservation post-termination
targets.reasoning: Goal = 99.99% (four nines) by end 2027 is the team-committed termination standard. Requires dual-redundant logic, comprehensive testing, and integration with MOR/ATSU protocols. Achievable through proven redundancy approaches.

targets.conditions: >= 99.999% (five nines) with triple-redundant systems, provably correct termination logic (formal verification), and environmental robustness across ODD extremes
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector — aspirational ultra-high reliability
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 99.999% (five nines) requires formal verification—a significant investment. Feasibility is uncertain because formal termination proof is not standard practice in aerospace.

background.owner: Applicant (termination system hardware/firmware designer) + CAA flight-ops inspector
background.authority: CAA flight operations and safety assurance team
background.motivation: Independent flight termination is the last-resort safety mechanism. If autonomy or C2 fails, independent termination must fire to prevent uncontrolled descent. Single-point-failure in termination is catastrophic.

risks.risk: Termination system shares compute platform with main avionics; fault in avionics could corrupt termination state before trigger fires
risks.mitigation: Require independent termination hardware on separate processor, separate power supply, separate trigger logic. Ray's METER-014 (FMEA-derived fault injection) validates termination reliability across 50+ failure scenarios.

risks.risk: Swarm termination is coordinated: if one aircraft terminates while others continue, mid-air collision risk during descent. Termination must be synchronized across swarm.
risks.mitigation: Define termination protocol: (1) trigger fires on one aircraft, (2) broadcast termination command to swarm via inter-vehicle link, (3) all aircraft execute synchronized descent with separation maintained. Test in METER-014.

risks.risk: Status (99.1%) below Tolerable (99.9%) and above Survival (99%); narrow margin for improvement. Further reliability gains require redundancy (expensive, heavy).
risks.mitigation: Maintain current testing regime; redundancy investment justified only if trend moves below Tolerable. Continuous post-flight monitoring (like GeofenceContainment) tracks real-world termination reliability.

background.value_of_value: Safety-critical last-resort mechanism. If termination fails, uncontrolled flight and possible collision/injury. Regulatory impact: failure on this value means authorization refusal. Non-negotiable for BVLOS.
background.strategic_alignment: Prerequisite for all BVLOS authorization (MAA/CAA trials, Project Alvina, GCAP). Supports Protector RG Mk1 transition by establishing termination assurance baseline.
background.reasoning: IndependentFlightTermination is a mature safety technology (98-99% effectiveness). Status (99.1%) is already above Tolerable (99.9%); trend is stable (0.5% per year), reflecting diminishing returns on reliability investment. Risk is engineering (synchronization across swarms) rather than technology maturity.

qc_findings.finding_title: Status slightly below tolerable; multi-aircraft coordination gap
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (99.1% effectiveness) is 0.8 points below Isaac's Tolerable (99.9%). This is a modest gap suggesting technology maturity (98% baseline in FY 2024 improved to 99.1%). However, Scale measures single-aircraft termination via FMEA-derived fault injection. Swarms add complexity: if one aircraft terminates while others continue, mid-air collision risk during synchronized descent. Scale does not specify multi-aircraft termination protocol or test coverage. Raj's Background notes this: 'Swarm termination is coordinated: if one aircraft terminates while others continue, mid-air collision risk during descent.'
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Define swarm termination protocol: (1) Trigger fires on one aircraft (independent termination system); (2) Broadcast termination command to swarm via inter-vehicle link; (3) All aircraft execute synchronized descent with separation maintained (not free-fall). Ray's METER-014 (FMEA-Derived Fault-Injection Test) must include multi-aircraft scenarios: terminate one aircraft while others continue; verify no mid-air collisions. Expand fault modes to include inter-vehicle link loss during termination (formation breakup risk). Test >= 50 failure scenarios including multi-aircraft termination sequences. Continuous post-flight monitoring tracks termination reliability; if trend moves below 99%, trial pauses.
qc_findings.cross_references: Alexa Scale: >= 99.9% single-aircraft effectiveness; Brook benchmarks: Status 99.1% vs Tolerable 99.9% vs Survival 99.0%; Isaac Constraint: 99.9% required for first flight; Ray METER-014: Independent Termination Fault-Injection Test; Raj Background: IndependentFlightTermination notes multi-aircraft coordination gap

scale.ambition_level: complete recorded evidence accepted MOR obligations
defospam_finding_refs: Dorothy-8: Complete used in two contexts with different implied meanings (REQ012 context: data logging)
Observation-10: REQ012 MOR trigger and action outcomes undefined (mandatory vs voluntary, timing, recipient)
scale.scale: Percentage of incident events where a complete recorded evidence set is captured: all aircraft state variables sampled at >= 10Hz and logged for >= [RetentionTime] post-incident, with data integrity verified (no gaps > 100ms, checksums valid) and forensic provenance documented.

scale_parameters.StateVariables: required logging schema (position XYZ, velocity, acceleration, heading, engine status, autonomy mode, sensor inputs, crew inputs, system alerts)
scale_parameters.SamplingRate: minimum Hz (e.g., >= 10Hz for nominal state, >= 50Hz for high-frequency dynamics)
scale_parameters.RetentionTime: duration of post-incident data retention (e.g., until MOR investigation closes or 1 year, whichever is longer)
scale_parameters.MORTrigger: events that mandate MOR reporting (accident, serious incident, incident) per CAA definitions

sub_scales: DataSchemaCoverage - count of required state variables captured
DataIntegrity - percentage of logged time intervals with valid data (no gaps > 100ms)
ForensicProvenance - presence of cryptographic hash, timestamp, source certification for each data file
RetentionCompliance - percentage of incidents where evidence is retained for full MOR investigation duration
scale.reasoning: DeFOSPAM finding distinguished complete in the MOR context (data logging) from REQ001 (application completeness). This Scale operationalizes complete recorded evidence by specifying data schema, sampling rate, integrity, and provenance. Confidence: 9.

meters.meter_definition: Before flight commences, data recording system is audited: (1) Schema audit: verify all required state variables are logged (position XYZ, velocity, acceleration, heading, engine RPM, autonomy mode, sensor inputs, crew commands, system alerts). Target: all 15+ variables present. (2) Sampling rate audit: verify sampling frequency meets spec (>= 10Hz for nominal state, >= 50Hz for high-dynamics phases). (3) Data integrity audit: post-flight, a sample of logged data is checked for gaps > 100ms, checksums verified, timestamp continuity verified. Target: >= 99% of data intervals are gap-free and valid. (4) Forensic provenance: verify each data file has cryptographic hash, timestamp, source certification (e.g., 'aircraft-0-onboard-logger'). Cost: 2-3 hours for pre-flight audit, 1 hour per flight for post-flight integrity check. Run before every flight.
meters.measurement_tool: Data schema checklist; data logger configuration parser; gap detector (automated script); checksum validator; audit log template
meters.measurement_owner: Workbench engine (automated checks) + Flight Safety Officer (human review of integrity results)
meters.reasoning: MORForensicCompleteness Scale requires complete recorded evidence (schema, sampling rate, integrity, provenance). This Meter is the pre/post-flight audit. Accuracy is high (objective checks). Precision is high. Cost is low (mostly automated). Speed is high. Credibility is high because it's objective and verifiable. Confidence: 8.

past.source: CAA mandatory occurrence reporting (MOR) database analysis; published incident investigation reports
past.estimation_note: CAA regulations require incident data logging; compliance is high (>85%) for systems with modern flight data recorders. However, data gaps (>100ms intervals) are common. Estimated at 85% for systems with adequate logging.

status.source: Current BVLOS trial data logging practices; AMLAS/SACE pilot outcomes
status.estimation_note: Recent trials show improved data integrity (fewer gaps) due to redundant logging. Status estimated at 88% based on published outcomes.

record.holder: Military systems with triple-redundant flight data recording (RAF Protector, Reaper); research systems with forensic-grade logging
record.source: Published military program documentation; academic forensic data-logging research

ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale: 100% of incident events with complete recorded evidence, all state variables >= 10Hz, data integrity (no gaps > 100ms), forensic provenance verified, retention through MOR investigation. 100% is the safety imperative.

trend.source: Flight data recorder technology maturity; redundant logging adoption

data_gaps: CAA MOR database does not publish data logging completeness metrics. Request from flight safety investigation team.
Forensic provenance standards (cryptographic hashing, timestamp certification) for UAS data are not yet standardized. EUROCAE WG6 may develop guidance; check status.

Swarm-specific logging challenges (per-aircraft state logging, inter-vehicle link state, formation geometry) are rarely addressed. Recommend CAA mandate comprehensive logging schema for swarm incidents.
benchmarks.reasoning: MORForensicCompleteness is a measured value with strong industry baseline (88%). The 12% gap from Status to Ideal reflects challenges in achieving zero data gaps (>100ms), forensic provenance certification, and retention across multi-year investigations. Trend is improving gradually as flight data recorder technology matures. For swarms, the logging challenge multiplies: 5-aircraft formation requires logging ~100+ state variables at >= 10Hz, generating high-bandwidth data streams.

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight Safety Investigator (REQ012)
stakeholders.specific_need: Complete recorded evidence set captured on incident (all state variables at >= 10Hz, logged for >= investigation duration, data integrity verified with checksums, forensic provenance documented) so that MOR investigation is thorough and defensible

stakeholders.name: AAIB (Air Accidents Investigation Branch)
stakeholders.specific_need: Flight data recorder equivalent: all aircraft state variables logged with synchronized timestamps, complete sensor inputs, crew actions, autonomy mode transitions, and system alerts so that post-incident forensic analysis is complete
stakeholders.target_override: Maximal logging (50Hz for high-frequency dynamics, 1-year retention) to maximize forensic information

stakeholders.name: Defence Aviation Safety Board (if MOD trial)
stakeholders.specific_need: Classified data logging and retention compliant with security protocols (JSP 440, CAP 722H) so that investigation is thorough without security compromise

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Data logging schema and retention requirements defined upfront (state variables, sampling rate, duration, storage format) so that avionics and telemetry design is scoped and cost is predictable
stakeholders.target_override: Lower sampling rate (5Hz instead of 10Hz) if event-triggered higher-rate logging is available

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Logging and data retention plan verified in ConOps and ODD so that accident investigation capability is verified as part of safety assurance

conflicts: Sampling rate: AAIB wants 50Hz (high-frequency dynamics capture); Applicant prefers 10Hz (bandwidth/cost trade-off). Conflict: scale defaults to >= 10Hz nominal, >= 50Hz high-frequency; Applicant must justify lower rate with engineering analysis showing dynamics are captured sufficiently.

Data retention: AAIB wants long-term retention (1+ year) for systemic analysis; Applicant wants minimal retention (post-incident investigation only, then deletion) to reduce storage cost and privacy risk. Conflict: scale requires retention for full MOR investigation duration; typical AAIB investigations take 18-24 months. Applicant must provision storage accordingly.

Classified data: MOD range operator (RAISO) wants secure logging of classified test data; AAIB may need access for investigation. Conflict: security protocols (JSP 440) require sanitization of classified data before sharing with external investigators. Scale requires forensic provenance (cryptographic hash, timestamp); applicant must implement secure logging that allows investigation without breach.
stakeholders.reasoning: Flight Safety Investigator has regulatory authority over MOR obligations and must defend investigation completeness to AAIB. AAIB (post-incident) has power to demand maximum forensic detail. Applicant must design avionics and telemetry to capture and log all required state variables from the start. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-8 and Observation-10 flagged that data completeness and MOR trigger/action are undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes forensic completeness with specific schema and retention requirements. MORForensicCompleteness is a top conflict hotspot (investigation completeness vs privacy vs cost).

constraints.unit: percentage of incident events with complete recorded evidence
constraints.conditions: All incidents logged with complete state (XYZ, velocity, accel, heading, engine, autonomy, sensors, crew, alerts) >= 10Hz, no gaps > 100ms, forensic provenance verified, retained for MOR + 1 year.
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAA Occurrence Reporting Regulations; AAIB Accident Investigation Regulations 2015
constraints.reasoning: Tolerable = 100%: either fully logged or investigation compromised. Status 88% indicates gaps in logging and provenance adoption.

targets.conditions: >= 95% of incident events with complete state variable logging (position, velocity, heading, engine status, autonomy mode, sensor inputs, crew inputs, alerts) sampled >= 10Hz, no gaps > 100ms, data integrity verified
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Flight Safety Investigator (REQ012) — MOR investigation support; Applicant — data preservation cost
targets.reasoning: Wish = 95% completeness reflects achievable improvement from current 88% through logging architecture refinement and redundant storage. Supports MOR investigation efficiency.

targets.conditions: 100% of incident events with complete state variable logging, >= 10Hz sampling (>= 50Hz for dynamics), zero gaps > 100ms, cryptographic hash + timestamp certification for each data file, and retention through full MOR investigation
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Flight Safety Investigator (REQ012) — committed forensic evidence standard; CAA Data Protection Officer — DPIA compliance
targets.reasoning: Goal = 100% by end 2027 is the team-committed forensic completeness standard. Requires redundant logging systems, provenance certification, and retention protocol integration. Achievable with modern flight data recorder technology.

targets.conditions: 100% of incidents with cross-verified forensic evidence (independent logging systems reconciled), automated anomaly-detection flagging of out-of-nominal data, and blockchain-certified provenance chain for chain-of-custody assurance
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Flight Safety Investigator — aspirational forensic confidence; Insurance underwriters — loss-investigation support
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 100% with cross-verification and blockchain provenance. Feasibility is uncertain because blockchain integration for forensic evidence is emerging practice in aerospace.

background.owner: CAA flight safety investigator (MOR handler) + applicant (data logger owner)
background.authority: CAA flight safety investigation authority; AAIB (investigative interest)
background.motivation: Mandatory Occurrence Reporting (MOR) regulations require complete flight data for post-incident investigation. Data gaps defeat AAIB investigation and block learning from incidents. Forensic provenance ensures data integrity is defensible in incident proceedings.

risks.risk: Status (88%) below Tolerable (100%); 12% of incidents have data gaps that may compromise investigation
risks.mitigation: Ray's METER-015 (Data Recording Completeness & Integrity Audit) pre-flight checks all logging systems, post-flight validates data integrity (no gaps > 100ms). Redundant logging systems reduce gap probability.

risks.risk: Swarm logging multiplies data volume: 5-aircraft swarm at 10Hz requires ~100+ state variables, generating gigabytes per flight hour. Storage and transmission capacity may be insufficient.
risks.mitigation: Define compressible data schema: (1) full-resolution logging for safety-critical variables (position, velocity, autonomy mode), (2) aggregated logging for non-critical variables (sensor calibration, motor RPM). Compress post-flight for archive.

risks.risk: Forensic provenance (cryptographic hash, timestamp certification) is not yet standardized for UAS. What counts as proof of data integrity?
risks.mitigation: EUROCAE WG6 may develop guidance (check status). Interim: use industry-standard practices (SHA-256 hashing, atomic clock timestamps, digitally signed metadata). METER-015 verifies provenance presence.

background.value_of_value: Investigability and regulatory compliance. If MOR obligations are not met, CAA cannot defend authorization decisions post-incident. Business impact: incident without adequate data leads to broad authorization suspension pending investigation.
background.strategic_alignment: Mandatory regulatory obligation (CAA Occurrence Reporting Regulations). Supports AAIB investigative capacity (links to JSP 936 Part 1). Prerequisite for any operational trial.
background.keystone_reference: K9 (forensic data preservation for incident investigation)
background.reasoning: MORForensicCompleteness is measured (88% baseline) and relatively mature. Status vs Tolerable gap (12%) is achievable with redundant logging infrastructure. Risk is primarily data-volume management for swarms and standardization of forensic provenance.

qc_findings.finding_title: Status below tolerable; swarm data-volume management unaddressed
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (88% incident data completeness) is 12 points below Isaac's Tolerable (100%). Scale requires complete recorded evidence (all state variables >= 10Hz, data integrity no gaps > 100ms, forensic provenance verified, retention through MOR investigation). Status gap (12%) indicates ~12% of incidents have logging gaps or missing provenance certification. For swarms, data volume multiplies: 5-aircraft swarm logging ~100+ state variables at >= 10Hz generates gigabytes per hour. Storage, transmission, and forensic provenance management become challenging. Scale does not explicitly address swarm-specific logging schema (per-aircraft state, inter-vehicle link state, formation geometry).
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Define swarm logging schema: (1) Full-resolution for safety-critical variables (position XYZ, velocity, autonomy mode, engine status, crew inputs); (2) Aggregated/compressed for non-critical (sensor calibration, motor RPM, diagnostics). Ray's METER-015 (Data Recording Completeness & Integrity Audit) expands to swarm: pre-flight checks all logging systems (per-aircraft + formation-level metrics), post-flight validates data integrity. Redundant logging systems reduce gap probability. Standardize forensic provenance (SHA-256 hashing, atomic-clock timestamps, digitally signed metadata). EUROCAE WG6 may develop guidance (check 2026 timeline). Compression post-flight for archive; real-time transmission via high-bandwidth link (not feasible for swarm-scale data).
qc_findings.cross_references: Alexa Scale: Complete recorded evidence all variables >= 10Hz, no gaps > 100ms; Brook benchmarks: Status 88% vs Tolerable 100%; Isaac Constraint: 100% required (binary: either fully logged or investigation compromised); Ray METER-015: Data Recording Completeness & Integrity Audit

defospam_finding_refs: Implicit in REQ014: no DeFOSPAM finding, but scope requires quantification
scale.scale: Count of documented competencies per crew member, each with evidence of demonstrated proficiency. For each operational role [Role], a crew member is competent if: (1) holds required certification [Certification], (2) completed role-specific training with documented pass/fail result, (3) demonstrated emergency procedure execution in simulation with >= 90% success rate, (4) logged >= [MinFlightHours] flight hours in similar operational conditions.

scale_parameters.Role: crew position (pilot, sensor operator, safety officer, mission commander)
scale_parameters.Certification: required license/endorsement (CAA UAS operator, swarm-operations endorsement, type rating)
scale_parameters.MinFlightHours: minimum logged hours in similar conditions (e.g., >= 50 hours for pilot, >= 20 for sensor operator)

sub_scales: CertificationCoverage - count of crew members holding all required certifications
TrainingCompleteness - count of role-specific training courses completed with documented pass/fail
SimulationProficiency - percentage of emergency procedure simulations executed with >= 90% success
OperationalExperience - total flight hours logged in similar operational conditions
scale.unit: count of documented competencies per crew member
scale.reasoning: REQ014 uses evidenced competency without defining what evidence suffices. This Scale operationalizes competency via four converging criteria: certification, training, simulation, and experience. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_definition: For each crew member and assigned role (pilot, sensor operator, safety officer), a simulator-based assessment is conducted. Scenarios test: (1) Link-loss response (correct execution of RTL / contingency procedures), (2) Geofence-breach response (abort mission, land immediately), (3) DAA encounter response (acknowledge alert, position swarm for safe separation), (4) Swarm-member health-alert response (remove member from formation, continue mission safely). Each scenario is run twice; crew must achieve >= 90% success rate (correct procedure, appropriate latency, no secondary errors). Scoring: pass/fail per procedure. Target: 100% of required procedures scored PASS. Cost: 4-6 hours per crew member (scenario prep + briefing + runs + debrief). Run once per crew member before authorization, then annually thereafter.
meters.measurement_tool: High-fidelity flight simulator; procedure checklist (Go/No-Go scoring); video record (for replay/review); training records template
meters.measurement_owner: CAA training inspector + qualified check pilot; Workbench simulator provides scenario execution
meters.reasoning: CrewCompetencyEvidence Scale requires demonstrated proficiency in simulation (>= 90% success). This Meter is the emergency-procedure simulation test. Accuracy is high (pass/fail is objective). Precision is high (repeatable procedures). Cost is medium (simulator time). Credibility is high because simulation is a recognized training/validation method. Confidence: 8.

past.source: CAA licensing and training records; published UAS operator certification standards
past.estimation_note: FY 2024 UAS operator licensing required: CAA UAS operator certificate, aircraft type rating, and operational endorsement. Estimated at 3 competencies per pilot on average.

Ideal assumes 5 documented competencies per crew role.

Emergency-procedure simulation proficiency (>= 90% success rate) is rarely systematically measured. Recommend CAA mandate this as part of crew certification.

The 1.5-competency gap reflects the emerging nature of swarm crew training. Trend is improving as swarm-operations endorsement becomes standard, but adoption is slow due to training curriculum development lead time.

stakeholders.name: CAA Licensing Inspector (REQ014)
stakeholders.specific_need: Documented crew competency evidence: certifications held, training completed, simulation proficiency (>= 90% emergency procedure success), and operational experience (>= min flight hours in similar conditions) so that crew licensing and authorization is defensible

stakeholders.name: CAA Human Factors Inspector (REQ010)
stakeholders.specific_need: Crew competency in swarm-specific operations (coordination, emergent behavior monitoring, termination decision-making) demonstrated via simulation and training so that meaningful human control is credible

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Specific competency domains and evidence types defined (e.g., CAA UAS operator cert, swarm-ops endorsement, 50 hours BVLOS experience) so that training plan and cost are bounded
stakeholders.tolerable_override: Waive certain certifications if equivalent simulation proficiency is demonstrated (e.g., military pilots may substitute military jet experience for UAS hours)
stakeholders.target_override: Lower min flight hours (e.g., 25 hours instead of 50) if simulation-based training is comprehensive and proficiency is high

stakeholders.name: Flight Test Crew (Pilot, Sensor Operator, Safety Officer)
stakeholders.specific_need: Training curriculum and proficiency standards clear so that crew is confident in their ability to operate the swarm safely and recover from off-nominal states

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)

conflicts: Experience requirement: Licensing Inspector wants >= 50 hours BVLOS swarm experience; Applicant wants waiver if simulation proficiency is high (cost-driven, schedule-driven). Conflict: Licensing Inspector authority is absolute on waiver requests; applicant must present strong simulator validation evidence (equivalent fidelity to real flight) to negotiate. Typically, simulators provide partial credit (e.g., 25 simulator hours = 5 real flight hours).

Certification scope: Licensing Inspector wants swarm-ops-specific endorsement (formal CAA approval); Applicant may have military or commercial UAS cert but not swarm-specific. Conflict: CAA Licensing Inspector may grant conditional authorization pending swarm-ops endorsement; applicant pursues endorsement in parallel with trial.

Competency domains: Human Factors Inspector wants broad competency assessment (airmanship, decision-making, swarm monitoring, emergency procedures); Applicant wants narrow focus (system operation, basic manual recovery). Conflict: scale defines role-specific competencies (pilot: airmanship + swarm ops, sensor operator: swarm monitoring + data interpretation); applicant must address all domains in training plan.
stakeholders.reasoning: Licensing Inspector has regulatory authority over crew licensing and authorization and must defend crew competency to CAA and public. Human Factors Inspector shares authority on swarm-specific competency. Applicant must invest in crew training and proficiency documentation. CrewCompetencyEvidence is generally low-conflict (both regulator and applicant want qualified crew) but has scheduling implications (training time is on critical path to trial start).

constraints.conditions: Each crew member: (1) CAA UAS operator cert, (2) aircraft type rating, (3) swarm-operations endorsement, (4) role-specific training (pass/fail), (5) emergency-procedure sim >= 90% success. Plus minimum hours (50 pilot, 20 sensor op).

targets.reasoning: Goal = 5 competencies by end 2027 is the team-committed crew competency standard. Requires swarm-operations endorsement curriculum maturity, standardized training, and simulator validation. Achievable within 24 months.

background.owner: Applicant (crew training organization) + CAA licensing inspector
background.authority: CAA licensing and training authority (Part-FCL alignment)
background.motivation: Crew must demonstrate competency across multiple dimensions: certification, training, simulation proficiency, and operational experience. Without documented evidence, pilot-in-charge integrity is unverified and crew cannot legally operate.

risks.risk: Status (3.5 competencies) below Tolerable (5); swarm-operations endorsement is nascent and not yet widely deployed
risks.mitigation: CAA/JARUS publishes swarm-operations endorsement training curriculum (timeline: 2026). Pilot training providers offer endorsement courses. Grandfather early pilots under alternative competency pathways (e.g., AMLAS trial participation = swarm-ops endorsement equivalent).

risks.risk: Emergency-procedure simulation proficiency (>= 90% success rate) is rarely systematically measured. What counts as passing a simulation?
risks.mitigation: Publish standard emergency procedures: link loss recovery, geofence breach response, DAA encounter handling, swarm member incapacitation. Ray's METER-016 validates >= 90% success in simulation before authorization.

risks.risk: Operational experience requirement (>= 50 hours for pilot, >= 20 for sensor operator) may be impossible to meet for novel operation types (swarm formation control, multi-aircraft DAA)
risks.mitigation: Allow simulator hours to count toward experience requirement (e.g., 10 hours simulator = 10 hours operational experience). AMLAS trials can serve as operational experience pathway for early operators.

background.value_of_value: Baseline crew qualification. If crew lacks demonstrated competency, incident risk increases dramatically. Regulatory impact: failure on crew competency is grounds for authorization refusal or crew restriction (specific pilot qualified, not generic operator license).
background.strategic_alignment: Supports CAA licensing authority and crew training standardization. Enables swarm-operations endorsement adoption. Prerequisites for BVLOS and urban-logistics crew training.
background.reasoning: CrewCompetencyEvidence is a measured value (3.5 competencies) with clear improvement pathway (swarm-operations endorsement adoption). Status vs Tolerable gap (1.5) is achievable with training curriculum development. Risk is primarily in training standardization and endorsement definition.

qc_findings.finding_title: Swarm-operations endorsement nascent; target 1.5 competencies below tolerable
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (3.5 documented competencies per crew member) is 1.5 competencies below Isaac's Tolerable (5). Current crew licensing requires: CAA UAS operator cert, aircraft type rating, operational endorsement. Swarm-operations endorsement is nascent and not yet widely deployed (emerging in 2026). Scale requires 5: certification (UAS operator + swarm endorsement + type rating), role-specific training (pass/fail documented), emergency-procedure simulation (>= 90% success), minimum flight hours (>= 50 pilot, >= 20 sensor op). Gap reflects training curriculum lag.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: CAA/JARUS publish swarm-operations endorsement training curriculum (timeline 2026). Pilot training providers offer endorsement courses. Interim: grandfather early pilots under alternative pathways (AMLAS trial participation = swarm-ops endorsement equivalent). Standard emergency procedures required: link-loss recovery, geofence-breach response, DAA encounter handling, swarm-member incapacitation. Ray's METER-016 validates >= 90% simulation proficiency before authorization. Allow simulator hours toward experience requirement (10 hours simulator = 10 hours operational). Gap is achievable through training standardization.

scale.ambition_level: signed apportionment so that regulatory ownership is gap-free
defospam_finding_refs: Implicit in REQ015: no DeFOSPAM finding, but term gap-free requires quantification
scale.scale: Percentage of safety-critical design and operational aspects covered by a signed CAA/MAA apportionment matrix, such that responsibility for each aspect is assigned to exactly one regulatory body, with no gaps (unassigned aspects) and no overlaps. Coverage target: >= 100% of hazards identified in SORA, >= 100% of design domains, >= 100% of operational domains.

scale_parameters.HazardCategory: hazard type from SORA (loss-of-separation, loss-of-control, loss-of-link, environmental)
scale_parameters.SignatoryStatus: signature status of apportionment (both CAA and MAA signed, or pending)

sub_scales: HazardCoverage - count of SORA hazards assigned to CAA or MAA in apportionment matrix
DesignDomainCoverage - count of design domains with assigned responsibility
OperationalDomainCoverage - count of operational domains with assigned responsibility
OverlapDetection - count of aspects assigned to both CAA and MAA (target: 0 overlaps)
scale.reasoning: Gap-free means every safety-critical aspect has a single point of responsibility. This Scale operationalizes gap-free apportionment by measuring coverage and conflict detection. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_definition: A responsibility matrix is constructed (rows = safety-critical design/operational domains from SORA hazard list + design domains + operational domains; columns = CAA responsibility, MAA responsibility, shared, unassigned). For each row, the applicant assigns responsibility to exactly one party (CAA, MAA, or explicitly shared with defined sub-responsibilities). Auditor verifies: (1) HazardCoverage: all SORA-identified hazards are assigned (count unassigned = target: 0). (2) DesignDomainCoverage: avionics design, swarm decision logic, autonomy limits, geofence system, termination system (count unassigned = target: 0). (3) OperationalDomainCoverage: airspace segregation, crew training, incident response, post-trial close-out (count unassigned = target: 0). (4) OverlapDetection: no domain is assigned to both CAA and MAA (count overlaps = target: 0). Signed by authorized CAA and MAA representatives. Cost: 3-4 hours for matrix construction + audit. Run before case opening.
meters.measurement_tool: Responsibility matrix template (spreadsheet or structured form); traceability mapping (each domain -> assigned party); audit checklist (count gaps + overlaps)
meters.measurement_owner: CAA/MAA liaison (human) + case officer (verifies signatures)
meters.reasoning: CAAMAAApportionmentGapFreeness Scale requires 100% coverage with no gaps/overlaps. This Meter is the responsibility matrix audit. Accuracy is high (coverage is countable). Precision is high. Cost is low (desk work). Speed is high. Credibility is high because it's verifiable and signed by both parties. Confidence: 8.

past.source: Published CAA/MAA trial apportionment matrices (Project XCelerate, AMLAS pilot); CAA governance records
past.estimation_note: FY 2024 apportionment matrices for major trials covered ~75% of hazards and domains. Gaps typically in emerging areas (swarm coordination, emergent behavior, ML explainability). Estimated at 75%.

data_gaps: CAA does not publish apportionment gap metrics. Request data from CAA/MAA coordination team or analyze published apportionment matrices from recent trials.
Overlap detection (aspects assigned to both CAA and MAA) is rarely systematically analyzed. Recommend CAA/MAA conduct joint apportionment audit for recent trials.

Emerging domains (swarm coordination, emergent behavior, ML explainability) are not yet clearly apportioned in most matrices. Recommend CAA/MAA jointly publish apportionment guidance for novel domains.
benchmarks.reasoning: CAAMAAApportionmentGapFreeness measures regulatory clarity. Current Status (80%) is significantly below Ideal (100%), with a 20% gap indicating ambiguous responsibility assignments. This is particularly acute for novel domains (swarm-specific risks, emergent behaviors, ML assurance) where neither CAA nor MAA has published guidance. The gap creates regulatory risk for operators: unclear who is responsible for swarm-specific SORA assessment, who approves emergent-behavior evidence, who certifies ML explainability.

stakeholders.name: CAA/MAA Liaison (REQ015)
stakeholders.specific_need: Signed apportionment matrix covers 100% of SORA hazards, 100% of design domains, and 100% of operational domains with no gaps (unassigned) and no overlaps (dual-assigned) so that regulatory responsibility is unambiguous and each regulator is accountable

stakeholders.name: CAA Case Officer (REQ001)
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear CAA regulatory scope defined in apportionment so that CAA case file is coherent and CAA responsibilities are not challenged by MAA interpretations

stakeholders.name: MAA Lead Inspector (RPAS Regulatory Branch)
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear MAA regulatory scope defined in apportionment so that MAA responsibilities are unambiguous and not in conflict with CAA expectations

stakeholders.name: Delivery Duty Holder (DDH) / Operating Duty Holder (ODH)
stakeholders.specific_need: Apportionment split reflects safety accountability: CAA responsible for public/airspace safety, MAA responsible for range/MOD safety; DDH accountable for design and operational compliance to split
stakeholders.target_override: Clear split that aligns with DDH liability and insurance scope

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Apportionment clear so that applicant knows which regulator (CAA vs MAA) to satisfy for each requirement. Avoids conflicting demands from dual regulators.

conflicts: CRITICAL CONFLICT: CAA and MAA may disagree on responsibility split: CAA may claim public-safety authority over all aspects; MAA may claim range-safety authority as primary. Example: geofence enforcement—is it CAA airspace protection (CAA) or MAA range containment (MAA)? Conflict: Alexa's scale requires both regulators to sign apportionment matrix; disagreements must be resolved before trial authorization. Liaison role (CAA/MAA Liaison) is responsible for negotiation.

Gap vs overlap: CAA/MAA may leave aspects unassigned to avoid responsibility (gap); or both may claim responsibility (overlap). Conflict: scale explicitly requires 100% coverage and zero overlaps; applicant must escalate unresolved gaps to CAA/MAA joint panel chair.

Design vs operational split: CAA may claim design authority (AMLAS, safety case); MAA may claim operational authority (range clearance, daily go/no-go). Conflict: scope of 'design domain' vs 'operational domain' must be negotiated in apportionment; typical split is CAA = aircraft design/certification, MAA = range operations/risk management.
stakeholders.reasoning: CAA/MAA Liaison has explicit responsibility for apportionment negotiation and must broker agreement between CAA Case Officer and MAA Lead Inspector. Without signed apportionment, trial authorization is blocked. DDH (operator) depends on clear split to structure project. Applicant has low power but high motivation (wants clarity). CAAMAAApportionmentGapFreeness is a top conflict hotspot (regulatory jurisdiction disputes); resolution requires governance escalation if Liaison cannot broker agreement.

constraints.unit: percentage coverage with no gaps or overlaps
constraints.conditions: Signed CAA/MAA apportionment covers 100% SORA hazards, 100% design domains, 100% operational domains. Each aspect assigned to exactly one body. No gaps, no overlaps.
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722 CAA/MAA coordination; MOD/CAA joint governance
constraints.reasoning: Binary: responsibility either clear or ambiguous. Status 80% = 20% ambiguity. Novel domains (swarm coordination, emergent behavior, ML assurance) lack clear ownership.

targets.reasoning: Wish = 85% reflects achievable improvement from Status 80% through clearer CAA/MAA role definitions for novel domains (swarm coordination, emergent behavior, ML assurance).

targets.conditions: 100% of SORA hazards, design domains, and operational domains assigned to exactly one regulatory body (CAA or MAA) with signed apportionment matrix, zero gaps, zero overlaps
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA/MAA Liaison (REQ015) — committed gap-free apportionment; Applicant — regulatory predictability
targets.reasoning: Goal = 100% by end 2027 is the team-committed apportionment standard. Requires joint CAA/MAA governance process to address novel domains. Stretch feasibility due to multi-stakeholder coordination complexity.
