defospam_finding_refs: Observation-6: REQ008 Emergent swarm behavior deviation - no specification of what happens when behavior deviates from assurance case
scales.scale: Count of distinct emergent behaviors documented in the swarm decision-making architecture case, each with a defined boundary and a detection/recovery mechanism if boundary is exceeded. A swarm is bounded if: (1) >= 5 emergent behaviors are identified and bounded, (2) each boundary is quantified, (3) test evidence demonstrates the swarm stays within bounds in >= 95% of flight test hours.

scale_parameters.EmergentBehavior: collective swarm behavior not reducible to single-aircraft logic (formation geometry, coordinated maneuver, flocking, collective decision-making, swarm split/rejoin)
scale_parameters.BoundaryDefinition: quantified limits on behavior (e.g., formation tolerance +/- 10m, coordination latency <= 2 seconds)
scale_parameters.TestEvidence: flight test hours accumulating evidence of in-bounds behavior

sub_scales: EmergentBehaviorInventory - count of distinct emergent behaviors identified and documented
BoundaryQuantification - percentage of identified emergent behaviors with quantified state limits and detection criteria
RecoveryCapability - count of out-of-bounds behaviors for which a recovery/abort action is pre-defined
TestCoverage - percentage of test flight hours where swarm operates within all defined bounds
scales.reasoning: DeFOSPAM finding flagged that emergent behavior deviation outcomes are undefined. This Scale operationalizes emergent-behaviour evidence by requiring explicit inventory of emergent behaviors, quantified boundaries for each, and test evidence. Confidence: 7.

scales.ambition_level: ML components are assurable and forensically explainable
defospam_finding_refs: Dorothy-4: Forensically explainable undefined; unrealistic standard may result (CRITICAL)
Observation-7: REQ009 ML explainability failure outcome undefined (decision override, logging, abort?)
scales.scale: Percentage of safety-critical ML model decisions where the explainability evidence allows independent verification of the decision logic. Evidence sufficiency determined by model type: decision trees = 100% requirement; SVMs = feature importance + decision boundary; neural networks = saliency maps + LIME/SHAP with >= 80% decision-path coverage.

scale_parameters.TestSet: hold-out test dataset used to evaluate explainability (e.g., 500 representative decision cases)
scale_parameters.SafetyCriticality: usage context (safety-critical = affects loss-of-separation, termination; non-safety-critical = affects performance optimization)

sub_scales: ModelTransparencyLevel - by-model-type explainability requirement
ReviewerReconstruction - percentage of test decisions where independent reviewers can reconstruct the decision
FrozenModelDocumentation - presence of frozen-model manifest (training data provenance, hyperparameters, validation performance)
DecisionLogging - presence of runtime logging capturing ML decision inputs, internal states, and explainability evidence
scales.reasoning: DeFOSPAM findings flagged that forensically explainable is unrealistic for neural networks but undefined for other model types. This Scale operationalizes forensically explainable pragmatically with model-type-specific approaches. Confidence: 8.

defospam_finding_refs: Dorothy-2: Meaningful human control lacks measurable definition (CRITICAL)
Observation-8: REQ010 Crew take-over fails - outcome when crew cannot regain manual control is undefined
scales.scale: Composite score across four dimensions of human control, each scored 0-100: (1) SituationalAwareness: crew update frequency on swarm state (target >= 2Hz for nominal, >= 10Hz for off-nominal). (2) AuthorityPreservation: percentage of decisions requiring explicit crew authorization (target: 100% for irreversible decisions, >= 80% for reversible). (3) TakeoverLatency: seconds available for crew to regain control before unsafe state (target: >= 10 seconds). (4) WorkloadBounds: maximum concurrent events and duty cycle per hour (target: <= 3 concurrent alarms, <= 6 minutes active control per hour).

scale_parameters.OperationalPhase: mission phase (pre-flight, takeoff, transit, encounter, termination, recovery)
scale_parameters.DecisionType: decision category (irreversible such as flight termination, reversible such as re-routing, autonomous such as obstacle avoidance)

sub_scales: SituationalAwarenessScore - [0-100] based on state update frequency and display comprehensiveness
AuthorityPreservationScore - [0-100] percentage of decisions requiring crew sign-off
TakeoverLatencyScore - [0-100] based on time available to regain control
WorkloadScore - [0-100] based on concurrent event load and duty cycle
scales.reasoning: DeFOSPAM findings flagged that meaningful human control is inherently subjective and lacks measurable criteria. This Scale operationalizes meaningful by defining four measurable dimensions (awareness, authority, speed, workload) commonly used in human factors standards. Confidence: 8.

scales.ambition_level: independent termination is effective in any off-nominal state
defospam_finding_refs: Observation-9: REQ011 Flight termination post-outcome sub-actions undefined (crew alert, ATSU notify, data preserve, recovery)
scales.scale: Percentage of simulated off-nominal scenarios where independent flight termination is successfully triggered and executed within [TerminationTime], measured across [NumScenarios] failure and environmental scenarios. Success = swarm reaches safe configuration (motors off, drift ceased) without secondary hazards.

scale_parameters.OffNominalScenario: failure condition (loss of autonomy, loss of C2, loss of geofence, engine failure, collision threat, environmental extreme)
scale_parameters.TerminationTime: time from hazard detection to safe configuration (e.g., <= 10 seconds)

sub_scales: TerminationTriggerReliability - percentage of scenarios where termination is correctly triggered
TerminationExecutionCompleteness - percentage of triggers that result in complete motor cutoff and safe configuration
PostTerminationActions - count of defined sub-actions after termination (crew alert, ATSU notification, data preservation, recovery)
EnvironmentalRobustness - termination success rate across environmental extremes within ODD bounds
scales.reasoning: DeFOSPAM finding flagged that flight termination outcomes are undefined beyond motor cutoff. This Scale operationalizes independent termination by requiring reliable trigger, complete execution, post-termination actions, and environmental robustness. Confidence: 8.

scales.ambition_level: complete recorded evidence accepted MOR obligations
defospam_finding_refs: Dorothy-8: Complete used in two contexts with different implied meanings (REQ012 context: data logging)
Observation-10: REQ012 MOR trigger and action outcomes undefined (mandatory vs voluntary, timing, recipient)
scales.scale: Percentage of incident events where a complete recorded evidence set is captured: all aircraft state variables sampled at >= 10Hz and logged for >= [RetentionTime] post-incident, with data integrity verified (no gaps > 100ms, checksums valid) and forensic provenance documented.

scale_parameters.StateVariables: required logging schema (position XYZ, velocity, acceleration, heading, engine status, autonomy mode, sensor inputs, crew inputs, system alerts)
scale_parameters.SamplingRate: minimum Hz (e.g., >= 10Hz for nominal state, >= 50Hz for high-frequency dynamics)
scale_parameters.RetentionTime: duration of post-incident data retention (e.g., until MOR investigation closes or 1 year, whichever is longer)
scale_parameters.MORTrigger: events that mandate MOR reporting (accident, serious incident, incident) per CAA definitions

sub_scales: DataSchemaCoverage - count of required state variables captured
DataIntegrity - percentage of logged time intervals with valid data (no gaps > 100ms)
ForensicProvenance - presence of cryptographic hash, timestamp, source certification for each data file
RetentionCompliance - percentage of incidents where evidence is retained for full MOR investigation duration
scales.reasoning: DeFOSPAM finding distinguished complete in the MOR context (data logging) from REQ001 (application completeness). This Scale operationalizes complete recorded evidence by specifying data schema, sampling rate, integrity, and provenance. Confidence: 9.

defospam_finding_refs: Implicit in REQ014: no DeFOSPAM finding, but scope requires quantification
scales.scale: Count of documented competencies per crew member, each with evidence of demonstrated proficiency. For each operational role [Role], a crew member is competent if: (1) holds required certification [Certification], (2) completed role-specific training with documented pass/fail result, (3) demonstrated emergency procedure execution in simulation with >= 90% success rate, (4) logged >= [MinFlightHours] flight hours in similar operational conditions.

scale_parameters.Role: crew position (pilot, sensor operator, safety officer, mission commander)
scale_parameters.Certification: required license/endorsement (CAA UAS operator, swarm-operations endorsement, type rating)
scale_parameters.MinFlightHours: minimum logged hours in similar conditions (e.g., >= 50 hours for pilot, >= 20 for sensor operator)

sub_scales: CertificationCoverage - count of crew members holding all required certifications
TrainingCompleteness - count of role-specific training courses completed with documented pass/fail
SimulationProficiency - percentage of emergency procedure simulations executed with >= 90% success
OperationalExperience - total flight hours logged in similar operational conditions
scales.unit: count of documented competencies per crew member
scales.reasoning: REQ014 uses evidenced competency without defining what evidence suffices. This Scale operationalizes competency via four converging criteria: certification, training, simulation, and experience. Confidence: 8.

scales.ambition_level: signed apportionment so that regulatory ownership is gap-free
defospam_finding_refs: Implicit in REQ015: no DeFOSPAM finding, but term gap-free requires quantification
scales.scale: Percentage of safety-critical design and operational aspects covered by a signed CAA/MAA apportionment matrix, such that responsibility for each aspect is assigned to exactly one regulatory body, with no gaps (unassigned aspects) and no overlaps. Coverage target: >= 100% of hazards identified in SORA, >= 100% of design domains, >= 100% of operational domains.

scale_parameters.HazardCategory: hazard type from SORA (loss-of-separation, loss-of-control, loss-of-link, environmental)
scale_parameters.SignatoryStatus: signature status of apportionment (both CAA and MAA signed, or pending)

sub_scales: HazardCoverage - count of SORA hazards assigned to CAA or MAA in apportionment matrix
DesignDomainCoverage - count of design domains with assigned responsibility
OperationalDomainCoverage - count of operational domains with assigned responsibility
OverlapDetection - count of aspects assigned to both CAA and MAA (target: 0 overlaps)
scales.reasoning: Gap-free means every safety-critical aspect has a single point of responsibility. This Scale operationalizes gap-free apportionment by measuring coverage and conflict detection. Confidence: 8.

scales.ambition_level: phased gates so that evidence builds progressively and is reversible
defospam_finding_refs: Observation-11: REQ016 Phased gate progression criteria undefined (trigger, data handling, re-entry procedure)
scales.scale: Count of well-defined phased trial gates, where each gate has: (1) Clear Criterion: quantified evidence requirement. (2) Reversibility: documented procedure to exit the current phase if new evidence suggests unacceptable risk. (3) Data Immutability: all evidence data preserved and audit-logged so gates can be re-evaluated. (4) Re-entry Procedure: re-entry criteria and evidence refresh requirements defined.

scale_parameters.PhaseLabel: trial phase (e.g., phase-1-ground-test, phase-2-vlos-single-aircraft, phase-3-vlos-swarm, phase-4-bvlos-swarm)
scale_parameters.ReversibilityScenario: conditions triggering phase reversal (unexpected failure mode, environmental change, crew fatigue)

sub_scales: GateCount - number of distinct phases with explicit gates (target: >= 4)
CriterionClarity - percentage of gates with quantified progression criteria
ReversibilityDocumentation - percentage of gates with documented exit criteria and phase-reversal procedures
DataImmutability - evidence that all phase data is preserved in write-once storage or audit-logged
scales.reasoning: DeFOSPAM finding flagged that phased gates lack explicit progression criteria and reversibility procedures. This Scale operationalizes phased gates by requiring clear evidence thresholds, documented exit/reversal procedures, and immutable data. Confidence: 8.

defospam_finding_refs: Flo-10: Four Critical Features Have No User Story (REQ017-020)
Observation-12: REQ017 Public stakeholder engagement outcomes absent (NO USER STORY)
scales.scale: Count of stakeholder groups engaged prior to trial authorization, with documented consultation evidence. Target: >= 5 stakeholder groups engaged (e.g., local residents, landowners, environmental, aviation community, local media). For each group, engagement completeness measured as: (1) Prior notification (timing >= [NotificationLeadTime] before flight). (2) Documented feedback collection (survey, workshop, or open forum). (3) Response to feedback (acceptance, mitigation, or documented rejection with rationale). (4) Residual concerns (unresolved issues logged and escalated if necessary).

scale_parameters.StakeholderGroup: community audience (residents within [Radius], landowners, environmental groups, aviation operators, media)
scale_parameters.NotificationLeadTime: advance notice before first flight (e.g., >= 30 days)

sub_scales: StakeholderIdentification - count of stakeholder groups identified and engaged (target: >= 5)
PriorNotification - percentage of stakeholders receiving advance notice >= [NotificationLeadTime]
FeedbackCollection - count of distinct feedback mechanisms used and percentage of stakeholders responding
FeedbackResponseCoverage - percentage of feedback items receiving documented response
scales.reasoning: DeFOSPAM findings flagged that REQ017 (public engagement) has no user story and CAP 1616 compliance criteria are undefined. This Scale operationalizes community consultation by requiring stakeholder identification, prior notification, structured feedback collection, and documented response. Confidence: 7.

meters.meter_definition: For each submitted pack, the Workbench component 3 (Artefact Detector) automatically parses the submission and maps each artefact (ConOps, SORA, Safety Case, Training Plan, Personnel List, Environmental Assessment, Insurance, Ground Survey, crew credentials, ML manifest if applicable) against a regulatory artefact library. For each artefact class, the system records: presence/absence, file format validation (PDF/XLSX/DOCX), and page count. A pack passes the Quick Gate if all 8 mandatory artefact types are present, validly formatted, and exceed minimum page thresholds (ConOps >= 5 pages, SORA >= 3, Safety Case >= 10). Run on every submission within 10 minutes of upload. Output: RAG matrix (Red = missing artefact, Amber = present but below threshold, Green = present and sufficient). Triggers manual review if any Amber or Red flags.
meters.measurement_tool: Workbench Artefact Detector component (automated file parser + clause mapper); RAG matrix dashboard
meters.measurement_owner: Workbench engine (automated); CAA case officer reviews exceptions
meters.reasoning: Per Planguage guidance, the Meter is the measurement process, not the Scale. This Meter operationalizes PackCompleteness by running automated presence/absence detection on each artefact class at intake. It's fast, cheap, and credible because it delegates to the Workbench's existing parsing capability. Accuracy is high for file presence; precision is high because the same detector always produces the same output. Confidence: 9.

meters.meter_definition: After Quick Gate passes, a detailed audit runs on each artefact: for each required section (identified from regulatory checklist per operation type [OpType]), human or semi-automated reviewer verifies that the section contains substantive content, not placeholder text. For ConOps, mandatory sections are: (1) Swarm composition and roles, (2) Operational phases with crew triggers, (3) Failure modes and contingency responses, (4) Worst-credible scenarios and crew procedures. For SORA: (1) Loss-of-separation hazard tree, (2) Swarm-specific interdependency evidence, (3) Risk mitigation controls. Reviewer marks each section: Present & Substantive (green), Present But Vague (amber), Absent (red). Section-level coverage target: >= 90% green. Records paragraph pointers (page, line range) for traceability. Run once per case, triggered after Quick Gate passes, before SORA assessment begins.
meters.measurement_tool: Clause-by-clause checklist (Workbench or manual audit spreadsheet); semi-automated text classifier (detect placeholder keywords: 'TBD', 'to be determined', 'TBC', single-sentence sections); human reviewer scoring sheet
meters.measurement_owner: CAA case officer + independent contract auditor (mixed human-in-the-loop); Workbench engine screens for obvious gaps
meters.reasoning: PackCompleteness has two sub-scales: Quick (automated file presence) and Thorough (substantive content). This Meter captures the thorough, costly path. Accuracy is medium because 'substantive' is subjective; precision is medium for the same reason. Cost is medium (2-4 hours for full audit). Credibility is high because independent human audit builds stakeholder confidence. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_definition: SORA assessor reviews the submitted SORA evidence document and extracts all risk items (hazards, failure modes, controls, residual risks). For each risk item, assessor tags whether it explicitly addresses swarm-specific failure modes: (1) Coordinated-link-loss cascades (e.g., one aircraft's C2 loss triggers loss-of-separation within swarm), (2) Emergent misbehavior (e.g., consensus deadlock, oscillation), (3) Unintended inter-vehicle interactions (e.g., RF interference, wake effects). Assessor records: Risk ID, SORA hazard category, swarm-specific tag (Yes/No), evidence confidence (High/Medium/Low). Target: >= 3 distinct swarm-aware risk items per aircraft, distributed across the three categories above. Count is target metric; each item must cite supporting evidence (test report, design review memo, simulation data). Run once per case after Quick Gate, before SAIL derivation.
meters.measurement_tool: SORA risk-item extraction checklist; swarm-specific tag taxonomy; evidence traceability log (hyperlinks to supporting docs)
meters.measurement_owner: CAA SORA assessor (human domain expert)
meters.reasoning: The Scale (SwarmAwareSORAEvidence) requires count of swarm-aware risk items. The Meter is the process of extracting, tagging, and counting. Accuracy is medium because swarm-aware vs. single-aircraft interpretation requires domain judgment. Precision is medium for the same reason. Cost is medium (3-5 hours for full SORA review). Credibility is high because SORA assessor signature carries regulatory weight. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_definition: Two independent safety-case reviewers (neither has seen ConOps before) are given: (1) ConOps document, (2) ConOps Clarity Scoring Sheet (structured questionnaire: 'For takeoff phase, describe nominal behavior boundary in 2-3 sentences' + 'For takeoff phase, what is worst-credible behavior?' + 10 similar phase-by-phase questions). Each reviewer independently scores clarity 0-100 per phase. Answers are recorded verbatim. After both review, answers are compared. Cohen's kappa is calculated on structured questions (ordinal scale: Absent=0, Vague=1, Clear=2). ConOps passes Clarity Meter if: (1) kappa >= 0.80 across all phases (high inter-rater agreement), (2) Both reviewers score >= 80/100 overall clarity. Cost to run: 4-6 hours (2-3 hours per reviewer). Run once per case before Geofence/DAA assurance begins.
meters.measurement_tool: Structured clarity scoring sheet (questionnaire + ordinal scale); kappa calculator; independent reviewer pairing process
meters.measurement_owner: CAA safety-case reviewers (2x independent domain experts); coordinating case officer tabulates results
meters.reasoning: ConOpsClarity's sub-scale is ReviewerAgreement (inter-rater reliability, kappa >= 0.8). This Meter is the explicit dual-review consensus test. Accuracy and precision are high because kappa is a mathematically well-defined inter-rater metric. Cost is medium (multi-reviewer time). Credibility is high because it's objective and reproducible. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_definition: Before first flight, Range Safety Officer (RSO) or Workbench simulator runs 10,000 Monte Carlo trials of geofence enforcement. Each trial: (1) Initializes swarm in random position within authorized area, (2) Simulates random failure modes from list: C2 loss, GPS loss, altimeter failure, geofence logic fault, single-aircraft power loss. (3) Runs geofence logic at 10Hz, (4) Checks if aircraft exceeds boundary by >= 50m (pre-configured tolerance). Success = aircraft recovers to within boundary or triggers RTL before 50m breach. Target: >= 99.9% of trials show zero boundary exceedance. Outputs: pass/fail heatmap (boundary hot spots), distribution of boundary-approach distances, time-to-containment histograms. If pass, geofence is cleared for flight. If fail, airborne limits are tightened and sim re-runs. Cost: 2-4 hours for full 10k trial suite on modern hardware.
meters.measurement_tool: Workbench simulator (Monte Carlo engine) or independent flight-dynamics simulator (e.g., jMAVSim, Gazebo); trial data logger (CSV output)
meters.measurement_frequency: per-submission + event-triggered (re-run after any geofence code change)
meters.measurement_owner: Workbench engine (automated) + Range Safety Officer (human sign-off on sim assumptions)
meters.reasoning: GeofenceContainment Scale specifies 99.9% success rate across Monte Carlo trials. This Meter IS the simulation gauntlet. Accuracy and precision are high because simulator is deterministic and repeatable. Cost is medium (compute time). Credibility is high because it's transparent and auditable. This is load-bearing: evidence of geofence containment before first flight is non-negotiable for safety. Confidence: 9.

meters.meter_definition: After Monte Carlo passes, a controlled flight test is conducted with Range Safety Officer present and termination system armed. Setup: (1) Swarm launches within authorized area. (2) RSO deliberately commands aircraft to approach boundary via GPS waypoint or C2 manual input. (3) Geofence logic is monitored in real-time. (4) Test measures: time from boundary approach alert to RTL trigger, distance at RTL initiation, post-RTL containment time. Test data: 5 deliberate approach attempts per flight, 3-5 flights. Success = all approaches trigger RTL before boundary exceedance; containment time measured in seconds. This is a fly-away stress test; it validates that the geofence actually works under real conditions (GPS noise, latency, wind, etc.) not just in simulator. Cost: 1-2 days of range time, RSO supervision, safety equipment. Run once per operation type before first autonomous flight.
meters.measurement_tool: Live airborne GPS receiver + geofence monitor log; RSO observation + video record; post-flight telemetry analysis
meters.measurement_frequency: per-review + event-triggered (re-run after major swarm or geofence redesign)
meters.measurement_owner: Range Safety Officer (human); Workbench logs data; independent post-flight analysis
meters.reasoning: This Meter is the live fly-away test specified in guidance. It's the expensive, thorough validation that geofence containment is real. Accuracy and precision are high because you're measuring actual aircraft behavior. Cost is high (range time, personnel). Speed is low (days). Credibility is VERY high because it demonstrates working hardware + software integration. Justified cost: geofence is load-bearing safety-critical for BVLOS swarm ops. Confidence: 9.

meters.meter_definition: Controlled simulation test: swarm is flying autonomous waypoint mission. Test operator cuts C2 link (RF kill switch or simulator command). Telemetry system logs: (1) Link loss detection time (latency from actual link break to system recognizing loss), (2) Swarm response: do swarm members continue waypoint navigation? Do inter-vehicle links maintain cohesion? (3) Survival duration: how long before swarm enters unsafe state (loss-of-separation, uncontrolled descent, etc.). Test runs 10 scenarios with link-loss duration ranging from 100ms to 30+ seconds. Targets: (a) Autonomous survival >= 30 seconds for loss-of-C2 only, (b) Crew takeover latency <= 5 seconds (time for crew to see alert and manually intervene), (c) Inter-vehicle link-loss recovery within 10 seconds. Data logged: link loss duration, survival time, separation maintained, crew reaction time. Run in simulation first (10 trials, 1 hour), then in live flight test (3-5 sorties, 1-2 days).
meters.measurement_tool: Workbench simulator (C2 link-loss injection) + live flight test with RF kill switch; telemetry logger; separation monitoring (ADS-B or inter-vehicle ranging)
meters.measurement_frequency: per-submission + per-review + event-triggered (re-test after link architecture change)
meters.measurement_owner: Workbench engine (sim); Range Safety Officer + flight test crew (live); data analyst
meters.reasoning: C2LinkResilience Scale specifies time thresholds (30s autonomous, 120s with crew). This Meter is the hold-safe survival test. Accuracy is high because timings are measured objectively. Precision is high because scenarios are repeatable. Cost is medium (sim + flight time). Credibility is high because it's grounded in both simulation and live flight. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_definition: DAA system is tested against a suite of >= 100 encounter scenarios drawn from DO-365 (Detect-and-Avoid standards). Scenarios span: (1) Cooperative intruders (ADS-B equipped, altitude/velocity known), (2) Non-cooperative intruders (radar-only, no velocity info), (3) Head-on, crossing, overtake geometries. For each scenario: (1) Intruder is injected into swarm flight path, (2) DAA system detects intruder and initiates resolution, (3) System logs: detection latency, resolution maneuver (altitude change, lateral displacement, speed change), outcome (separation achieved, secondary conflict, miss). Target metrics: (a) >= 95% of encounters result in recovery to >= 500ft horizontal, >= 100ft vertical separation, (b) Recovery achieved within [RecoveryTime], typically 60 seconds, (c) No secondary conflicts (re-engagement with same or new intruder). Decision Consistency sub-scale: same encounter scenario repeated 3 times; DAA decision must be identical >= 95% of trials (determinism check). Run in simulation first (100 scenarios, 2 hours compute time), then subset in live flight test (10-15 encounters per sortie, 3-5 flights).
meters.measurement_tool: DO-365 test scenario database (100 pre-built encounter geometries); Workbench simulator with intruder injection + DAA monitor; live flight test with chase aircraft or simulated intruder track injection
meters.measurement_frequency: per-submission + per-review + event-triggered (re-test after DAA logic change)
meters.measurement_owner: Workbench engine (sim) + qualified DAA tester (live); independent analysis
meters.reasoning: DAAPerformance Scale specifies encounter resolution success rate, recovery time, decision consistency. This Meter is the DO-365 test suite. Accuracy is high (separation measured objectively). Precision is high (deterministic scenarios). Cost is medium (compute + flight time). Credibility is VERY high because DO-365 is an external, recognized standard. Confidence: 9.

meters.meter_definition: Autonomy designer submits a structured manifest of emergent behaviors identified in the swarm architecture. For each behavior (e.g., formation tightening, consensus deadlock, flocking), the manifest documents: (1) Description of the behavior (what happens, when does it occur), (2) Quantified boundary (state space limits, e.g., 'formation tolerance +/- 10m', 'consensus latency <= 2 seconds'), (3) Detection mechanism ('if inter-aircraft separation drops below 5m, trigger alert'), (4) Recovery action ('expand formation limits by 20%, reset consensus algorithm'). Auditor reviews manifest and verifies: (1) >= 5 distinct emergent behaviors identified, (2) Each behavior has a quantified boundary (not vague), (3) Each behavior has a defined detection + recovery mechanism. Targets: >= 5 behaviors, 100% with boundaries, 100% with detection/recovery. Cost: 2-3 hours for full audit. Run once per case before autonomous flight begins.
meters.measurement_tool: Emergent Behavior Manifest template (structured form); auditor checklist (presence/absence of boundaries, detection, recovery)
meters.measurement_owner: CAA autonomy reviewer (human domain expert)
meters.reasoning: SwarmEmergentBoundedness Scale requires >=5 emergent behaviors with quantified boundaries. This Meter is the documentation audit. Accuracy is medium because 'emergent' is subjective; precision is medium for the same reason. Cost is low (desk audit). Speed is high. Credibility is medium because it's documentation-based; actual test evidence follows in METER-010. Confidence: 7.

meters.meter_name: Emergent Behavior Flight Test Coverage Validator
meters.meter_definition: During flight testing, telemetry logs capture swarm state at 10Hz or higher. After each flight, data is analyzed: for each emergent behavior from the manifest (METER-009), auditor verifies: (1) Did the behavior occur during flight? If yes, did it stay within documented boundary? (2) If boundary was exceeded, was detection mechanism triggered? (3) Was recovery action effective? Cumulative metric: percentage of flight-hours where swarm operated within all documented emergent-behavior boundaries. Target: >= 95% of test flight hours show in-bounds behavior. Threshold: if >= 1 out-of-bounds event occurs in 5 flight-hours, the behavior boundary is re-tightened and additional test hours are required. Data logged: timestamp, behavior trigger event, state (in-bounds vs. out-of-bounds), recovery outcome. Cost: 1 hour per flight for data analysis + 0.5 day for cumulative trend analysis. Run continuously throughout phased trial program (phases 2-4).
meters.measurement_tool: Flight telemetry parser (extract swarm state); emergent behavior boundary checker (automated comparison); trend analysis dashboard
meters.measurement_owner: Workbench engine (automated parsing) + CAA autonomy reviewer (human interpretation); Range Safety Officer monitors real-time alerts
meters.reasoning: SwarmEmergentBoundedness Scale requires test evidence (>= 95% flight hours in-bounds). This Meter is the post-flight analysis. Accuracy is high because boundaries are objective limits. Precision is high (same analyzer always produces same result on same data). Cost is low (automated parsing + human review). Speed is high. Credibility is high because it's grounded in real flight data. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_definition: For each safety-critical ML model (e.g., autonomy-mode classifier, emergency-decision network), a post-hoc forensic test is conducted: (1) Select N=50 representative decisions from logged flight data. (2) For each decision, freeze the model state at that moment (inputs, weights, internal activations if available). (3) Provide explainability evidence (model type dependent): decision-tree = decision path (unambiguous), SVM = feature importance + decision boundary visualization, neural network = saliency map + LIME/SHAP explanation. (4) Ask two independent domain experts (not the model developers): 'Given the explainability evidence, do you agree this decision makes sense?' Score: agrees (1), partially agrees (0.5), disagrees (0). (5) Target: >= 80% of decisions scored as agrees/partially-agrees by both reviewers. Cost: 2-3 hours per model. Run before first autonomous flight; repeat after model retraining.
meters.measurement_tool: Model decision log (timestamp + inputs + decision), frozen model state snapshot, explainability framework (LIME/SHAP for neural networks, feature importance for SVM, decision path for trees), independent reviewer scoring sheet
meters.measurement_owner: ML assurance specialist (human) + domain expert reviewers; Workbench captures model state logs
meters.reasoning: MLForensicExplainability Scale requires reviewer reconstruction of decisions. This Meter IS the forensic test. Accuracy is medium because expert judgment on 'makes sense' is subjective. Precision is medium for the same reason. Cost is medium (expert time). Credibility is high because independent reviewers + standardized evidence framework. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_name: Crew State-Update Frequency & Display Completeness Audit
meters.meter_definition: Human factors inspector reviews ConOps and UI mockups/actual system. Verifies crew display updates swarm state at target frequency: nominal operations >= 2Hz (swarm position, health, altitude), off-nominal scenarios >= 10Hz (alerts, DAA events, link status). Inspector also audits display completeness: does crew see (1) individual aircraft state (position, velocity, altitude, health), (2) swarm-level metrics (formation geometry, inter-aircraft separation, consensus state), (3) external threats (detected intruders, geofence proximity, weather radar), (4) crew command authority (what decisions does crew control vs. autonomy?). Scoring rubric: 0-100 scale where 100 = full state visibility at required frequency, 0 = no display. Target: >= 80/100. Cost: 2 hours for audit. Run once before crew training begins.
meters.measurement_tool: Display audit checklist (component list); frequency measurement (Hz counter in live system or simulator); human factors scoring rubric
meters.measurement_owner: CAA human factors inspector (human domain expert)
meters.reasoning: MeaningfulHumanControl sub-scale is SituationalAwarenessScore (0-100). This Meter is the display audit. Accuracy and precision are medium because display adequacy is subjective. Cost is low. Speed is high. Credibility is medium; the real test is whether crew performs well in simulation/flight (METER-013). Confidence: 7.

meters.meter_name: Crew Workload & Takeover Latency Simulation Test
meters.meter_definition: Crew is placed in high-fidelity simulator with full swarm flight scenario. Scenario unfolds: (1) Nominal transit (baseline workload measurement), (2) Unexpected event injection (e.g., DAA encounter, C2 glitch, swarm member health alert). During simulation: (1) Crew's workload is measured via NASA-TLX questionnaire at key points (target: workload <= 70% during nominal, <= 85% during events). (2) Takeover latency is measured: from time an alert is displayed to time crew issues manual command (target: <= 5 seconds). (3) Concurrent event load is counted: how many simultaneous alarms/alerts? (target: <= 3 concurrent). (4) Duty cycle is logged: total time crew is actively controlling aircraft (target: <= 6 minutes per hour). Test runs 5 scenarios, each 30-60 minutes. Composite score (0-100): weight as (50% workload) + (25% takeover latency) + (25% event load). Target: >= 80/100. Cost: 1-2 days for test design + crew prep + run + analysis. Run once before first flight; repeat if crew composition changes.
meters.measurement_tool: High-fidelity flight simulator (visual, motion if available); NASA-TLX workload questionnaire; event-timing instrument (millisecond-level latency measurement); duty-cycle timer
meters.measurement_owner: CAA human factors inspector (test designer + observer) + independent test pilot; Workbench simulator provides scenario execution
meters.reasoning: MeaningfulHumanControl Scale specifies workload, takeover latency, concurrent events. This Meter is the simulation test. Accuracy is high because latencies and workload are objectively measured. Precision is medium because simulator fidelity varies. Cost is high (full simulator time). Speed is low (days). Credibility is VERY high because it's grounded in human factors standards (NASA-TLX) and realistic simulation. This is load-bearing: without evidence of adequate crew control, authorization cannot be granted. Confidence: 9.

meters.meter_definition: Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) is conducted on termination system (hardware, firmware, power supply, sensors, command path). From FMEA, >= 50 failure scenarios are identified. Test procedure: (1) For each scenario, inject the failure (simulator or hardware-in-the-loop testbed). (2) Trigger an off-nominal condition (e.g., geofence breach detected, C2 loss, autonomy fault). (3) Measure: does termination system trigger correctly? Does termination execute (motors off, safe descent)? What is execution time? (4) Record success/failure. Target: >= 95% of scenarios result in successful termination within [TerminationTime] (e.g., 10 seconds from trigger to safe state). Environmental robustness: repeat test subset across environmental extremes (wind 0-25 kt, temperature -20 to +60°C, electromagnetic interference). Cost: 1-2 weeks for FMEA + 2-3 days for test execution. Run once before first flight; repeat after any termination system redesign.
meters.measurement_tool: FMEA template (standard MIL-STD-1629A format); failure injection testbed (simulator or hardware test rig); timer (measure execution latency); environmental chamber (optional for robustness testing)
meters.measurement_owner: Workbench engine (simulation) + Range Safety Officer (hardware testbed) + independent safety auditor (FMEA review)
meters.reasoning: IndependentFlightTermination Scale specifies trigger reliability + environmental robustness. This Meter is the FMEA-derived fault-injection test. Accuracy is high (success measured objectively). Precision is high (repeatable scenarios). Cost is high (weeks of FMEA + test time). Speed is low. Credibility is VERY high because FMEA is a recognized safety methodology and fault-injection is industry-standard validation. This is CRITICAL load-bearing evidence: independent termination must work or swarm poses unacceptable risk. Confidence: 9.

meters.meter_definition: Before flight commences, data recording system is audited: (1) Schema audit: verify all required state variables are logged (position XYZ, velocity, acceleration, heading, engine RPM, autonomy mode, sensor inputs, crew commands, system alerts). Target: all 15+ variables present. (2) Sampling rate audit: verify sampling frequency meets spec (>= 10Hz for nominal state, >= 50Hz for high-dynamics phases). (3) Data integrity audit: post-flight, a sample of logged data is checked for gaps > 100ms, checksums verified, timestamp continuity verified. Target: >= 99% of data intervals are gap-free and valid. (4) Forensic provenance: verify each data file has cryptographic hash, timestamp, source certification (e.g., 'aircraft-0-onboard-logger'). Cost: 2-3 hours for pre-flight audit, 1 hour per flight for post-flight integrity check. Run before every flight.
meters.measurement_tool: Data schema checklist; data logger configuration parser; gap detector (automated script); checksum validator; audit log template
meters.measurement_owner: Workbench engine (automated checks) + Flight Safety Officer (human review of integrity results)
meters.reasoning: MORForensicCompleteness Scale requires complete recorded evidence (schema, sampling rate, integrity, provenance). This Meter is the pre/post-flight audit. Accuracy is high (objective checks). Precision is high. Cost is low (mostly automated). Speed is high. Credibility is high because it's objective and verifiable. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_definition: For each crew member and assigned role (pilot, sensor operator, safety officer), a simulator-based assessment is conducted. Scenarios test: (1) Link-loss response (correct execution of RTL / contingency procedures), (2) Geofence-breach response (abort mission, land immediately), (3) DAA encounter response (acknowledge alert, position swarm for safe separation), (4) Swarm-member health-alert response (remove member from formation, continue mission safely). Each scenario is run twice; crew must achieve >= 90% success rate (correct procedure, appropriate latency, no secondary errors). Scoring: pass/fail per procedure. Target: 100% of required procedures scored PASS. Cost: 4-6 hours per crew member (scenario prep + briefing + runs + debrief). Run once per crew member before authorization, then annually thereafter.
meters.measurement_tool: High-fidelity flight simulator; procedure checklist (Go/No-Go scoring); video record (for replay/review); training records template
meters.measurement_owner: CAA training inspector + qualified check pilot; Workbench simulator provides scenario execution
meters.reasoning: CrewCompetencyEvidence Scale requires demonstrated proficiency in simulation (>= 90% success). This Meter is the emergency-procedure simulation test. Accuracy is high (pass/fail is objective). Precision is high (repeatable procedures). Cost is medium (simulator time). Credibility is high because simulation is a recognized training/validation method. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_definition: A responsibility matrix is constructed (rows = safety-critical design/operational domains from SORA hazard list + design domains + operational domains; columns = CAA responsibility, MAA responsibility, shared, unassigned). For each row, the applicant assigns responsibility to exactly one party (CAA, MAA, or explicitly shared with defined sub-responsibilities). Auditor verifies: (1) HazardCoverage: all SORA-identified hazards are assigned (count unassigned = target: 0). (2) DesignDomainCoverage: avionics design, swarm decision logic, autonomy limits, geofence system, termination system (count unassigned = target: 0). (3) OperationalDomainCoverage: airspace segregation, crew training, incident response, post-trial close-out (count unassigned = target: 0). (4) OverlapDetection: no domain is assigned to both CAA and MAA (count overlaps = target: 0). Signed by authorized CAA and MAA representatives. Cost: 3-4 hours for matrix construction + audit. Run before case opening.
meters.measurement_tool: Responsibility matrix template (spreadsheet or structured form); traceability mapping (each domain -> assigned party); audit checklist (count gaps + overlaps)
meters.measurement_owner: CAA/MAA liaison (human) + case officer (verifies signatures)
meters.reasoning: CAAMAAApportionmentGapFreeness Scale requires 100% coverage with no gaps/overlaps. This Meter is the responsibility matrix audit. Accuracy is high (coverage is countable). Precision is high. Cost is low (desk work). Speed is high. Credibility is high because it's verifiable and signed by both parties. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_name: Phased Gate Criteria & Reversibility Procedure Specification
meters.meter_definition: For each trial phase (e.g., Phase 1: 2-aircraft VLOS, Phase 2: 4-aircraft VLOS, Phase 3: 4-aircraft BVLOS), a gate specification document is drafted. For each gate, the document contains: (1) Gate Criterion (quantified evidence threshold, e.g., 'Phase 1 gate: 10 successful flights with zero loss-of-separation incidents'). (2) Reversibility Procedure (if new evidence emerges that suggests unacceptable risk, how does trial exit the phase? E.g., 'If loss-of-separation event occurs, Phase 1 gate fails; trial reverts to ground testing'). (3) Data Immutability (all phase evidence is preserved in write-once storage or audit-logged so gates can be re-evaluated). (4) Re-entry Procedure (what additional evidence is required before re-attempting gate progression? E.g., '5 additional flights with new geofence design before Phase 2 re-try'). Auditor verifies: Gate count >= 4, 100% of gates have quantified criteria, 100% have documented exit/reversal procedures, data immutability confirmed. Cost: 2-3 hours for document review. Run once at trial start.
meters.measurement_tool: Gate specification template (structured form); reversibility procedure checklist; data immutability verification (audit log + write-once storage config)
meters.measurement_owner: CAA programme manager (human) + case officer
meters.reasoning: PhasedGateReversibility Scale requires >= 4 gates with clear criteria and reversibility. This Meter is the gate specification audit. Accuracy is high (criteria are countable). Precision is high. Cost is low. Speed is high. Credibility is high because it's documented and verifiable. Confidence: 8.

meters.meter_name: CAP 1616 Consultation Audit & Community Engagement Report
meters.meter_definition: CAP 1616 (Engagement, Coordination and Access for Airspace Users) prescribes a consultation process. Meter: auditor reviews consultation evidence package submitted by applicant. Verifies: (1) StakeholderIdentification: applicant has identified >= 5 distinct stakeholder groups (residents within [Radius] km, landowners, environmental groups, aviation operators, media). (2) PriorNotification: >= 30 days advance notice issued to stakeholders before first flight. (3) FeedbackCollection: structured feedback mechanism (survey, workshop, forum) documented; track percentage of stakeholders responding. (4) FeedbackResponseCoverage: for each feedback item, applicant has documented response (acceptance, mitigation, or rejection with rationale). Target: >= 80% of feedback items receive documented response. Consultation log archived with case file. Cost: 3-4 hours for audit. Run before TDA approval.
meters.measurement_tool: CAP 1616 consultation checklist; stakeholder contact log; feedback collection records (survey results, meeting minutes); response log (spreadsheet or database)
meters.measurement_owner: CAA engagement officer (human) + case officer
meters.reasoning: PublicEngagementCoverage Scale requires >= 5 stakeholder groups engaged with documented feedback response. This Meter is the CAP 1616 audit. Accuracy is medium (stakeholder adequacy is subjective). Precision is medium. Cost is low (desk audit). Speed is high. Credibility is medium because it's documentary evidence; actual community satisfaction is harder to measure. Confidence: 7.

past.source: JARUS SORA adoption anecdata; industry reports on first-submission pack acceptance rates
past.estimation_note: Based on public guidance from CAA innovation hub and ASTM F3639 early adoption reports. PDRA packs show ~70-80% first-submission completeness; BVLOS swarm packs much lower (~30-50%). Estimated at 70% as a weighted average across operation types.

status.source: Industry anecdata; no live CAA operational data available
status.estimation_note: Baseline estimated at 72% based on slow industry maturation. No measured data on current pack completeness across UK UAS operators. Estimate assumes modest improvement from FY 2024 due to AMLAS/SACE pilot learning.

record.holder: Mature commercial BVLOS operators (e.g., Airborne Support, DJI Enterprise partners)
record.source: Public case studies and CAA trial program outcomes (AMLAS, SACE pilot sites); EUROCAE WG6 maturity assessments

ideal.rationale: All mandatory document types and sections present on first readiness check, per Alexa's scale definition. 100% is the theoretical maximum for a well-governed regulatory submission.

trend.source: Inference from AMLAS/SACE pilot data and growing JARUS SORA adoption

data_gaps: CAA annual safety reports do not publish first-submission pack completeness rates disaggregated by operation type. Request this metric from CAA Innovation Hub or through FOIA.
No published data on completeness rates for BVLOS swarm packs specifically. Requires targeted survey of operators participating in CAA trials (Project XCelerate, AMLAS, SACE).
Industry anecdata on rejection rates is scattered across consultancy reports and operator internal metrics. Suggest CAA mandate completeness reporting as part of trial KPIs.
benchmarks.reasoning: PackCompleteness is the first gate in the authorization flow. Industry estimates suggest ~70-80% of first submissions are rejected due to incomplete documentation, especially for novel operation types like swarms. Bridging the 28% gap from Status to Ideal is critical for CAA throughput and operator experience. Without field data, benchmarks rely on inference from AMLAS/SACE pilot learning and published ASTM frameworks.

past.source: JARUS SORA v2.5 adoption timeline; EUROCAE WG6 swarm guidance release (late 2024)
past.estimation_note: JARUS SORA v2.5 explicitly addressed swarm-specific failure modes only in 2024-2025. Pre-2025 SORA packages had near-zero swarm-aware risk assessment. Estimated 5% as a small fraction of early adopters.

status.source: JARUS SORA v2.5 adoption curve; AMLAS/SACE pilot integration
status.estimation_note: Status estimated at 15% based on ~18 months of JARUS SORA v2.5 deployment and AMLAS pilot momentum. Early operators integrating swarm-aware risk taxonomy, but majority still using legacy SORA v2.0.

record.unit: count of evidence items per aircraft (evidence coverage)
record.holder: Academic and research operators (e.g., Centre for Assuring Autonomy, York CAA centre); AMLAS pilot frontrunners
record.source: AMLAS case study outputs; EUROCAE WG6 published exemplars (published 2025)

ideal.unit: percentage of submissions with >= 3 distinct swarm-specific SORA items per aircraft
ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale, target is >= 3 distinct items per aircraft establishing swarm-aware risk factors. Ideal assumes 80% of submissions meet this standard at authorization.

trend.source: JARUS SORA v2.5 adoption curve and AMLAS/SACE pilot learning dissemination

data_gaps: CAA does not publish swarm-aware SORA evidence metrics in annual safety reports. Request disaggregated data from SORA assessment team or AMLAS integration workstream.
JARUS SORA v2.5 adoption rates by operator are not publicly tracked. EUROCAE WG6 may have internal data; suggest CAA liaison to obtain.
Published exemplars of swarm-aware SORA evidence are limited to AMLAS case studies. Recommend CAA publish additional public guidance or case studies to drive adoption.
benchmarks.reasoning: SwarmAwareSORAEvidence is a novel value — JARUS SORA v2.5 only recently addressed swarm-specific failure modes. Status (15%) is alarmingly far from Ideal (80%), reflecting the state of emerging technology regulation. The 10-15% quarterly improvement rate is optimistic but achievable with AMLAS/SACE learning feedback loops. This is a critical gap: swarm-aware risk assessment is foundational to safe authorization.

past.source: CAA safety case review feedback (anecdata); ASTM F3642 clarity assessment guide
past.estimation_note: Estimated at 50% based on public feedback that ConOps documents often lack explicit worst-credible behavior boundaries. PDRA operators tend to score higher (~60-70%); swarm operators much lower (~30-40%).

status.source: CAA safety case reviewer feedback; no formal metrics published
status.estimation_note: Status estimated at 55% based on incremental improvement from AMLAS/SACE case study learning. Many operators still conflate nominal behavior with operational limits rather than worst-credible boundaries.

record.holder: Mature military aviation programs (RAF Protector, Reaper); leading civil operators (e.g., Airborne Support)
record.source: Public RAF Protector program documentation; AUVSI published best-practice ConOps exemplars

ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale: all ConOps sections explicitly define nominal and worst-credible behavior for each phase, with inter-rater reliability (kappa >= 0.8). 100% clarity is the theoretical maximum.

trend.source: CAA ConOps guidance maturity; industry adoption of ASTM F3642 clarity criteria

data_gaps: CAA does not publish ConOps clarity metrics in annual reports. Request inter-rater reliability data (kappa scores) from CAA safety case review team.
ASTM F3642 deployment and adoption rates are unknown. Contact ASTM F38 committee for empirical clarity assessment data.
Military program ConOps exemplars (RAF Protector, Reaper) are classified. Suggest CAA request sanitized exemplars for civil UAS operator reference.
benchmarks.reasoning: ConOpsClarity measures the shared understanding of what the aircraft will do under nominal and off-nominal conditions. Current Status (55%) is significantly below Ideal (100%), reflecting a widespread gap in safety case documentation quality. This gap directly impacts operational risk assessment and crew training design. Low inter-rater agreement suggests ConOps documents are ambiguous or insufficiently detailed.

past.source: CAA annual safety reports on geofence-related incidents; published BVLOS trial outcomes (Project XCelerate, AUVSI data)
past.estimation_note: CAA safety reports document fly-away incidents; geofence-related containment failures are rare but non-zero. Estimated baseline effectiveness at >= 99.5% for well-designed systems, based on published incident rates.

status.source: Continuous monitoring via CAA incident reporting (MOR); trial outcome data from AMLAS/SACE
status.estimation_note: Status based on reported containment effectiveness in recent BVLOS trials. Slight improvement from FY 2024 due to better geofence algorithm maturity and Monte Carlo validation practices.

record.holder: Leading military and commercial operators with redundant containment (RAF Protector; Airborne Support BVLOS fleet)
record.source: Published trial outcomes; AUVSI flight test data; EUROCAE WG6 field experience

ideal.rationale: Zero boundary exceedances in any simulated or operational scenario. 100% containment is the safety imperative for autonomous flight over populated areas.

trend.source: CAA incident data; diminishing returns on geofence redundancy investment

data_gaps: CAA annual safety reports aggregate incidents by category but do not separately report geofence-specific containment failures. Request disaggregated data from CAA Flight Safety branch.
Monte Carlo validation practices and acceptance thresholds vary widely across operators. Suggest CAA mandate EUROCAE WG6 geofence validation standard (in development).
Military program geofence performance data (RAF Protector, Reaper) is classified. Suggest CAA request performance summaries for civil operator benchmark reference.
benchmarks.reasoning: GeofenceContainment is a safety-critical value with the highest measured baseline (99.5%+). The gap from Status to Ideal is minimal (0.4%), indicating mature technology and strong industry practice. However, as swarm operations scale, the probability of at least one aircraft breaching geofence in a multi-aircraft formation increases combinatorially. The stable trend reflects engineering maturity but suggests continued investment in Monte Carlo validation and redundancy is required to achieve Ideal.

past.source: BVLOS trial outcomes (Project XCelerate, published ASTM F3550 test data)
past.estimation_note: Commercial BVLOS systems typically demonstrate 30-60 seconds of autonomous survival after C2 loss, with crew takeover available within 120 seconds. Estimated at 30 seconds for conservative baseline.

status.source: Current BVLOS swarm trial data; AMLAS/SACE pilot outcomes
status.estimation_note: Recent swarm operators report 45-60 seconds autonomous survival, with enhanced inter-vehicle link loss protocols. Status estimated at 45 seconds (middle of observed range).

record.holder: Military autonomous systems (RAF Protector, Reaper); leading civil operators with redundant C2
record.source: Published RAF Protector program documentation; AUVSI best-practice data

ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale: >= 30 seconds autonomous survival for loss-of-C2; >= 120 seconds if crew takeover available. Ideal set at 300 seconds as a stretch target for highly autonomous swarms with mission-level decision-making.

trend.source: Autonomous decision-making algorithm maturity; inter-vehicle communication redundancy improvements

data_gaps: CAA does not publish C2 link resilience benchmarks in annual safety reports. Request test data from BVLOS trial programs (XCelerate, AMLAS, SACE).
ASTM F3550 test results are proprietary to operators. Suggest CAA sponsor publication of anonymized benchmark data from recent trials.
Inter-vehicle link loss resilience is rarely tested or reported separately. Recommend CAA mandate this as a distinct KPI for swarm operations.
benchmarks.reasoning: C2LinkResilience measures the time available for autonomous survival and crew takeover. Current Status (45 seconds) is well above Alexa's minimum requirement (30 seconds) but far below Ideal (300 seconds). The 255-second gap reflects the engineering challenge of implementing safe autonomous decision-making without ground control. Swarms face additional risk: loss of inter-vehicle link can trigger formation breakup. Trend is improving but slowly.

past.source: DO-365 minimum operational performance standard; published ASTM F3442 test data
past.estimation_note: DO-365 defines minimum DAA performance at 99.9% detection of cooperative intruders at 90% confidence. Non-cooperative detection much lower (70-90%). Estimated at 85% as a weighted average.

status.source: BVLOS trial DAA test results; AMLAS/SACE performance metrics
status.estimation_note: Recent trials show modest improvement in non-cooperative encounter resolution. Status estimated at 88% based on published AMLAS case study outcomes.

record.holder: Military autonomous systems with advanced radar and sensor fusion (RAF Protector, Reaper); research systems with cooperative airspace
record.source: Published military program data; ASTM F3442 exemplary system outcomes

ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale: consistent resolution with pre-defined decision rules, recovery within target time, no secondary conflicts, >= 95% decision consistency. Ideal assumes 99.95% resolution success for all encounter types (cooperative and non-cooperative).

trend.source: Sensor fusion algorithms; non-cooperative detection algorithm maturity

data_gaps: CAA does not publish disaggregated DAA performance metrics (cooperative vs non-cooperative encounter resolution rates). Request from flight safety assessment team.
ASTM F3442 test data is proprietary to operators and aircraft manufacturers. Suggest CAA sponsor publication of anonymized performance benchmarks from DO-365 certification testing.
Swarm coordination upon encounter is rarely tested separately. Recommend CAA mandate this as a distinct DAA sub-metric for multi-aircraft operations.
benchmarks.reasoning: DAAPerformance measures the reliability of collision avoidance in populated airspace. Current Status (88%) significantly exceeds the 85% baseline but remains below Ideal (99.95%). The 11.95% gap reflects challenges in non-cooperative encounter detection and decision consistency, particularly for novel swarm formations. Trend is improving but constrained by non-cooperative radar detection physics and algorithm reliability plateaus.

past.source: AMLAS pilot program case studies; EUROCAE WG6 early swarm guidance (FY 2024)
past.estimation_note: Early swarm programs (2023-2024) identified few emergent behaviors explicitly. Most focused on coordinated position-keeping rather than formation geometry, decision-making, or flocking. Estimated at 2 behaviors (formation + coordination latency).

status.source: AMLAS/SACE pilot maturity; EUROCAE WG6 swarm decision-making guidance (released 2025)
status.estimation_note: Recent operators identifying additional emergent behaviors (flocking, coordinated maneuver, swarm split/rejoin). Status estimated at 3 behaviors, approaching Alexa's scale target of >= 5.

record.holder: Research swarms and leading AMLAS pilot operators (Centre for Assuring Autonomy, University of Bristol swarm research)
record.source: Published AMLAS case studies; EUROCAE WG6 exemplary behavior inventories

ideal.unit: count of behaviors with quantified boundaries, recovery mechanisms, and >= 95% test coverage
ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale: >= 5 emergent behaviors with quantified boundaries, detection criteria, and documented recovery mechanisms. Ideal set at 8 to accommodate complex swarm coordination (formation + geometry + maneuver + decision + flocking + split/rejoin + emergent failure + collective evasion).

trend.source: AMLAS/SACE learning; EUROCAE WG6 swarm taxonomy maturity

data_gaps: No standard taxonomy of emergent swarm behaviors exists. EUROCAE WG6 is developing guidance (target: 2026). Request draft when available.
AMLAS case study data on emergent behavior identification is proprietary. Suggest CAA request anonymized exemplars for public reference.
Test coverage of emergent behaviors (percentage of flight test hours within bounds) is not systematically reported. Recommend CAA mandate this KPI for swarm authorizations.
benchmarks.reasoning: SwarmEmergentBoundedness is a novel domain with minimal industry practice. Current Status (3 behaviors) is significantly below Ideal (8), reflecting early-stage understanding of swarm dynamics. The 5-behavior gap reflects the fundamental challenge: swarm behavior emerges from interaction of multiple aircraft, is difficult to predict, and can violate safety invariants. Trend is optimistic but depends on EUROCAE WG6 guidance adoption and operator investment in emergent-behavior testing.

past.source: AMLAS pilot program; industry survey of UAS ML practices (ASTM F38 working group)
past.estimation_note: Early ML-based UAS systems (2023-2024) had minimal explainability instrumentation. Decision trees scored high (>80% explainability); neural networks very low (~5-10%). Weighted average estimated at 20%.

status.source: AMLAS/SACE pilot outcomes; evolving industry practice on LIME/SHAP adoption
status.estimation_note: Modest improvement from AMLAS case studies demonstrating LIME/SHAP instrumentation and frozen-model documentation. Status estimated at 28% based on slow industry adoption of explainability tooling.

record.holder: Academic research systems (Centre for Assuring Autonomy; University of Bristol safety-critical ML lab); AMLAS pilot frontrunners with decision-tree-based systems
record.source: Published AMLAS case studies; academic ML assurance research

ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale: >= 80% decision-path coverage via saliency maps + LIME/SHAP for neural networks; 100% for decision trees. Ideal set at 90% to reflect practical limits of neural network interpretability.

trend.source: LIME/SHAP tooling maturity; AMLAS guidance adoption; regulatory pressure on ML transparency

ASTM F38 ML assurance standard is in development (target: 2026). Explainability metrics will be refined; current benchmarks may shift.
Frozen-model documentation and decision logging practices are not systematically measured. Recommend CAA mandate this as a certification KPI for ML-based systems.
benchmarks.reasoning: MLForensicExplainability is a critical challenge for autonomous swarm regulation. Current Status (28%) is dangerously far from Ideal (90%), reflecting the fundamental tension between model performance (neural networks, which are opaque) and explainability (decision trees, which are slower). The 62% gap is the single largest data gap in this analysis. Status improvement relies on industry adoption of explainability tooling (LIME/SHAP, saliency mapping) and CAA acceptance of proxy explanations for neural networks.

past.source: CAA human factors inspection data; ASTM F38 early human factors guidance
past.estimation_note: FY 2024 BVLOS operators showed moderate human control: situational awareness typically >= 2Hz, authority preservation ~70% for reversible decisions, takeover latency >= 10 seconds available. Composite estimated at 45 (SituationalAwareness: 60, AuthorityPreservation: 50, TakeoverLatency: 70, WorkloadBounds: 20).

status.source: Recent BVLOS swarm trial human factors assessments; AMLAS/SACE crew training data
status.estimation_note: Modest improvement in crew training and HMI design. Status estimated at 52 (SituationalAwareness: 65, AuthorityPreservation: 55, TakeoverLatency: 75, WorkloadBounds: 35). Swarm operations increase workload burden.

record.holder: Military crewed aircraft with advanced cockpit design (e.g., F-35, Apache); leading civil operators with mature HMI (Airborne Support)

ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale: SituationalAwareness >= 2Hz nominal / >= 10Hz off-nominal; AuthorityPreservation >= 100% for irreversible / >= 80% for reversible; TakeoverLatency >= 10 seconds; WorkloadBounds <= 3 concurrent alarms, <= 6 min active control/hour. Ideal score reflects full compliance with all four dimensions.

trend.source: HMI design maturity; crew training standardization; swarm automation limiting crew decision load

data_gaps: CAA does not publish human factors assessment metrics. Request data from human factors inspection team or conduct structured survey of recent BVLOS trials.
No standard human factors workload measurement tool exists for UAS crews. ASTM F38 is developing guidance; recommend CAA adopt when available.
Swarm-specific human factors (multi-aircraft display design, alert prioritization, crew coordination) are rarely assessed. Recommend CAA commission dedicated human factors study for swarm operations.
benchmarks.reasoning: MeaningfulHumanControl is inherently subjective but measurable via four converging dimensions. Current Status (52) is moderately below Ideal (100), reflecting ongoing design work in crewed-autonomous teaming. The largest deficit is WorkloadBounds (35 vs 100): swarm operations generate multiple concurrent events (engine failure, loss-of-link, emergent behavior anomaly) that can overwhelm crews. Trend is improving but constrained by the fundamental challenge of managing high-dimensional swarm state with limited crew attention.

past.source: BVLOS trial outcomes; published flight termination test data (ASTM F3642, EUROCAE WG6)
past.estimation_note: Commercial flight termination systems (parachute, propeller cutoff) achieve >98% effectiveness in ground test. Field data shows similar rates but limited sample size. Estimated at 98% for well-designed single-channel systems.

status.estimation_note: Dual-redundant and cross-linked termination systems in recent swarms show >99% effectiveness. Status estimated at 99.1% based on published trial outcomes.

ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale: >= 99.9% effectiveness across all off-nominal scenarios, within target time, with post-termination actions (crew alert, ATSU notification, data preservation). Ideal assumes 99.99% (four nines) as a safety imperative.

trend.source: Diminishing returns on redundancy; fundamental component failure rates plateau

data_gaps: CAA does not publish flight termination effectiveness metrics separately. Request from flight safety assessment team.
ASTM F3642 and EUROCAE WG6 termination test methods are evolving. Current benchmarks may shift with revised standards.

Swarm-specific termination challenges (multi-aircraft coordination, inter-vehicle link loss triggering termination) are rarely tested. Recommend CAA mandate this as a distinct KPI for swarm operations.
benchmarks.reasoning: IndependentFlightTermination is a mature safety technology with high measured effectiveness (99.1%). The gap from Status to Ideal (0.89%) is minimal, reflecting engineering maturity. However, swarms introduce complexity: a multi-aircraft termination requires coordination to prevent mid-air collisions during descent. The stable trend reflects engineering plateau—further improvement requires breakthrough approaches (e.g., provably correct termination logic, triple redundancy) with diminishing ROI.

past.source: CAA mandatory occurrence reporting (MOR) database analysis; published incident investigation reports
past.estimation_note: CAA regulations require incident data logging; compliance is high (>85%) for systems with modern flight data recorders. However, data gaps (>100ms intervals) are common. Estimated at 85% for systems with adequate logging.

status.source: Current BVLOS trial data logging practices; AMLAS/SACE pilot outcomes
status.estimation_note: Recent trials show improved data integrity (fewer gaps) due to redundant logging. Status estimated at 88% based on published outcomes.

record.holder: Military systems with triple-redundant flight data recording (RAF Protector, Reaper); research systems with forensic-grade logging
record.source: Published military program documentation; academic forensic data-logging research

ideal.rationale: Per Alexa's scale: 100% of incident events with complete recorded evidence, all state variables >= 10Hz, data integrity (no gaps > 100ms), forensic provenance verified, retention through MOR investigation. 100% is the safety imperative.

trend.source: Flight data recorder technology maturity; redundant logging adoption

data_gaps: CAA MOR database does not publish data logging completeness metrics. Request from flight safety investigation team.
Forensic provenance standards (cryptographic hashing, timestamp certification) for UAS data are not yet standardized. EUROCAE WG6 may develop guidance; check status.

Swarm-specific logging challenges (per-aircraft state logging, inter-vehicle link state, formation geometry) are rarely addressed. Recommend CAA mandate comprehensive logging schema for swarm incidents.
benchmarks.reasoning: MORForensicCompleteness is a measured value with strong industry baseline (88%). The 12% gap from Status to Ideal reflects challenges in achieving zero data gaps (>100ms), forensic provenance certification, and retention across multi-year investigations. Trend is improving gradually as flight data recorder technology matures. For swarms, the logging challenge multiplies: 5-aircraft formation requires logging ~100+ state variables at >= 10Hz, generating high-bandwidth data streams.

past.source: CAA licensing and training records; published UAS operator certification standards

The 1.5-competency gap reflects the emerging nature of swarm crew training. Trend is improving as swarm-operations endorsement becomes standard, but adoption is slow due to training curriculum development lead time.

past.source: Published CAA/MAA trial apportionment matrices (Project XCelerate, AMLAS pilot); CAA governance records
past.estimation_note: FY 2024 apportionment matrices for major trials covered ~75% of hazards and domains. Gaps typically in emerging areas (swarm coordination, emergent behavior, ML explainability). Estimated at 75%.

status.estimation_note: Modest improvement from AMLAS/SACE integration and clearer CAA/MAA role definitions. Status estimated at 80%.

benchmarks.reasoning: CAAMAAApportionmentGapFreeness measures regulatory clarity. Current Status (80%) is significantly below Ideal (100%), with a 20% gap indicating ambiguous responsibility assignments. This is particularly acute for novel domains (swarm-specific risks, emergent behaviors, ML assurance) where neither CAA nor MAA has published guidance. The gap creates regulatory risk for operators: unclear who is responsible for swarm-specific SORA assessment, who approves emergent-behavior evidence, who certifies ML explainability.

past.source: Published trial protocols (Project XCelerate, AMLAS pilot); EUROCAE WG6 guidance (FY 2024)

status.estimation_note: Recent trials showing improved gate definition and early reversibility documentation. Status estimated at 2.5 gates.

Current Status (2.5 gates) is far below Ideal (6), reflecting early-stage adoption of reversible phased trial design. The 3.5-gate gap reflects the regulatory innovation challenge: traditional aerospace test and evaluation is forward-only (pass/fail gates) rather than reversible. For swarms, this is critical: discovering an unexpected emergent behavior should trigger phase reversal, not program cancellation.

REQ017 has no user story and is explicitly absent from the requirements spec, indicating regulatory deprioritization. However, GDPR and CAP 1616 implicitly mandate community consultation. Swarm operations over UK airspace will trigger public concern (noise, privacy, safety). The trend is improving slowly but remains inadequate for social-license sustainability.

constraints.conditions: all mandatory document types and sections contain substantive content on first readiness check; binary gate (pass/fail)

constraints.reasoning: PackCompleteness is binary by design—pack either passes acceptance gate or rejected for rework. Tolerable = 100% per Alexa's scale. Industry baseline (72%) reflects widespread rework cycles; constraint is to improve upstream intake practices.

constraints.unit: count of evidence items per aircraft
constraints.deadline: by SORA Assessment gate (before first flight trial)
constraints.conditions: at least 3 distinct SORA evidence items per aircraft addressing swarm-specific failure modes (loss-of-separation, loss-of-coordination, emergent-failure)
constraints.regulatory_anchor: JARUS SORA v2.5 swarm-aware risk taxonomy
constraints.reasoning: JARUS SORA v2.5 (2025) introduced swarm guidance. Status 15% far below Ideal 80%. Tolerable set at >= 3 items/aircraft as absolute minimum for defensible risk assessment. Below this, authorization is unattainable.

constraints.unit: count of evidence items per aircraft
constraints.conditions: JARUS SORA v2.5 conformance mandates >= 5 distinct evidence items per aircraft for complex swarms (>= 3 aircraft)
constraints.regulatory_anchor: JARUS SORA v2.5 Appendix C (swarm-specific risk assessment methodology)
constraints.reasoning: Regulatory minimum from JARUS SORA v2.5 published guidance. Industry adoption slow (15%) due to novelty.

constraints.conditions: ConOps sections explicitly define nominal and worst-credible behavior for >= 85% of operational phases, with inter-rater clarity (kappa >= 0.8)
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B ConOps clarity and ODD definition
constraints.reasoning: Status 55% significantly below Tolerable 85%. Clarity essential for crew training and safety case coherence. Current gap (30 points) indicates lack of explicit worst-credible boundaries.

constraints.conditions: Monte Carlo simulation (>= 10,000 trials) shows >= 99.5% success preventing boundary exceedance across ODD and single/dual failures
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B loss-of-control containment criteria
constraints.reasoning: Status 99.6% already above Tolerable. Minimal gap reflects technology maturity. Swarm ops increase breach probability combinatorially.

constraints.conditions: below 99% containment, regulator cannot authorize due to excessive fly-away risk
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B authorization revocation threshold
constraints.reasoning: Survival threshold: below 99%, uncontrolled breach risk is unacceptable and authorization refused.

constraints.conditions: for populated trial areas (residential density > 0.1/hectare), regulator demands 99.9% standard
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B populated-area risk acceptance
constraints.reasoning: Regulatory override for populated airspace. Higher population density requires lower breach tolerance.

constraints.conditions: C2 link maintains >= 99% availability; loss triggers autonomous safe state within 30 seconds
constraints.gap_from_status: availability metric (99% vs status 45-second survival time not directly comparable)
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B link resilience and autonomous safety
constraints.reasoning: 99% availability is binding constraint. Below this, crew cannot reliably regain control. Status (45-second survival) meets Survival (30s) but link uptime is regulator requirement.

constraints.deadline: mandatory minimum for all BVLOS operations
constraints.conditions: after C2 loss, aircraft maintains safe state (within geofence) >= 30 seconds for crew RTL trigger
constraints.regulatory_anchor: EUROCAE WG6 autonomy survivability baseline
constraints.reasoning: 30 seconds = minimum human reaction time for crew detection and RTL command. Status already meets this.

constraints.conditions: DAA resolves >= 99.5% of cooperative and non-cooperative encounter scenarios predictably, with recovery within target time and no secondary conflicts
