constraints.reasoning: DO-365 baseline 99.9% for cooperative. Tolerable 99.5% accounts for non-cooperative difficulty and swarm complexity. Status 88% far below; primary gap is non-cooperative resolution.

constraints.conditions: DO-365 minimum: 99.9% detection confidence for cooperative DAA systems
constraints.regulatory_anchor: DO-365 Minimum Operational Performance Standard
constraints.reasoning: Table-stakes regulatory compliance. Non-cooperative detection is primary challenge.

constraints.unit: percentage of test flight hours within documented bounds
constraints.conditions: swarm operates within all emergent-behavior boundaries >= 95% of accumulated test flight hours; out-of-bounds events logged
constraints.regulatory_anchor: EUROCAE WG6 swarm assurance (draft 2025); RA 2330 military precedent
constraints.reasoning: Tolerable: >= 95% compliance. Status (3 behaviors identified, ~40% estimated compliance) far below. Once boundaries defined, swarm must respect them or behavior is uncontrolled.

constraints.unit: percentage of test flight hours within bounds
constraints.conditions: out-of-bounds in > 10% of hours triggers trial pause and emergent-behavior investigation
constraints.regulatory_anchor: RA 2330 behavior compliance margin precedent
constraints.reasoning: Survival: 90% compliance minimum. Higher out-of-bounds frequency suggests behaviors not well-understood. Trial pauses.

constraints.unit: percentage of decisions reconstructable by independent reviewer
constraints.conditions: Decision trees 100%; SVMs feature importance + boundary; neural networks saliency + LIME/SHAP >= 80% coverage. >= 80% safety-critical decisions independently reconstructable.
constraints.regulatory_anchor: AAIB Accident Investigation Regulations 2015 investigability requirement
constraints.reasoning: Tolerable: >= 80%. Status 28% dangerously low; 52-point gap largest in analysis. Ensures AAIB can investigate most decisions.

constraints.conditions: below 70%, AAIB refuses to investigate black-box decisions, blocking authorization
constraints.regulatory_anchor: AAIB Accident Investigation Regulations 2015
constraints.reasoning: Survival: below 70%, system is unassurable. AAIB will not investigate.

constraints.conditions: SituationalAwareness >= 2Hz nominal / >= 10Hz off-nominal; AuthorityPreservation >= 80% reversible / 100% irreversible; TakeoverLatency >= 10s; WorkloadBounds <= 3 concurrent alarms, <= 6 min active/hour. Score 70+.
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722 human oversight; EU Regulation 2019/945 operational authorization criteria
constraints.reasoning: Tolerable composite 70 reflects balance between autonomy and crew authority. Status 52 below Tolerable. Largest deficit WorkloadBounds (35/100)—swarm events overwhelm crew. Crew must retain meaningful control.

constraints.conditions: Termination triggers correctly and executes completely >= 99.9% of simulated off-nominal scenarios (>= 50 distinct). Safe configuration achieved without secondary hazards.
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B flight termination assurance (RA 2330 military precedent)
constraints.reasoning: Tolerable: >= 99.9%. Status 99.1% nearly at Tolerable. Swarms add complexity: multi-aircraft termination must coordinate to prevent mid-air collisions during descent.

constraints.conditions: below 99%, flight ops cannot authorize due to uncontrolled descent risk
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722B authorization gate
constraints.reasoning: Survival: failures > 1% make uncontrolled descent probable. Status meets this.

constraints.unit: percentage of incident events with complete recorded evidence
constraints.conditions: All incidents logged with complete state (XYZ, velocity, accel, heading, engine, autonomy, sensors, crew, alerts) >= 10Hz, no gaps > 100ms, forensic provenance verified, retained for MOR + 1 year.
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAA Occurrence Reporting Regulations; AAIB Accident Investigation Regulations 2015
constraints.reasoning: Tolerable = 100%: either fully logged or investigation compromised. Status 88% indicates gaps in logging and provenance adoption.

constraints.conditions: Each crew member: (1) CAA UAS operator cert, (2) aircraft type rating, (3) swarm-operations endorsement, (4) role-specific training (pass/fail), (5) emergency-procedure sim >= 90% success. Plus minimum hours (50 pilot, 20 sensor op).
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722C crew licensing and training requirements
constraints.reasoning: Tolerable: 5 competencies per crew member. Status 3.5 below. Gap reflects nascent swarm-operations endorsement adoption.

constraints.unit: percentage coverage with no gaps or overlaps
constraints.conditions: Signed CAA/MAA apportionment covers 100% SORA hazards, 100% design domains, 100% operational domains. Each aspect assigned to exactly one body. No gaps, no overlaps.
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 722 CAA/MAA coordination; MOD/CAA joint governance
constraints.reasoning: Binary: responsibility either clear or ambiguous. Status 80% = 20% ambiguity. Novel domains (swarm coordination, emergent behavior, ML assurance) lack clear ownership.

constraints.unit: percentage of gates with documented exit/reversal criteria
constraints.conditions: Every phased gate has: clear criterion, documented exit/reversal procedure, data immutability (write-once or audit-log), re-entry criteria. All evidence preserved.
constraints.gap_from_status: N/A — binary design requirement (either all gates reversible or not)
constraints.regulatory_anchor: EUROCAE WG6 phased trial guidance (draft 2025); military T&E doctrine (reversible gates)
constraints.reasoning: Tolerable = all gates reversible by design. Status 2.5 gates far below Ideal >= 6. Reversibility essential: trial pauses if emergent behavior detected, rather than cancels.

constraints.conditions: Engaged >= 5 groups (residents within 5km, landowners, environmental, aviation, media) with prior notification (>= 30 days), feedback collection, documented response, escalation of residual concerns.
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 1616 airspace change consultation process
constraints.reasoning: Tolerable per CAP 1616: >= 5 groups, >= 30-day lead. Status 2 far below. Social license critical for BVLOS swarms in populated areas. REQ017 has no user story but CAP 1616 applies.

constraints.conditions: For trials within 5km of residential areas, engage >= 6 groups (add local council as mandatory 6th per enhanced CAP 1616)
constraints.regulatory_anchor: CAP 1616 populated-area consultation mandate; GDPR data protection
constraints.reasoning: Regulatory override for populated trials. Higher engagement (6 groups, local council) required for planning coordination and social license.

targets.reasoning: Stretch = 8 behaviors with automated anomaly detection. Feasibility is uncertain because this requires integration of ML-based behavior monitoring into assurance case—a novel integration challenge.

targets.conditions: >= 80% of safety-critical ML decisions explainable via model-type-specific evidence (decision-tree: 100%, SVM: feature importance + boundary, neural network: LIME/SHAP with >= 80% coverage)

increments.milestone: by end Phase 2 (ML assurance case framework)

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA AI/ML Assurance Specialist (REQ009) — forensic ML evidence; Applicant — cost-driven model selection (trees vs neural nets)
targets.reasoning: Wish = 80% explainability reflects vocal regulator ambition to mainstream ML assurance. Current 28% status is dangerously low. 80% is achievable with LIME/SHAP adoption, frozen-model documentation, and explicit model-type selection criteria.

targets.conditions: >= 90% of safety-critical ML decisions with full explainability evidence, frozen-model manifest (training data provenance, hyperparameters, validation performance), and runtime decision logging with >= 80% decision-path coverage for neural networks

increments.milestone: by first approved MPTF (Model Preparation Test Flight)

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA AI/ML Assurance Specialist (REQ009) — committed ML explainability standard; CAA Safety-Case Reviewer — hazard cause-effect traceability
targets.reasoning: Goal = 90% by end 2027 is the team-committed ML assurance standard. Requires ASTM F38 ML standard adoption, AMLAS guidance dissemination, and operator investment in explainability tooling. Stretch feasibility due to neural network opacity challenge.

targets.conditions: >= 95% of safety-critical ML decisions with certified explainability (independent reviewer sign-off on decision reconstruction), certified frozen-model provenance (hash + timestamp), and automated anomaly-detection logging of out-of-distribution inputs
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA AI/ML Assurance Specialist — aspirational model forensic confidence; CAA Flight Safety Investigator (REQ012) — MOR investigation readiness
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 95% with certified provenance and anomaly detection. Feasibility is uncertain because certified explainability certification infrastructure does not yet exist in aerospace.

targets.conditions: Composite score >= 70 across SituationalAwareness (>= 2Hz nominal update frequency), AuthorityPreservation (>= 90% of irreversible decisions require crew sign-off), TakeoverLatency (>= 10 seconds available)

increments.milestone: by end Phase 3 (VLOS crew training validated)

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Human Factors Inspector (REQ010) — crew fitness and workload; Applicant — operational efficiency
targets.reasoning: Wish = 70 composite reflects achievable human factors improvement through HMI design iteration and crew training maturation. Targets situational awareness and authority preservation as primary levers.

targets.conditions: Composite >= 85 with SituationalAwareness >= 10Hz off-nominal, AuthorityPreservation >= 90% reversible + 100% irreversible, TakeoverLatency >= 10 sec, WorkloadBounds <= 0.6 duty cycle (per Alexa's sub-scale), all validated in VLOS and BVLOS trials

increments.milestone: by first joint panel (human factors readiness)

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Human Factors Inspector (REQ010) — committed human control standard; CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011) — contingency trigger clarity
targets.reasoning: Goal = 85 by end 2027 is the team-committed human control standard. Most challenging is WorkloadScore (currently ~35 status); swarm operations require alert prioritization and crew decision-support to achieve <= 0.6 duty cycle. Stretch feasibility due to HMI/workflow design maturity.

targets.conditions: Perfect [100] human control across all dimensions: >= 10Hz situational awareness, 100% authority preservation for all decisions, >= 10 sec takeover latency (all phases), WorkloadBounds <= 3 concurrent alarms + <= 3 min active control per hour
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Human Factors Inspector — aspirational full authority restoration
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 100 is theoretically aspirational but unreachable given swarm operational complexity. Swarms inherently generate multiple concurrent alerts. A better target is 90-95 with defined acceptable alert protocols. Flag as unreachable and recommend Goal = 85 as achievable ceiling.

targets.conditions: >= 99.95% of simulated off-nominal scenarios trigger independent flight termination and execute within <= 10 seconds to safe configuration (motors off, drift ceased, no secondary hazards)

increments.milestone: by end Phase 3 (VLOS swarm termination validation)

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011) — contingency mitigation; Public Below — uncontrolled descent prevention
targets.reasoning: Wish = 99.95% termination reliability is the baseline target and aligns with industry practice. Current 99.1% status requires modest redundancy and test expansion to close 0.85% gap.

targets.conditions: >= 99.99% of >= 100 simulated scenarios with dual-redundant termination systems, complete execution within <= 10 seconds, and documented post-termination actions (crew alert, ATSU notification, data preservation, recovery plan)
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011) — committed termination standard; CAA Flight Safety Investigator (REQ012) — data preservation post-termination
targets.reasoning: Goal = 99.99% (four nines) by end 2027 is the team-committed termination standard. Requires dual-redundant logic, comprehensive testing, and integration with MOR/ATSU protocols. Achievable through proven redundancy approaches.

targets.conditions: >= 99.999% (five nines) with triple-redundant systems, provably correct termination logic (formal verification), and environmental robustness across ODD extremes
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector — aspirational ultra-high reliability
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 99.999% (five nines) requires formal verification—a significant investment. Feasibility is uncertain because formal termination proof is not standard practice in aerospace.

targets.conditions: >= 95% of incident events with complete state variable logging (position, velocity, heading, engine status, autonomy mode, sensor inputs, crew inputs, alerts) sampled >= 10Hz, no gaps > 100ms, data integrity verified
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Flight Safety Investigator (REQ012) — MOR investigation support; Applicant — data preservation cost
targets.reasoning: Wish = 95% completeness reflects achievable improvement from current 88% through logging architecture refinement and redundant storage. Supports MOR investigation efficiency.

targets.conditions: 100% of incident events with complete state variable logging, >= 10Hz sampling (>= 50Hz for dynamics), zero gaps > 100ms, cryptographic hash + timestamp certification for each data file, and retention through full MOR investigation
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Flight Safety Investigator (REQ012) — committed forensic evidence standard; CAA Data Protection Officer — DPIA compliance
targets.reasoning: Goal = 100% by end 2027 is the team-committed forensic completeness standard. Requires redundant logging systems, provenance certification, and retention protocol integration. Achievable with modern flight data recorder technology.

targets.conditions: 100% of incidents with cross-verified forensic evidence (independent logging systems reconciled), automated anomaly-detection flagging of out-of-nominal data, and blockchain-certified provenance chain for chain-of-custody assurance
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Flight Safety Investigator — aspirational forensic confidence; Insurance underwriters — loss-investigation support
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 100% with cross-verification and blockchain provenance. Feasibility is uncertain because blockchain integration for forensic evidence is emerging practice in aerospace.

targets.conditions: >= 4 competencies per crew member: CAA UAS operator certificate, aircraft type rating, operational endorsement (BVLOS or swarm ops), and emergency-procedure certification
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Licensing Inspector (REQ014) — crew fitness; Applicant — training cost optimization
targets.reasoning: Wish = 4 competencies reflects achievable crew training maturation with swarm-operations endorsement adoption. Modest gap from Status.

targets.conditions: 5 competencies per crew member: full CAA licensing (operator + type + swarm endorsement), role-specific training (documented pass/fail), >= 90% simulator emergency-procedure success, >= 50 flight hours (pilot) / >= 20 hours (sensor op), and proficiency check within 12 months
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Licensing Inspector (REQ014) — committed crew competency standard; CAA Human Factors Inspector (REQ010) — workload fitness
targets.reasoning: Goal = 5 competencies by end 2027 is the team-committed crew competency standard. Requires swarm-operations endorsement curriculum maturity, standardized training, and simulator validation. Achievable within 24 months.

targets.conditions: 6 competencies: full certification, role-specific training (>= 90% pass), >= 95% simulator proficiency, >= 100 flight hours pilot / >= 50 sensor-op hours, annual proficiency check + advanced swarm-tactics endorsement
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Licensing Inspector — aspirational crew excellence; Military operators — high-autonomy swarm command
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 6 competencies with advanced swarm tactics. Achievable by year 1 steady-state with continued training investment and flight experience accumulation.

targets.conditions: >= 85% of safety-critical hazards (from SORA) and design/operational domains assigned to CAA or MAA with no unassigned gaps; overlap conflicts identified

increments.milestone: by end Phase 2 (apportionment alignment with SORA)

targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA/MAA Liaison (REQ015) — regulatory clarity; Applicant — unambiguous responsibility assignment
targets.reasoning: Wish = 85% reflects achievable improvement from Status 80% through clearer CAA/MAA role definitions for novel domains (swarm coordination, emergent behavior, ML assurance).

targets.conditions: 100% of SORA hazards, design domains, and operational domains assigned to exactly one regulatory body (CAA or MAA) with signed apportionment matrix, zero gaps, zero overlaps
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA/MAA Liaison (REQ015) — committed gap-free apportionment; Applicant — regulatory predictability
targets.reasoning: Goal = 100% by end 2027 is the team-committed apportionment standard. Requires joint CAA/MAA governance process to address novel domains. Stretch feasibility due to multi-stakeholder coordination complexity.

targets.conditions: 100% gap-free signed apportionment PLUS interoperability schema for coalition swarm variants (NATO/DCDC alignment) and automated apportionment audit (conflict detection via ML)
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA/MAA Liaison — aspirational coalition readiness; Ministry of Defence — multi-national interop
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 100% with NATO interop schema. Feasibility is unreachable because coalition interoperability is outside CAA/MAA scope and requires MOD/DCDC alignment. Recommend revising Goal to 100% national apportionment as the realistic ceiling.

targets.conditions: >= 4 distinct trial phases (ground test, VLOS single, VLOS multi, BVLOS nominal) each with explicit progression criteria and documented exit procedures if new risk evidence emerges
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Programme Manager (REQ016) — predictable trial progression; Applicant — unambiguous phase criteria
targets.reasoning: Wish = 4 gates reflects achievable expansion from Status 2.5 through systematic phase definition and reversibility procedure documentation.

targets.conditions: >= 5 phases (ground test, VLOS-single, VLOS-multi, BVLOS-nominal, BVLOS-swarm, autonomous-decision) with quantified criteria, reversibility procedures tested on each gate, and all evidence immutably logged
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Programme Manager (REQ016) — committed reversible-gate standard; CAA Flight Safety Investigator (REQ012) — data immutability
targets.reasoning: Goal = 5 gates by end 2027 is the team-committed phased-trial standard. Requires systematic gate design, reversibility procedure validation, and data governance. Stretch feasibility due to novel regulatory approach (traditional aerospace is forward-only).

targets.unit: count of gates with rollback budget <= 1 hour
targets.conditions: 6 phases with rollback procedures executable within 1-hour time budget (data revert, crew retraining, vehicle reconfiguration), automated data provenance verification, and simulation-based gate-reversal risk assessment
targets.stakeholder_driver: CAA Programme Manager — aspirational trial agility
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 6 gates with 1-hour rollback time budget. Feasibility is uncertain because operationalizing rapid phase reversals at scale is untested in aerospace regulation.

targets.conditions: >= 3 stakeholder groups engaged (local residents, aviation community, environmental) with >= 30-day prior notification before first flight
targets.stakeholder_driver: Applicant — social-license cost; CAA / Local Authority — CAP 1616 compliance (implicit)
targets.reasoning: Wish = 3 stakeholder groups reflects achievable CAP 1616 baseline engagement. Current Status 2 (aviation + authorities) is insufficient; residents and environmental groups are vocal about BVLOS trials.

targets.conditions: >= 5 stakeholder groups (residents, landowners, environmental, aviation, media) with prior notification >= 60 days, documented feedback collection (survey, workshop, forum), and documented response to all feedback (acceptance, mitigation, or justified rejection)

increments.milestone: by Submission Readiness Check (6 months pre-authorization)

targets.stakeholder_driver: Applicant — social-license assurance; CAA — CAP 1616 full compliance; Local Authority — community relations
targets.reasoning: Goal = 5 groups by end 2027 is the team-committed public engagement standard. Requires structured community consultation, feedback mechanisms, and transparent response protocols. Stretch feasibility because REQ017 has NO USER STORY and is absent from regulatory guidance.

targets.unit: count of groups with published feedback dashboard
targets.conditions: 6 stakeholder groups engaged + CAP 1616 + media engagement + independent community advocate + published consultation dashboard (feedback themes, applicant responses, residual concerns, dispute resolution mechanism)
targets.stakeholder_driver: Applicant — exceptional social-license strength; CAA — transparency precedent; Civil society — democratic accountability
targets.reasoning: Stretch = 6 groups + published transparency dashboard. Feasibility is uncertain because publishing feedback/disputes may invite legal challenge or Freedom of Information requests for applicant-sensitive data.

background_specs.motivation: Complete submission packs enable efficient case opening and reduce rework cycles. Incomplete packs block downstream review (SORA, safety case, crew training) and delay authorization decisions. Operator throughput depends on intake quality.

risks.risk: Operators submit incomplete packs to test regulator's expectations, triggering rejection and resubmission cycles
risks.mitigation: Publish prescriptive document checklist (per operation type) in CAA guidance before intake. Implement Quick Gate automated detector (METER-001) to flag gaps within 10 minutes of submission. Offer pre-submission consultation.

risks.risk: Swarm operations require novel documents (swarm decision-making architecture, emergent-behavior manifest) not yet standardized
risks.mitigation: CAA publishes swarm-specific document templates and examples (AMLAS case studies) 6 months before accepting first swarm packs. Provide operator guidance webinars.

risks.risk: Mandatory document list evolves as regulations mature (e.g., new ML explainability template), invalidating previously-accepted packs
risks.mitigation: Grandfather existing cases; grandfather-plus-6-month transition window for new documents. Use phased gate structure (PhasedGateReversibility) to allow evidence re-evaluation without full resubmission.

background_specs.value_of_value: Gate all other work. If pack is incomplete, no downstream review can proceed. Business impact: operator frustration, extended authorization timelines, reduced trial throughput. Regulatory impact: integrity of case file depends on complete evidence.
background_specs.strategic_alignment: Supports MAA/CAA Protector RG Mk1 transition by establishing consistent application intake process. Feeds BVLOS/urban-logistics expansion (CAA priority) by removing intake bottleneck.
background_specs.reasoning: PackCompleteness is the first gate in the authorization flow. Status (72%) vs Tolerable (100%) indicates 28% first-submission failure rate, directly correlating to operator rework cycles and CAA case officer workload. This is a foundational problem: poor intake efficiency cascades to all downstream activities.

background_specs.authority: CAA SORA Assessment Authority (aligned with JARUS SORA v2.5 working group)
background_specs.motivation: Swarm-aware SORA evidence documents coordinated-failure modes (loss-of-separation cascades, emergent misbehavior) that single-aircraft SORA misses entirely. Without swarm-specific risk pathways, SAIL and OSO determinations are defensibly unsound and authorization is at risk.

risks.risk: JARUS SORA v2.5 swarm guidance is recent (2025); operators and CAA assessors lack familiarity with swarm-specific risk taxonomy
risks.mitigation: CAA publishes exemplary swarm SORA evidence packages (draw from AMLAS case studies). Conduct assessor training workshops (2-3 day SORA v2.5 swarm module). Reference EUROCAE WG6 swarm guidance.

risks.risk: Operators claim swarm-aware SORA evidence without substantive content (e.g., 'loss-of-separation controlled via inter-vehicle link' without specifying detection latency or failsafe mechanism)
risks.mitigation: Ray's METER-003 (Swarm-Specific Risk Evidence Inventory Audit) defines operationally what counts as swarm-aware: >= 3 distinct items per aircraft with documented evidence (test report, design review, simulation data). Audit enforces specificity.

risks.risk: Status (15% of submissions) far below Ideal (80%); implies most current operators have not adopted swarm-aware SORA at all
risks.mitigation: AMLAS/SACE pilots develop swarm SORA templates and case studies (2025-2026). CAA publishes monthly SORA assessment updates as feedback loop: what swarm-aware evidence impressed assessors becomes public guidance.

background_specs.value_of_value: Safety-critical. Swarm-specific failure modes (coordinated link loss, formation instability, emergent decision-making cascades) can trigger loss-of-separation outcomes that single-aircraft hazard trees never predict. If SORA lacks swarm-aware risk pathways, SAIL determination becomes a guess, not defensible regulation.
background_specs.strategic_alignment: Directly enables MAA/CAA BVLOS swarm authorization pathway (Protector RG Mk1 early work, GCAP platform trials). Supports AMLAS/SACE learning dissemination. Prerequisite for urban-logistics swarms and Project Alvina attritable-mass experimentation.
background_specs.reasoning: Status (15%) vs Tolerable (3 items per aircraft) shows widespread adoption gap. Swarm regulations are nascent; operators are still learning what swarm-aware risk assessment looks like. High improvement rate (10-15% quarterly) suggests AMLAS/SACE learning is accelerating adoption, but baseline is dangerously low.

background_specs.owner: CAA safety-case reviewer + applicant ConOps author
background_specs.authority: CAA Innovation Hub (ConOps guidance); CAA case officer (review gate)
background_specs.motivation: ConOps clarity determines whether crew training is well-grounded and safety case hazard analysis is defensible. Vague ConOps (e.g., 'swarm will maintain formation' without specifying tolerance or recovery procedure) leads to crew confusion and unanalyzed failure modes.

risks.risk: Status (55%) far below Tolerable (85%); indicates ConOps documents define nominal behavior but omit worst-credible boundaries
risks.mitigation: Publish ConOps template with mandatory sections: nominal behavior (quantified parameters), worst-credible boundary (explicit limits for each operational phase). Use inter-rater reliability test (Ray's METER-004, kappa >= 0.8) as gate.

risks.risk: Swarm operations add complexity: ConOps must describe formation geometry, inter-aircraft separation, swarm decision logic, and coordinated failure modes—most operators lack framework for this
risks.mitigation: Publish exemplary swarm ConOps (draw from AMLAS case studies). Require swarm-specific sections: (1) nominal formation geometry and tolerance, (2) inter-vehicle link loss behavior, (3) collective decision-making (consensus, voting, leader-follower).

risks.risk: Reviewers interpret ConOps differently; two independent reviewers score clarity inconsistently
risks.mitigation: Ray's METER-004 operationalizes this: dual-reviewer test with structured questionnaire and kappa >= 0.8 target. If reviewers disagree, ConOps is sent back for clarification.

background_specs.value_of_value: Crew training and investigability depend on clear ConOps. If worst-credible scenarios are undefined, crew procedures are ad-hoc and incident investigation is blocked (post-incident: 'how was the aircraft supposed to behave in this state?'). Regulatory impact: ambiguous ConOps triggers authorization refusal.
background_specs.strategic_alignment: Supports CAA priority (BVLOS expansion, urban-logistics) by establishing clear ConOps review process. Enables crew competency assessment (REQ014, CrewCompetencyEvidence) by grounding training on explicit operational boundaries.
background_specs.keystone_reference: K3 (operational clarity for crew training and hazard analysis)
background_specs.reasoning: Current Status (55%) reflects widespread gap in operational boundary definition. Swarm operations are nascent; most ConOps documents treat swarm formation as a simple waypoint list rather than a dynamic, emergent system with explicit worst-credible limits. Trend is improving slowly (3-5% per year).

background_specs.authority: CAA airspace change team (trial segregation approval)
background_specs.motivation: Geofence is the automated last line of defense against uncontrolled BVLOS flight. Single-point-failure in geofence enforcement can release aircraft into uncontrolled airspace, creating public-below and third-party safety hazard. Keystone K1 safety mechanism.

risks.risk: Monte Carlo simulation (METER-005) assumes benign GPS error model; real environment may have multipath, spoofing, or jamming outside ODD bounds
risks.mitigation: Expand Monte Carlo environmental ODD to include GPS denial (up to 60s), GPS accuracy degradation (up to 50m CEP), electromagnetic interference. Conduct live fly-away test (METER-006) in representative RF environment.

risks.risk: Geofence logic shares compute platform with autonomy logic; failure in autonomy (e.g., control loop instability) could corrupt geofence state
risks.mitigation: Require independent termination system (IndependentFlightTermination) on separate hardware path. Geofence enforcement must not depend on main autonomy processor. Use watchdog timer as secondary containment mechanism.

risks.risk: Status (99.6%) nearly meets Tolerable (99.5%); but Status may be measured in benign simulation conditions, not representative of real swarm operations
risks.mitigation: Conduct METER-006 live fly-away test with entire swarm (not single aircraft), evaluating formation integrity during geofence enforcement. Multi-aircraft interactions (e.g., formation flutter) may expose geofence assumptions.

risks.risk: Gap analysis: Tolerable (99.5%) vs Survival (99.0%) is only 0.5%; implies very narrow regulatory margin if geofence performance degrades after authorization
risks.mitigation: Establish continuous post-flight monitoring (METER-010): each flight accumulates real-world geofence performance data. If trend moves below 99.5%, trial pauses pending investigation.

background_specs.value_of_value: Safety-critical. Uncontrolled BVLOS beyond geofence could result in collision with third-party aircraft, property damage, or public injury. Regulatory impact: failure on this value means authorization refusal. No geofence evidence = no BVLOS approval.
background_specs.strategic_alignment: Prerequisite for MAA/CAA BVLOS authorization (Protector RG Mk1 trials, GCAP platform early work). Enables Project Alvina attritable-mass experimentation in segregated airspace. CAA BVLOS expansion and urban-logistics depend on reliable geofence assurance.
background_specs.keystone_reference: K1 (automated containment; safety-critical single-point failure mitigation)
background_specs.reasoning: GeofenceContainment is the most-mature safety technology in this set. Status (99.6%) is already above Tolerable (99.5%), indicating technology maturity. Risk is primarily in simulation-to-reality gap and multi-aircraft interactions. Trend is stable (0.1% per year improvement), reflecting engineering plateau.

background_specs.owner: Applicant (C2 system designer) + CAA spectrum specialist
background_specs.motivation: C2 link loss is inevitable in RF-contested environments. Autonomous survival time is the grace period for crew detection and RTL trigger. Without documented survival time (>= 30 seconds), BVLOS authorization cannot be defended if link loss occurs.

risks.risk: Status (45 seconds autonomous survival) meets Alexa's minimum (>= 30s) but differs from Isaac's Tolerable constraint on link availability (99%). Conflation of two metrics: survival time vs. availability.
risks.mitigation: Ray's METER-007 measures both: autonomous survival time AND link availability. Clarify: survival time = duration after link loss before unsafe state; availability = percentage of mission time link is operational. Both must pass.

risks.risk: Swarms compound risk: if lead aircraft loses link, followers may lose inter-vehicle link (RF interference, network topology collapse). Cascading link loss can trigger uncontrolled formation breakup.
risks.mitigation: Define swarm-specific C2 resilience: (1) individual aircraft survival after main C2 loss, (2) swarm cohesion during inter-vehicle link loss (maintenance of separation). Test both independently (METER-007).

risks.risk: RF environment during trial may not match ODD assumptions (e.g., cellular tower interference, tactical RF testing nearby). Link resilience test may be invalidated by environment change.
risks.mitigation: Conduct RF environment survey pre-trial (spectrum monitoring, interference characterization). Update METER-007 assumptions based on survey. Re-test if RF environment changes materially.

background_specs.value_of_value: Enables BVLOS operations. Without documented C2 resilience, crew cannot regain control after link loss and aircraft is uncontrollable. Regulatory impact: insufficient survival time means authorization refusal or VLOS-only constraint.
background_specs.strategic_alignment: Supports CAA BVLOS expansion and urban-logistics (priority). Prerequisite for Project Alvina attritable-mass swarm trials (resilience to communication degradation in contested RF environment).
background_specs.reasoning: Status (45 seconds) vs Tolerable (99% availability) are different metrics; confusion here is a major risk. Current industry practice measures survival time; Isaac's constraint emphasizes link availability as primary metric. Need clarity on which constraint dominates.

background_specs.owner: Applicant (DAA algorithm developer) + CAA DAA assessor
background_specs.authority: CAA flight safety assessment team (aligned with DO-365 / F3442 certification body)
background_specs.motivation: DAA system resolves encounters with cooperative and non-cooperative aircraft. Failure results in mid-air collision. DO-365 is the regulatory standard; swarms must demonstrate consistent, predictable resolution across cooperative and non-cooperative encounter geometries.

risks.risk: Status (88%) far below Tolerable (99.5%) and Regulatory (99.9%); indicates significant gap in non-cooperative encounter resolution
risks.mitigation: Invest in non-cooperative radar detection algorithm development and testing. Ray's METER-008 runs 100+ encounter scenarios; analyze failures and iterate radar/decision logic. Target >= 99% before authorization.

risks.risk: Swarm coordination during encounter is rarely tested (sub-scale DecisionConsistency); decision consistency may fail if swarm formation state affects encounter resolution logic
risks.mitigation: Extend METER-008 to include swarm-formation-aware encounters: e.g., formation tight (3m separation) vs. loose (50m separation). DAA logic must resolve encounters consistently regardless of formation geometry.

risks.risk: DO-365 certification assumes single-aircraft DAA; multi-aircraft coordinated resolution is not yet in standard. Swarms may trigger resolution maneuvers that interfere with each other.
risks.mitigation: Define swarm DAA protocol: (1) lead aircraft triggers resolution, (2) followers execute coordinated maneuver (offset formation, altitude change). Test coordinated resolution in METER-008 subset.

background_specs.value_of_value: Safety-critical. DAA failure results in collision with third-party aircraft (manned or unmanned). Regulatory impact: failure on DAA means authorization refusal for any airspace with other air traffic. Status (88%) far below Tolerable (99.5%) indicates significant development work required.
background_specs.strategic_alignment: Prerequisite for CAA BVLOS expansion into Class G airspace with civilian air traffic. Enables MAA/CAA trials in airspace shared with military training routes. Required for urban-logistics scenarios with helicopter traffic.
background_specs.reasoning: DAA is a mature domain (DO-365 standard exists) but swarm adaptation is nascent. Status gap (88% vs 99.5%) reflects engineering challenge of non-cooperative detection in high-clutter environments and multi-aircraft decision coordination. Trend is improving slowly (2-4% per year), constrained by algorithmic limits and sensor physics.

background_specs.authority: CAA innovation / autonomy assurance team (emerging role; no precedent yet)
background_specs.motivation: Swarm behavior emerges from interaction of individual control laws; emergent behavior can violate safety invariants unexpectedly. Documentation of emergent behaviors and quantified boundaries is essential to assure swarms are controllable within operational envelope.

risks.risk: Status (3 behaviors identified) far below Tolerable (>= 95% flight hours within bounds); implies emergent behaviors are not yet systematically identified or bounded
risks.mitigation: METER-009 (Emergent Behavior Boundary Documentation Audit) requires operator to identify >= 5 distinct emergent behaviors (formation, coordination latency, flocking, decision deadlock, split/rejoin). METER-010 validates each is within bounds in >= 95% of flight hours.

risks.risk: Emergent behavior taxonomy is not standardized; operators may identify different behaviors or miss critical ones. No accepted list of 'expected swarm behaviors'.
risks.mitigation: EUROCAE WG6 is developing swarm-behavior taxonomy (target: 2026 guidance). AMLAS case studies establish accepted behaviors. Publish monthly CAA guidance update on emergent-behavior identification.

risks.risk: Survival threshold (Isaac: 90% flight hours within bounds) is lower than Tolerable (95%); if swarm operates outside bounds in > 10% of flights, does trial pause or continue?
risks.mitigation: Clarify policy: if any single emergent behavior exceeds bounds in > 5% of flights, trial pauses pending investigation. Operator re-tightens boundary or demonstrates bounds are incorrect and updates manifest. Re-test required.

background_specs.value_of_value: Swarm assurance. If emergent behaviors are undocumented and unbounded, swarms can exhibit runtime surprises (unintended formation geometry changes, oscillation, consensus deadlock) that violate safety constraints. Regulatory impact: inability to explain swarm behavior blocks authorization.
background_specs.strategic_alignment: Prerequisite for autonomous swarm authorization (MAA/CAA trials). Supports Project Alvina attritable-mass experimentation (emergent behavior in large swarms is critical challenge). Enables GCAP platform early autonomy work.
background_specs.reasoning: SwarmEmergentBoundedness is the most-nascent value in this set. Status (3 behaviors) vs Tolerable (95% flight hours in bounds) are measuring different things: the count of behaviors identified vs. the test coverage of those behaviors. This confusion reflects that swarm assurance is still being defined. Large gap (Status far below Tolerable) and improving trend (1-2 behaviors per year) indicate active learning.

background_specs.owner: Applicant (ML model developer) + CAA AI/ML assurance specialist + AAIB (investigative interest)
background_specs.authority: CAA AI/ML assurance authority (emerging; parallels AMLAS/SACE work)
background_specs.motivation: ML-based autonomy decision-making (mode selection, emergency response, formation adjustment) must be forensically explainable for post-incident investigation. Black-box neural networks defeat AAIB investigative capacity and block authorization based on AI-safety grounds.

risks.risk: Status (28%) far below Tolerable (80%) and dangerously close to Survival (70%); indicates widespread lack of ML explainability instrumentation
risks.mitigation: AMLAS guidance mandates explainability approach by model type: decision trees (100%), SVMs (feature importance + boundary visualization), neural networks (LIME/SHAP saliency maps). Ray's METER-011 tests reconstruction by independent reviewer (>= 80% target).

risks.risk: Neural networks (high performance) are inherently opaque; SHAP/LIME explanations are proxy approximations, not ground truth. AAIB may reject proxy explanations as insufficient for accident investigation.
risks.mitigation: Recommend decision-tree or SVM models for safety-critical decisions (mode selection, termination trigger). Reserve neural networks for non-critical optimization (swarm formation smoothing). AMLAS/SACE case studies establish acceptable proxy explanations.

risks.risk: Gap from Status (28%) to Survival (70%) is only 42%; system could enter unassurable state very quickly if additional ML components are added
risks.mitigation: Freeze model development after authorization gate. All ML components must pass explainability test (METER-011) before integration. Post-deployment model updates require re-certification.

background_specs.value_of_value: Forensic integrity and public trust. If ML decisions cannot be explained post-incident, investigation is blocked and public confidence in autonomous swarms is destroyed. Regulatory impact: failure on this value means AAIB refusal to investigate, which blocks authorization on AI-safety grounds (links to JSP 936 Part 1 RAISO accountability).
background_specs.strategic_alignment: Links to JSP 936 Part 1 (RAISO accountability for autonomy). Supports MAA interest in Project Alvina (attritable swarms with autonomy). Enables early GCAP autonomy work by establishing explainability baseline.
background_specs.keystone_reference: K7 (ML forensic explainability for post-incident investigation)
background_specs.reasoning: MLForensicExplainability is the single-largest gap in this analysis: 62% gap from Status (28%) to Ideal (90%). This reflects the fundamental tension between model performance (neural networks are fast) and explainability (neural networks are opaque). Gap closure depends on industry adoption of explainability tooling (LIME/SHAP) and regulatory acceptance of proxy explanations.

background_specs.owner: Applicant (HMI designer) + CAA human factors inspector + DDH (legal/ethical implications)
background_specs.authority: CAA human factors and innovation teams; DDH policy guidance on AI oversight
background_specs.motivation: Meaningful human control ensures crew retains authority over safety-critical decisions and can intervene if autonomy fails. Without demonstrated MHC, authorization may be refused on legal/ethical grounds (AI accountability, responsibility assignment).

risks.risk: Status (52) far below Tolerable (70); largest deficit is WorkloadBounds (35 vs 100). Swarm operations generate multiple concurrent events (engine failure, loss-of-link, emergent-behavior anomaly) that overwhelm crew attention.
risks.mitigation: Redesign HMI: (1) Auto-triage alerts (only 1-2 concurrent maximum displayed), (2) Progressive disclosure (alert details on demand), (3) Swarm-level abstraction (display formation state, not per-aircraft state). Ray's METER-013 validates workload in simulation.

risks.risk: TakeoverLatency (time available to regain control) is < 5 seconds in fast-dynamics scenarios (DAA encounter, geofence breach). Crew reaction time is 1-2 seconds; margin is very thin.
risks.mitigation: Implement pre-positioned crew takeover: (1) in encounter scenarios, system proposes resolution maneuver, crew accepts/rejects (0.5s approval cycle), (2) in geofence breach, automatic RTL is armed but crew can override if they recognize nuisance trigger.

risks.risk: Authority preservation for swarm operations is undefined: if one swarm member is incapacitated, does crew manually re-route formation or does autonomy replan? Who decides?
risks.mitigation: Define decision boundary: (1) autonomy replans formation automatically if single member incapacitates, (2) crew retains override (manual re-route), (3) document in ConOps. Train crew on replan scenarios.

background_specs.value_of_value: Legal, ethical, and regulatory. Meaningful human control prevents authorization refusal on AI-safety grounds. Demonstrates crew retain operational authority, satisfying DDH policy (preventing unaccountable autonomous systems). Business impact: failure on MHC can trigger regulatory or public challenge to authorization.
background_specs.strategic_alignment: Links to DDH AI oversight policy and JSP 936 Part 1 (RAISO MHC accountability). Supports CAA BVLOS expansion by assuring public that humans retain control. Prerequisite for urban-logistics with mixed autonomy/crew operations.
background_specs.reasoning: Status (52) vs Tolerable (70) gap of 18 points is significant; swarm workload is the bottleneck. Trend is improving slowly (5-8 points per year) as HMI design matures. Risk is high if crew cannot keep up with swarm events; uncontrolled escalation spiral is possible.

background_specs.owner: Applicant (termination system hardware/firmware designer) + CAA flight-ops inspector
background_specs.authority: CAA flight operations and safety assurance team
background_specs.motivation: Independent flight termination is the last-resort safety mechanism. If autonomy or C2 fails, independent termination must fire to prevent uncontrolled descent. Single-point-failure in termination is catastrophic.

risks.risk: Termination system shares compute platform with main avionics; fault in avionics could corrupt termination state before trigger fires
risks.mitigation: Require independent termination hardware on separate processor, separate power supply, separate trigger logic. Ray's METER-014 (FMEA-derived fault injection) validates termination reliability across 50+ failure scenarios.

risks.risk: Swarm termination is coordinated: if one aircraft terminates while others continue, mid-air collision risk during descent. Termination must be synchronized across swarm.
risks.mitigation: Define termination protocol: (1) trigger fires on one aircraft, (2) broadcast termination command to swarm via inter-vehicle link, (3) all aircraft execute synchronized descent with separation maintained. Test in METER-014.

risks.risk: Status (99.1%) below Tolerable (99.9%) and above Survival (99%); narrow margin for improvement. Further reliability gains require redundancy (expensive, heavy).
risks.mitigation: Maintain current testing regime; redundancy investment justified only if trend moves below Tolerable. Continuous post-flight monitoring (like GeofenceContainment) tracks real-world termination reliability.

background_specs.value_of_value: Safety-critical last-resort mechanism. If termination fails, uncontrolled flight and possible collision/injury. Regulatory impact: failure on this value means authorization refusal. Non-negotiable for BVLOS.
background_specs.strategic_alignment: Prerequisite for all BVLOS authorization (MAA/CAA trials, Project Alvina, GCAP). Supports Protector RG Mk1 transition by establishing termination assurance baseline.
background_specs.reasoning: IndependentFlightTermination is a mature safety technology (98-99% effectiveness). Status (99.1%) is already above Tolerable (99.9%); trend is stable (0.5% per year), reflecting diminishing returns on reliability investment. Risk is engineering (synchronization across swarms) rather than technology maturity.

background_specs.owner: CAA flight safety investigator (MOR handler) + applicant (data logger owner)
background_specs.authority: CAA flight safety investigation authority; AAIB (investigative interest)
background_specs.motivation: Mandatory Occurrence Reporting (MOR) regulations require complete flight data for post-incident investigation. Data gaps defeat AAIB investigation and block learning from incidents. Forensic provenance ensures data integrity is defensible in incident proceedings.

risks.risk: Status (88%) below Tolerable (100%); 12% of incidents have data gaps that may compromise investigation
risks.mitigation: Ray's METER-015 (Data Recording Completeness & Integrity Audit) pre-flight checks all logging systems, post-flight validates data integrity (no gaps > 100ms). Redundant logging systems reduce gap probability.

risks.risk: Swarm logging multiplies data volume: 5-aircraft swarm at 10Hz requires ~100+ state variables, generating gigabytes per flight hour. Storage and transmission capacity may be insufficient.
risks.mitigation: Define compressible data schema: (1) full-resolution logging for safety-critical variables (position, velocity, autonomy mode), (2) aggregated logging for non-critical variables (sensor calibration, motor RPM). Compress post-flight for archive.

risks.risk: Forensic provenance (cryptographic hash, timestamp certification) is not yet standardized for UAS. What counts as proof of data integrity?
risks.mitigation: EUROCAE WG6 may develop guidance (check status). Interim: use industry-standard practices (SHA-256 hashing, atomic clock timestamps, digitally signed metadata). METER-015 verifies provenance presence.

background_specs.value_of_value: Investigability and regulatory compliance. If MOR obligations are not met, CAA cannot defend authorization decisions post-incident. Business impact: incident without adequate data leads to broad authorization suspension pending investigation.
background_specs.strategic_alignment: Mandatory regulatory obligation (CAA Occurrence Reporting Regulations). Supports AAIB investigative capacity (links to JSP 936 Part 1). Prerequisite for any operational trial.
background_specs.keystone_reference: K9 (forensic data preservation for incident investigation)
background_specs.reasoning: MORForensicCompleteness is measured (88% baseline) and relatively mature. Status vs Tolerable gap (12%) is achievable with redundant logging infrastructure. Risk is primarily data-volume management for swarms and standardization of forensic provenance.

background_specs.owner: Applicant (crew training organization) + CAA licensing inspector
background_specs.authority: CAA licensing and training authority (Part-FCL alignment)
background_specs.motivation: Crew must demonstrate competency across multiple dimensions: certification, training, simulation proficiency, and operational experience. Without documented evidence, pilot-in-charge integrity is unverified and crew cannot legally operate.

risks.risk: Status (3.5 competencies) below Tolerable (5); swarm-operations endorsement is nascent and not yet widely deployed
risks.mitigation: CAA/JARUS publishes swarm-operations endorsement training curriculum (timeline: 2026). Pilot training providers offer endorsement courses. Grandfather early pilots under alternative competency pathways (e.g., AMLAS trial participation = swarm-ops endorsement equivalent).

risks.risk: Emergency-procedure simulation proficiency (>= 90% success rate) is rarely systematically measured. What counts as passing a simulation?
risks.mitigation: Publish standard emergency procedures: link loss recovery, geofence breach response, DAA encounter handling, swarm member incapacitation. Ray's METER-016 validates >= 90% success in simulation before authorization.

risks.risk: Operational experience requirement (>= 50 hours for pilot, >= 20 for sensor operator) may be impossible to meet for novel operation types (swarm formation control, multi-aircraft DAA)
risks.mitigation: Allow simulator hours to count toward experience requirement (e.g., 10 hours simulator = 10 hours operational experience). AMLAS trials can serve as operational experience pathway for early operators.

background_specs.value_of_value: Baseline crew qualification. If crew lacks demonstrated competency, incident risk increases dramatically. Regulatory impact: failure on crew competency is grounds for authorization refusal or crew restriction (specific pilot qualified, not generic operator license).
background_specs.strategic_alignment: Supports CAA licensing authority and crew training standardization. Enables swarm-operations endorsement adoption. Prerequisites for BVLOS and urban-logistics crew training.
background_specs.reasoning: CrewCompetencyEvidence is a measured value (3.5 competencies) with clear improvement pathway (swarm-operations endorsement adoption). Status vs Tolerable gap (1.5) is achievable with training curriculum development. Risk is primarily in training standardization and endorsement definition.

background_specs.authority: CAA Innovation Hub + MAA RPAS Regulatory Branch (joint authority)
background_specs.motivation: Civil/military regulatory boundary must be clearly defined. Ambiguous responsibility assignments (swarm-specific SORA assessment: CAA or MAA? ML explainability: CAA or RAISO?) create regulatory risk for operators and delay authorization decisions.

risks.risk: Status (80%) vs Tolerable (100%); 20% of responsibility assignments are ambiguous or overlapping, particularly for novel domains (swarm coordination, ML explainability)
risks.mitigation: CAA/MAA joint task force publishes apportionment guidance for swarm-specific domains: (1) swarm-aware SORA assessment = CAA, (2) emergent-behavior assurance = joint CAA/autonomy-reviewer, (3) ML explainability = CAA + AAIB interest (not MAA unless classified). Use METER-017 to verify gap-free apportionment before case opening.

risks.risk: Security classification handling (REQ018) is completely absent from apportionment matrices. If trial involves classified MOD work, how is CAA presented with evidence without compromise?
risks.mitigation: CAA/MAA establish classified-apportionment process: (1) sanitized summary suitable for CAA review, (2) classified annex retained by MAA, (3) joint CAA/MAA panel reviews classified evidence in secure facility. Not resolved in current Background scope.

risks.risk: Apportionment boundaries may shift during trial (new risk discovered, new regulations published). Frozen apportionment may become stale.
risks.mitigation: PhasedGateReversibility allows apportionment re-evaluation at phase gates. Maintain apportionment matrix as living document; CAA/MAA approve updates before implementation.

background_specs.value_of_value: Regulatory clarity and operational authorization. Ambiguous responsibility blocks authorization (operator doesn't know who to answer to). Business impact: delayed authorization, scope creep as operators pursue dual-path approvals (CAA and MAA both).
background_specs.strategic_alignment: Supports MAA/CAA joint governance (MOD/civilian interface). Prerequisite for classified military trials (Protector RG Mk1, GCAP early airframe work) and civilian-military mixed operations (Project Alvina).
background_specs.reasoning: CAAMAAApportionmentGapFreeness is a governance issue, not a technical value. Status (80%) vs Tolerable (100%) gap reflects absence of CAA/MAA guidance on novel domains (swarm-specific, ML assurance). Gap closure depends on CAA/MAA collaboration, not operator action. High priority for authorization pathway clarity.

background_specs.owner: CAA programme manager (trial governance) + applicant (phased trial execution)
background_specs.motivation: Phased trial gates allow progressive evidence accumulation and rapid learning cycles. Reversibility ensures unexpected findings (emergent-behavior anomaly, higher-than-expected failure rate) trigger phase reversal, not program cancellation, enabling safe innovation.

risks.risk: Status (2.5 well-defined gates) far below Tolerable (>= 4 gates); most trials have forward-only progression (pass/fail gates, not reversible)
risks.mitigation: Publish phased-trial template: Phase 1 (ground), Phase 2 (VLOS single), Phase 3 (VLOS swarm), Phase 4 (BVLOS nominal), Phase 5 (BVLOS swarm). Ray's METER-018 validates reversibility procedures for each gate.

risks.risk: Reversibility procedures are undefined: if Phase 3 discovers emergent-behavior anomaly, what triggers reversal? Criteria for re-entry? Timeline for evidence refresh?
risks.mitigation: METER-018 requires explicit gate specification: (1) progression criterion (e.g., 10 successful flights, zero emergent-behavior exceedances), (2) exit criterion (e.g., 1 out-of-bounds event detected), (3) re-entry criteria (e.g., 5 additional flights with new geofence design before Phase 2 re-try).

risks.risk: Data immutability is not enforced; evidence from failed phase can be lost, preventing gate re-evaluation if conditions change
risks.mitigation: Mandate write-once evidence storage or comprehensive audit logging. All phase data preserved indefinitely. METER-018 verifies immutability before phase commences.

background_specs.value_of_value: Programme management and safe innovation. Without reversibility, operators fear discovering unanticipable problems (emergent behavior, crew workload spike) will trigger trial cancellation and loss of investment. Reversibility enables operators to take calculated risks knowing evidence is preserved and gates can be re-evaluated.
background_specs.strategic_alignment: Enables MAA/CAA innovation trials (Protector RG Mk1, GCAP early work, Project Alvina). Supports BVLOS expansion by reducing operator risk and acceleration trial schedules. Prerequisite for regulatory learning-by-doing.
background_specs.keystone_reference: K12 (reversible trial progression for safe innovation)
background_specs.reasoning: PhasedGateReversibility is a governance/process value with minimal industry practice (2.5 gates identified vs 6 ideal). Gap closure depends on CAA adoption of reversible-gate paradigm and operator buy-in. Trend is improving as AMLAS/SACE models reversible-gate processes, but progress is slow due to regulatory conservatism.

background_specs.owner: Applicant (community engagement lead) + CAA airspace change team (TDA consultation)
background_specs.authority: CAA Innovation Hub (policy guidance) + CAA airspace team (TDA consultation process)
background_specs.motivation: Community consultation (CAP 1616) and public engagement builds social license for BVLOS swarm operations. Without engagement, public opposition can delay or block trial authorization. GDPR and human rights law require prior notification to affected individuals.

risks.risk: Status (2 stakeholder groups engaged) far below Tolerable (5 groups); indicates systematic under-engagement with community
risks.mitigation: Publish public-engagement template: (1) identify 5+ stakeholder groups (residents, landowners, environmental, aviation, media), (2) issue prior notification >= 30 days before first flight, (3) conduct structured feedback collection (survey, workshop, open forum), (4) document response to all feedback (acceptance, mitigation, or rejection with rationale).

risks.risk: REQ017 (Public Engagement) has no user story and is explicitly absent from requirements spec. Indicates regulatory deprioritization of public engagement.
risks.mitigation: CAA explicitly elevate public engagement to mandatory authorization criterion (not optional). Link to CAP 1616 and social-license risk. Publish public-engagement KPI as part of trial authorization dashboard.

risks.risk: Noise and safety concerns from residents can trigger media campaign against trial. Inadequate engagement leads to misinformation spiral and community opposition.
risks.mitigation: Proactive media strategy: (1) early notification to local media, (2) clear safety messaging (independent termination, geofence containment), (3) offer media site visits during trial, (4) publish incident-response protocols. Counter misinformation with fact-based communication.

background_specs.value_of_value: Social license and regulatory authorization. Without public engagement, community opposition can block trial approval (political pressure on CAA). Business impact: failure on public engagement is increasingly likely to trigger regulatory refusal or extended consultation timeline.
background_specs.strategic_alignment: Supports CAA BVLOS expansion and urban-logistics (priority). Prerequisite for trials in populated areas. Enables CAP 1616 compliance and GDPR data-protection obligations.
background_specs.reasoning: PublicEngagementCoverage is alarmingly low and deprioritized. Status (2 groups) vs Tolerable (5 groups) represents 3-group gap. REQ017 missing from requirements spec indicates regulatory blind spot. Swarm operations will generate significant public interest and concern; current engagement practices are inadequate. Trend is improving but dangerously slowly (0.5 groups/year).

stakeholders.name: CAA Case Officer (REQ001)
stakeholders.specific_need: All mandatory document types and sections present on first Readiness Check to avoid rework cycles and case rejection

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: ConOps, SORA, Safety Case sections complete before formal review to assess hazard coverage

stakeholders.name: CAA SORA Assessor (REQ002)
stakeholders.specific_need: SORA evidence pack substantive and swarm-aware before setpoint assignment

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear definition of what constitutes completeness to avoid rejection and rework cost; specific acceptance criteria upfront
stakeholders.tolerable_override: Lower bar on first submission (e.g., 80% completeness with remediation plan acceptable)

stakeholders.name: CAA Programme Manager (REQ016)
stakeholders.specific_need: Completeness enables phased gates and predictable trial progression schedule

conflicts: Applicant wants flexibility on first submission (incomplete pack with remediation path acceptable); Case Officer wants 100% completeness on submission (no rework cycles). Conflict resolved by Case Officer authority and DeFOSPAM clarification that 'complete' means all mandatory types present and substantive content in each.

DeFOSPAM Dorothy-8 identified that 'complete' has two meanings (REQ001: application completeness vs REQ012: data logging completeness). Stakeholder conflict: Case Officer interprets completeness as pack intake; Flight Safety Investigator interprets it as forensic evidence retention. Resolved by distinct scale parameters.
stakeholder_mappings.reasoning: The Case Officer is the gate-keeper for case opening and has highest power and motivation. Safety-Case Reviewer depends on Case Officer delivering a substantially complete pack. Applicant motivation is cost-driven (rework avoidance) but lacks regulatory power. Clear definition of completeness (all mandatory types + substantive sections) serves both regulator and applicant.

stakeholders.name: CAA SORA Assessor (REQ002)
stakeholders.specific_need: Explicit swarm failure modes (loss-of-separation, loss-of-coordination, emergent failures) quantified in risk evidence so SAIL and OSO setpoints are defensible

stakeholders.name: CAA Autonomy Reviewer (REQ008)
stakeholders.specific_need: Swarm interdependency and emergent behavior evidence to assure collective decision-making safety

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Swarm hazards and boundaries reflected in ConOps and ODD (worst-credible swarm behaviors explicitly modeled)

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Safety Manager
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear definition of 'swarm-aware' and minimum evidence items required (3 per aircraft) to avoid open-ended regulatory demands; cost-effective testing approach
stakeholders.target_override: Lower minimum count (e.g., 2 items per aircraft) if coordination mechanism and loss-of-separation evidence present

stakeholders.name: Applicant Autonomy Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Quantified bounds on emergent behaviors and coordination latency to guide design and testing

conflicts: SORA Assessor and Autonomy Reviewer want maximal swarm-specific evidence (all failure cascades enumerated, all emergent behaviors bounded); Applicant prefers minimal viable evidence to reduce engineering and testing cost. Conflict resolved by scale requiring >= 3 items per aircraft covering interdependency, emergent behavior, and coordination—a middle ground forcing explicit attention to swarm safety without unrealistic completeness.

Definition of 'swarm-aware' differs: regulator = evidence addressing multi-aircraft failure modes; applicant = evidence that coordination mechanism is reliable. Resolved by Alexa's scale specifying three sub-scales (interdependency, emergent behavior, coordination mechanism) capturing both intent.
stakeholder_mappings.reasoning: SORA Assessor drives risk classification and mandatory operating limits. Without clear swarm-aware evidence, SAIL/OSO setpoints are indefensible and applicant faces rejection. Autonomy Reviewer shares SORA Assessor's concern (collective safety). Applicant must provide evidence but negotiates scope. Primary stakeholder is SORA Assessor because SORA findings are the regulatory anchor for the trial.

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Explicit definition of nominal and worst-credible behaviors for each flight phase (takeoff, transit, loiter, landing, etc.) so that two independent reviewers score clarity identically and hazard scope is unambiguous

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear ConOps boundaries inform contingency planning: what behaviors trigger termination vs crew takeover vs autonomous recovery

stakeholders.name: CAA Airspace Regulator (REQ004, REQ005)
stakeholders.specific_need: ConOps worst-credible boundaries (max altitude, drift distance, airspace boundaries) to define Temporary Danger Area and geofence setpoints

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear definition of 'worst-credible' boundary (is it mean + 3-sigma? absolute worst-case physics? real test data?) to bound design and testing scope

stakeholders.name: Flight Test Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Quantified nominal and worst-credible parameters to design test cases and acceptance criteria

stakeholders.name: MAA Range Safety Officer
stakeholders.specific_need: ConOps worst-credible boundary definition to set range impact zones and test clearances (if trial is on MOD range)

conflicts: Safety-Case Reviewer demands inter-rater clarity (kappa >= 0.8); regulator reviewers may disagree on what constitutes 'worst-credible' (statistical vs absolute). Applicant prefers bounded definition (e.g., mean + 3-sigma) to limit testing; Safety Reviewer may demand absolute worst-case to close all scenarios. Conflict resolved by requiring explicit definition upfront + test evidence of reviewer agreement.

Airspace Regulator wants conservative (large) worst-credible boundaries to protect other airspace users and set wide TDA/geofence margins; Applicant wants tight boundaries to reduce trial footprint and enable higher operational ceiling. Conflict resolved by CAA Airspace Regulator power and safety priority; applicant must justify tight boundaries with strong evidence.
stakeholder_mappings.reasoning: Safety-Case Reviewer is the central gatekeeper for ConOps/ODD approval. Clear ConOps boundaries enable all downstream decisions (airspace segregation, geofence setpoints, contingency triggers, termination rules). Flight-Ops Inspector and Airspace Regulator depend on Safety Reviewer's ConOps approval. Applicant must invest in clarity upfront but benefits from unambiguous regulatory requirements.

stakeholders.name: CAA Airspace Regulator (REQ005)
stakeholders.specific_need: Automatic geofence containment with 99.9% success across Monte Carlo failure scenarios to ensure fly-away risk is eliminated and TDA can be tightly bounded

stakeholders.name: Third-Party Air Users (civil aviation)
stakeholders.specific_need: Hard containment guarantee so that swarm cannot drift into their airspace and create collision risk. Zero-tolerance for boundary exceedance.
stakeholders.target_override: 100% containment (no exceptions); single breach is unacceptable

stakeholders.name: Public Below (non-segregated operations / ground risk)
stakeholders.specific_need: Geofence prevents uncontrolled aircraft drift into populated areas. Protection from fly-away risk.

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Flexibility in geofence boundaries and design approach (hard constraint vs soft recovery vs crew intervention) to manage design and certification cost. Wants proportional risk-based requirements.
stakeholders.tolerable_override: 98% or 99% success if crew intervention is available on-site and response time is < 5 seconds
stakeholders.target_override: 95% success if multi-redundant termination is demonstrated

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Geofence failure modes and worst-credible recovery time specified in ConOps to assess hazard mitigation completeness

stakeholders.name: MAA Range Safety Officer
stakeholders.specific_need: If trial on MOD range: geofence ensures aircraft cannot exit range boundary. Hard containment required.
stakeholders.target_override: 100% success rate; range safety non-negotiable

conflicts: CRITICAL CONFLICT: Airspace Regulator demands 99.9% success (hard containment); Applicant wants proportional requirement based on crew intervention availability and response time (98-99% acceptable if crew is present). Third-Party Air Users and Public want zero breaches. Conflict resolution: CAA Airspace Regulator power is absolute; geofence is a hard safety requirement. Applicant cannot negotiate target downward; must invest in redundancy and Monte Carlo validation.

Design approach conflict: Applicant may propose soft geofence (warning boundary) with crew takeover; Airspace Regulator requires hard geofence (automatic motor cutoff or autonomous containment maneuver). Conflict resolved by regulatory safety priority: hard containment is non-negotiable.

Test scope conflict: Applicant wants limited failure mode coverage (single failures); CAA demands comprehensive Monte Carlo including dual failures, GPS denial, sensor faults, and software faults. Conflict resolved by scale requiring dual failure coverage and environmental ODD bounds.
stakeholder_mappings.reasoning: Airspace Regulator has absolute power to approve or reject geofence design and is accountable to Third-Party Air Users and Public. Without 99.9% containment evidence, TDA cannot be safely approved. Third-Party Air Users and Public depend on Airspace Regulator enforcement. Applicant must meet this hard requirement; no negotiation on target. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-3 and Observation-2 flagged that containment breach action is undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes the 99.9% success criterion.

stakeholders.name: CAA Spectrum Specialist (REQ006)
stakeholders.specific_need: C2 link loss survival times quantified (>= 30 seconds autonomous, >= 120 seconds with crew takeover) to assess non-interference with other users and spectrum efficiency

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: Link loss response (failover behavior, crew takeover latency, termination decision) defined to ensure safe contingency execution

stakeholders.name: CAA DAA Assessor (REQ007)
stakeholders.specific_need: If C2 loss triggers autonomous DA logic, inter-vehicle coordination must remain safe. Link loss affects both C2 and inter-vehicle data; evidence required.

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Autonomous survival time threshold and crew takeover latency defined upfront to design C2 redundancy, failover logic, and user interface. Wants achievable targets (e.g., 30 sec autonomous is feasible with modern autopilots)
stakeholders.target_override: Lower survival times (e.g., 15 seconds autonomous, 60 seconds with crew takeover) if ground-based recovery is available

stakeholders.name: Flight Test Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Defined test scenarios for C2 loss (complete loss, partial degradation, inter-vehicle link loss, combined GPS+C2 loss) to structure flight test and acceptance criteria

stakeholders.name: Applicant Autonomy Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Inter-vehicle link loss behavior defined: does swarm split? Return to base individually? Maintain formation? Bounds on behavior during link loss inform algorithm design.

conflicts: Spectrum Specialist demands high survival times (>= 30 sec autonomous) to prove non-interference; Applicant may want lower thresholds (e.g., 15 sec) as easier to achieve. Conflict resolved by Spectrum Specialist regulatory authority and realistic automation capability (modern autopilots can sustain 30-60 seconds safely).

Flight-Ops Inspector wants crew takeover available as primary failover (>= 120 sec window); Applicant may propose autonomous termination instead (cost lower, crew not needed). Conflict: human factors and meaningful control requirements favor crew takeover; Alexa's scale requires both paths quantified but defaults to crew takeover + autonomous survival.

Inter-vehicle link loss scenario: Applicant prefers simple behavior (split to base individually); Regulators want swarm to maintain formation if possible (preserves safety case). Conflict resolved by requiring both sub-cases tested and behavior pre-defined in ConOps.
stakeholder_mappings.reasoning: Spectrum Specialist has regulatory authority over C2 link performance and must defend non-interference to other spectrum users (civil aviation, emergency services). Flight-Ops Inspector shares responsibility (contingency management). Applicant must design and test to meet 30-second survival minimum. Link loss is a critical failure mode (loss-of-separation risk) and Spectrum Specialist controls the approval threshold.

stakeholders.name: CAA DAA Assessor (REQ007)
stakeholders.specific_need: Detect-and-avoid resolution is predictable and consistent (>= 95% decision consistency, >= 500ft horizontal recovery) so that encounter outcomes are defensible and DO-365 / F3442 compliance is demonstrable

stakeholders.name: CAA Airspace Regulator (REQ004, REQ005)
stakeholders.specific_need: DAA system prevents loss-of-separation with non-cooperative aircraft in uncontrolled airspace. Resolution must not trigger secondary conflicts or endanger third parties.

stakeholders.name: Third-Party Air Users (civil aviation, commercial, general aviation)
stakeholders.specific_need: Swarm DAA response is predictable and non-aggressive so that encounter is resolved safely without forcing third-party maneuvers or creating secondary conflict
stakeholders.target_override: 100% consistency (zero unpredictable resolutions)

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear definition of 'predictable' and 'consistent' with quantified success thresholds (>= 95% decision consistency, >= 100 encounter test scenarios) to bound development and testing scope
stakeholders.target_override: Lower consistency threshold (e.g., 90%) if encounter type distribution is representative and coverage includes corner cases

stakeholders.name: Applicant Autonomy Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Defined encounter types (cooperative ADS-B, non-cooperative radar, pop-up) and decision rules to guide DAA algorithm design and testing. Reproducible test framework for consistency measurement.

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: DAA resolution behavior known and practiced in crew training so that crew can monitor and override if needed (meaningful human control)

conflicts: DAA Assessor wants maximum encounter coverage (all geometry types, all relative speeds, all weather conditions) to prove robustness; Applicant wants bounded test scenarios (representative sample of 100 encounters) to limit cost. Conflict resolved by DO-365 / F3442 standard applicability; Applicant must meet industry-standard encounter distribution.

Third-Party Air Users expect zero unpredictable resolutions (100% consistency); DAA Assessor sets realistic threshold (>= 95%) to acknowledge edge cases. Conflict resolved by regulatory authority: >= 95% consistency is the gate; residual 5% documented and acceptable.

Swarm coordination on encounter: Applicant may prefer single-aircraft DA response (each aircraft independently avoids); Airspace Regulator and DAA Assessor want coordinated swarm response to maintain formation and avoid secondary conflicts. Conflict: scale requires both swarm coordination on encounter and decision consistency.
stakeholder_mappings.reasoning: DAA Assessor has regulatory authority over detect-and-avoid performance and must defend consistency and predictability to Third-Party Air Users and Airspace Regulator. DO-365 / F3442 standards are the regulatory anchor. Applicant must design and test to meet >= 95% consistency and zero secondary conflicts. Third-Party Air Users have power (can formally object to trial) and depend on DAA Assessor's approval.

stakeholders.name: CAA Autonomy Reviewer (REQ008)
stakeholders.specific_need: Emergent behaviors identified, bounded, and tested (>= 5 behaviors, quantified limits, >= 95% flight test hours in bounds) to assure collective swarm behavior is predictable and safe

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Emergent behavior definitions and boundaries reflected in ConOps and ODD so that worst-credible collective behaviors are explicitly scoped in hazard analysis

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: Out-of-bounds emergent behavior triggers defined (detection mechanism + recovery action) to inform contingency planning and crew training

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear definition of 'emergent behavior' and minimum count (>= 5) to bound analysis scope; detection/recovery mechanisms must be feasible to implement and test
stakeholders.tolerable_override: Lower count (>= 3) if core behaviors (formation stability, coordinated maneuver, flocking) are thoroughly analyzed and tested

stakeholders.name: Applicant Autonomy Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Emergent behaviors quantified and detection criteria defined to guide swarm architecture design and flight testing. Wants proportional analysis (focus on safety-critical behaviors like loss-of-separation, emergent cohesion loss, unintended split)

conflicts: Autonomy Reviewer wants exhaustive emergent behavior inventory (formation geometry drift, coordination latency, flocking instability, split/rejoin behavior, multi-vehicle collision avoidance); Applicant wants focus on safety-critical behaviors only (formation loss-of-separation, coordinated maneuver stability). Conflict resolved by Alexa's scale requiring >= 5 distinct behaviors with quantified boundaries and test evidence—applicant must justify each behavior as either safety-critical or safety-relevant.

Detection mechanism conflict: Autonomy Reviewer wants automatic detection and autonomous recovery/abort (safety-critical); Applicant may prefer crew monitoring and manual intervention (lower automation cost).
