Conflict resolved by meaningful human control requirements (REQ010)—crew must be in the loop, but detection can be automatic.

Test coverage: Autonomy Reviewer wants >= 95% of flight test hours in bounds (stringent); Applicant prefers lower threshold if out-of-bounds occurrences are rare and handled safely. Conflict: scale requires >= 95% to assure boundedness; residual 5% must be documented and analyzed.
stakeholder_mappings.reasoning: Autonomy Reviewer has regulatory authority over swarm decision-making and emergent behavior assurance. Without bounded emergent behaviors, swarm is unpredictable and uninsurable. Safety-Case Reviewer depends on Autonomy Reviewer's conclusions. Applicant must invest in behavioral analysis and flight testing to identify, quantify, and bound emergent behaviors. DeFOSPAM Observation-6 flagged that emergent deviation outcomes are undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes this by requiring pre-defined recovery mechanisms.

stakeholders.name: CAA AI/ML Assurance Specialist (REQ009)
stakeholders.specific_need: ML decision logic is verifiable by independent reviewers (decision trees = 100%, neural networks = LIME/SHAP with >= 80% coverage) so that safety assurance case can be closed and operational deployment is defensible

stakeholders.name: AAIB / Flight Safety Investigator (post-incident)
stakeholders.specific_need: ML model decisions captured and explainable from logged decision inputs and feature saliency so that incident root cause can be determined forensically
stakeholders.target_override: Maximal explainability (every decision path reconstructable); zero black-box models

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Autonomy Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Practical definition of 'forensically explainable' with model-type-specific approaches so that cost-prohibitive explainability requirements are avoided (e.g., decision trees acceptable, neural networks require SHAP, SVMs require feature importance)
stakeholders.target_override: Lower explainability threshold for non-safety-critical decisions (e.g., performance optimization) vs safety-critical decisions (full reconstruction required)

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: ML components assurable in safety case so that hazard mitigation claims can be verified. Explainability is one part of broader assurance case (training data provenance, validation performance, operational monitoring)

stakeholders.name: Defence Aviation Safety Board / MOD Safety Authority
stakeholders.specific_need: If trial uses classified data or MOD models, explainability must be demonstrable without revealing classified information. Security vs transparency trade-off.

conflicts: CRITICAL CONFLICT: AAIB wants maximal explainability (every decision path reconstructable, zero black-box); Applicant wants practical threshold (neural networks with SHAP >= 80% coverage acceptable). Conflict: AI/ML Assurance Specialist and AAIB have power (post-incident investigation authority); applicant must invest in explainability. Alexa's scale operationalizes pragmatic approach: decision-tree models = 100%, neural networks = saliency maps + LIME/SHAP with >= 80% coverage. This is middle ground but weighted toward explainability.

Cost vs assurance: Applicant prefers simpler models (SVMs, decision trees) to avoid explainability cost; Autonomy Engineer prefers neural networks (better performance on swarm coordination decisions). Conflict: explainability requirement incentivizes simpler models; applicant must choose between performance and cost.

Safety-critical vs performance: Applicant wants lower explainability for performance-optimization decisions (e.g., trajectory smoothing); AI/ML Specialist argues all swarm-coordination decisions are safety-critical. Conflict: scale defaults to safety-critical = full reconstruction, non-safety-critical = feature importance sufficient. Burden is on applicant to justify low-criticality classification.
stakeholder_mappings.reasoning: AI/ML Assurance Specialist has regulatory authority over ML assurance case and must defend explainability to AAIB and Safety-Case Reviewer. AAIB (post-incident) has power to demand maximum forensic detail. Applicant must design, train, and document ML models with explainability in mind from the start. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-4 flagged 'forensically explainable' as unrealistic for neural networks; Alexa's scale operationalizes pragmatic model-type-specific approaches. MLForensicExplainability is a primary conflict hotspot (cost vs assurance vs performance).

stakeholders.name: CAA Human Factors Inspector (REQ010)
stakeholders.specific_need: Crew situational awareness (>= 2Hz nominal, >= 10Hz off-nominal), authority preservation (100% on irreversible, >= 80% on reversible decisions), takeover latency (>= 10 seconds), and workload bounds (>= 3 concurrent alarms, <= 6 min/hour control) demonstrate meaningful human control throughout

stakeholders.name: CAA Autonomy Reviewer (REQ008)
stakeholders.specific_need: Autonomous decision boundaries and override capability defined so that crew can interrupt and regain control in off-nominal states (relates to emergent behavior recovery)

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Quantified meaningful control thresholds (awareness frequency, authority percentage, latency bounds) to design crew interface and training, avoiding open-ended 'meaningful' requirement
stakeholders.tolerable_override: Lower state update frequency (1Hz nominal) if crew interface displays aggregate swarm health in real-time
stakeholders.target_override: Higher automation (< 80% authority preservation) if autonomous safety margins are wide and crew training emphasizes monitoring vs intervention

stakeholders.name: Applicant Flight Test Crew (Pilot, Sensor Operator)
stakeholders.specific_need: Crew interface and control latency enable safe and usable monitoring and intervention during flight test. Not overly workload-intensive. Tangible feedback on system state.

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: Crew takeover and termination decision points clear in ConOps so that emergency procedures can be trained and practiced

stakeholders.name: RAISO / Range Safety Officer
stakeholders.specific_need: If on MOD range: crew control must enable range safety officer to interdict flight if needed. Meaningful control supports range safety integration.

conflicts: CRITICAL CONFLICT: Human Factors Inspector wants high control authority (100% on irreversible, >= 80% on reversible) and fast takeover (>= 10 sec); Applicant prefers high autonomy (>= 90% autonomous decisions) to reduce crew workload and cost. Conflict: meaningful human control is a regulatory principle (CAP 722 requirement); Applicant must accept >= 80% authority preservation on reversible decisions. Takeover latency (10 seconds) is achievable with modern autopilots and crew interfaces.

Situational awareness trade-off: Human Factors Inspector wants high update frequency (>= 2Hz nominal, >= 10Hz off-nominal) to enable meaningful control; Applicant wants lower frequency (1Hz) to reduce interface complexity and crew workload. Conflict: scale requires >= 2Hz nominal (industry standard for UAS situational awareness); off-nominal can be negotiated down to 5Hz if safety-critical alerts are instant.

Workload vs autonomy: Applicant wants high autonomy to reduce crew mental load (cost-driven); Human Factors Inspector worries that over-automation creates monitoring fatigue and complacency (safety concern). Conflict: scale balances both by limiting concurrent alarms (>= 3) and active control duty cycle (<= 6 min/hour) so crew remains engaged without burnout.
stakeholder_mappings.reasoning: Human Factors Inspector has regulatory authority over meaningful human control and crew fitness and must defend control authority to CAA, Parliament (EU Regulation 2019/945 compliance), and public. Applicant prefers automation (lower cost) but regulatory requirement forces human-in-the-loop design. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-2 flagged 'meaningful' as undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes it via four measurable dimensions (awareness, authority, latency, workload). MeaningfulHumanControl is a top conflict hotspot (automation vs safety).

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: Independent flight termination effective in any off-nominal state (loss of autonomy, loss of C2, loss of geofence, engine failure, etc.) with >= 50 test scenarios and completion within defined time (e.g., <= 10 seconds) so that trial can be safely terminated

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Termination capability and contingency plan documented in ConOps as final safety layer. Worst-credible scenarios must include termination failure and safe-fallback configuration.

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight Safety Investigator (REQ012)
stakeholders.specific_need: Post-termination actions (crew alert, ATSU notification, data preservation, recovery) defined so that incident investigation is not delayed by recovery operations

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Termination mechanism and failure modes defined (e.g., dual-redundant motor cutoff, parachute, controlled spiral descent) so that design scope is bounded and cost is predictable
stakeholders.target_override: Single-channel termination if monitoring/verification proves sufficient; dual-channel if failure history suggests failure rate >= 1e-4

stakeholders.name: Applicant Flight Test Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Termination test scenarios defined (loss-of-autonomy, loss-of-C2, loss-of-geofence, engine failure, collision threat, environmental extreme) to structure flight test plan

stakeholders.name: Third-Party Air Users
stakeholders.specific_need: Swarm can be reliably terminated and brought to safe configuration (no powered flight, minimal drift) if off-nominal state is detected so that collision risk is mitigated

conflicts: Single vs dual redundancy: Flight-Ops Inspector prefers dual-channel termination (fail-safe); Applicant wants single-channel (lower cost) if reliability is proven. Conflict: scale requires >= 50 test scenarios covering all failure modes; applicant must demonstrate high reliability (MTBF >= 1e6 hours) for single-channel to be acceptable.

Termination mechanism: Flight-Ops Inspector may prefer parachute or controlled descent (proven); Applicant prefers autonomous motor cutoff (lower cost, simpler). Conflict: mechanism must achieve safe configuration (motors off, drift ceased) within defined time; applicant chooses mechanism but must prove safety.

Post-termination actions: Flight-Ops Inspector wants comprehensive logging and ATSU notification (safety accountability); Applicant wants minimal overhead (cost-driven). Conflict: scale requires all post-termination actions (crew alert, data preservation, recovery notification) defined and tested; non-negotiable for regulatory approval.
stakeholder_mappings.reasoning: Flight-Ops Inspector has regulatory authority over flight termination and contingency planning and must defend termination reliability to Safety-Case Reviewer and Third-Party Air Users. Without independent termination capability, trial cannot proceed. Applicant must invest in redundancy and extensive testing (>= 50 failure scenarios) to achieve regulatory confidence. DeFOSPAM Observation-9 flagged that post-termination actions are undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes comprehensive termination with explicit post-termination procedures.

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight Safety Investigator (REQ012)
stakeholders.specific_need: Complete recorded evidence set captured on incident (all state variables at >= 10Hz, logged for >= investigation duration, data integrity verified with checksums, forensic provenance documented) so that MOR investigation is thorough and defensible

stakeholders.name: AAIB (Air Accidents Investigation Branch)
stakeholders.specific_need: Flight data recorder equivalent: all aircraft state variables logged with synchronized timestamps, complete sensor inputs, crew actions, autonomy mode transitions, and system alerts so that post-incident forensic analysis is complete
stakeholders.target_override: Maximal logging (50Hz for high-frequency dynamics, 1-year retention) to maximize forensic information

stakeholders.name: Defence Aviation Safety Board (if MOD trial)
stakeholders.specific_need: Classified data logging and retention compliant with security protocols (JSP 440, CAP 722H) so that investigation is thorough without security compromise

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Data logging schema and retention requirements defined upfront (state variables, sampling rate, duration, storage format) so that avionics and telemetry design is scoped and cost is predictable
stakeholders.target_override: Lower sampling rate (5Hz instead of 10Hz) if event-triggered higher-rate logging is available

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Logging and data retention plan verified in ConOps and ODD so that accident investigation capability is verified as part of safety assurance

conflicts: Sampling rate: AAIB wants 50Hz (high-frequency dynamics capture); Applicant prefers 10Hz (bandwidth/cost trade-off). Conflict: scale defaults to >= 10Hz nominal, >= 50Hz high-frequency; Applicant must justify lower rate with engineering analysis showing dynamics are captured sufficiently.

Data retention: AAIB wants long-term retention (1+ year) for systemic analysis; Applicant wants minimal retention (post-incident investigation only, then deletion) to reduce storage cost and privacy risk. Conflict: scale requires retention for full MOR investigation duration; typical AAIB investigations take 18-24 months. Applicant must provision storage accordingly.

Classified data: MOD range operator (RAISO) wants secure logging of classified test data; AAIB may need access for investigation. Conflict: security protocols (JSP 440) require sanitization of classified data before sharing with external investigators. Scale requires forensic provenance (cryptographic hash, timestamp); applicant must implement secure logging that allows investigation without breach.
stakeholder_mappings.reasoning: Flight Safety Investigator has regulatory authority over MOR obligations and must defend investigation completeness to AAIB. AAIB (post-incident) has power to demand maximum forensic detail. Applicant must design avionics and telemetry to capture and log all required state variables from the start. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-8 and Observation-10 flagged that data completeness and MOR trigger/action are undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes forensic completeness with specific schema and retention requirements. MORForensicCompleteness is a top conflict hotspot (investigation completeness vs privacy vs cost).

stakeholders.name: CAA Licensing Inspector (REQ014)
stakeholders.specific_need: Documented crew competency evidence: certifications held, training completed, simulation proficiency (>= 90% emergency procedure success), and operational experience (>= min flight hours in similar conditions) so that crew licensing and authorization is defensible

stakeholders.name: CAA Human Factors Inspector (REQ010)
stakeholders.specific_need: Crew competency in swarm-specific operations (coordination, emergent behavior monitoring, termination decision-making) demonstrated via simulation and training so that meaningful human control is credible

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Specific competency domains and evidence types defined (e.g., CAA UAS operator cert, swarm-ops endorsement, 50 hours BVLOS experience) so that training plan and cost are bounded
stakeholders.tolerable_override: Waive certain certifications if equivalent simulation proficiency is demonstrated (e.g., military pilots may substitute military jet experience for UAS hours)
stakeholders.target_override: Lower min flight hours (e.g., 25 hours instead of 50) if simulation-based training is comprehensive and proficiency is high

stakeholders.name: Flight Test Crew (Pilot, Sensor Operator, Safety Officer)
stakeholders.specific_need: Training curriculum and proficiency standards clear so that crew is confident in their ability to operate the swarm safely and recover from off-nominal states

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Crew training and competency plan documented in ConOps and Training Plan so that human error risk is addressed in safety assurance case

conflicts: Experience requirement: Licensing Inspector wants >= 50 hours BVLOS swarm experience; Applicant wants waiver if simulation proficiency is high (cost-driven, schedule-driven). Conflict: Licensing Inspector authority is absolute on waiver requests; applicant must present strong simulator validation evidence (equivalent fidelity to real flight) to negotiate. Typically, simulators provide partial credit (e.g., 25 simulator hours = 5 real flight hours).

Certification scope: Licensing Inspector wants swarm-ops-specific endorsement (formal CAA approval); Applicant may have military or commercial UAS cert but not swarm-specific. Conflict: CAA Licensing Inspector may grant conditional authorization pending swarm-ops endorsement; applicant pursues endorsement in parallel with trial.

Competency domains: Human Factors Inspector wants broad competency assessment (airmanship, decision-making, swarm monitoring, emergency procedures); Applicant wants narrow focus (system operation, basic manual recovery). Conflict: scale defines role-specific competencies (pilot: airmanship + swarm ops, sensor operator: swarm monitoring + data interpretation); applicant must address all domains in training plan.
stakeholder_mappings.reasoning: Licensing Inspector has regulatory authority over crew licensing and authorization and must defend crew competency to CAA and public. Human Factors Inspector shares authority on swarm-specific competency. Applicant must invest in crew training and proficiency documentation. CrewCompetencyEvidence is generally low-conflict (both regulator and applicant want qualified crew) but has scheduling implications (training time is on critical path to trial start).

stakeholders.name: CAA/MAA Liaison (REQ015)
stakeholders.specific_need: Signed apportionment matrix covers 100% of SORA hazards, 100% of design domains, and 100% of operational domains with no gaps (unassigned) and no overlaps (dual-assigned) so that regulatory responsibility is unambiguous and each regulator is accountable

stakeholders.name: CAA Case Officer (REQ001)
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear CAA regulatory scope defined in apportionment so that CAA case file is coherent and CAA responsibilities are not challenged by MAA interpretations

stakeholders.name: MAA Lead Inspector (RPAS Regulatory Branch)
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear MAA regulatory scope defined in apportionment so that MAA responsibilities are unambiguous and not in conflict with CAA expectations

stakeholders.name: Delivery Duty Holder (DDH) / Operating Duty Holder (ODH)
stakeholders.specific_need: Apportionment split reflects safety accountability: CAA responsible for public/airspace safety, MAA responsible for range/MOD safety; DDH accountable for design and operational compliance to split
stakeholders.target_override: Clear split that aligns with DDH liability and insurance scope

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Apportionment clear so that applicant knows which regulator (CAA vs MAA) to satisfy for each requirement. Avoids conflicting demands from dual regulators.

conflicts: CRITICAL CONFLICT: CAA and MAA may disagree on responsibility split: CAA may claim public-safety authority over all aspects; MAA may claim range-safety authority as primary. Example: geofence enforcement—is it CAA airspace protection (CAA) or MAA range containment (MAA)? Conflict: Alexa's scale requires both regulators to sign apportionment matrix; disagreements must be resolved before trial authorization. Liaison role (CAA/MAA Liaison) is responsible for negotiation.

Gap vs overlap: CAA/MAA may leave aspects unassigned to avoid responsibility (gap); or both may claim responsibility (overlap). Conflict: scale explicitly requires 100% coverage and zero overlaps; applicant must escalate unresolved gaps to CAA/MAA joint panel chair.

Design vs operational split: CAA may claim design authority (AMLAS, safety case); MAA may claim operational authority (range clearance, daily go/no-go). Conflict: scope of 'design domain' vs 'operational domain' must be negotiated in apportionment; typical split is CAA = aircraft design/certification, MAA = range operations/risk management.
stakeholder_mappings.reasoning: CAA/MAA Liaison has explicit responsibility for apportionment negotiation and must broker agreement between CAA Case Officer and MAA Lead Inspector. Without signed apportionment, trial authorization is blocked. DDH (operator) depends on clear split to structure project. Applicant has low power but high motivation (wants clarity). CAAMAAApportionmentGapFreeness is a top conflict hotspot (regulatory jurisdiction disputes); resolution requires governance escalation if Liaison cannot broker agreement.

stakeholders.name: CAA Programme Manager (REQ016)
stakeholders.specific_need: Phased trial gates with clear progression criteria (>= 4 phases), documented reversibility procedures, immutable data (write-once audit log), and re-entry procedures so that evidence builds progressively and risk is managed (gates can be reversed if new evidence suggests unacceptable risk)

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Phased gates structure hazard closure: earlier gates address system architecture and component risks; later gates address integration and operational risks. Reversibility allows re-evaluation if hazard evidence changes.

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Phase progression criteria clear and achievable so that trial schedule is predictable. BUT reversibility adds uncertainty (fear that gates can be reset, delaying trial). Wants forward momentum and cost control.
stakeholders.tolerable_override: Redefine 'reversibility' as 'pause current phase and gather more data' rather than 'reset to previous phase and redo all work'
stakeholders.target_override: Fewer, larger phases (e.g., 2 phases: ground test + flight test) instead of >= 4 phases to reduce gate-crossing overhead

stakeholders.name: CAA Innovation Hub / Trial Manager
stakeholders.specific_need: Reversibility enables risk management: if phase test reveals unexpected failure mode, trial can pause, mitigation can be designed and tested, and phase can be re-entered. Supports adaptive governance.

stakeholders.name: CAA Case Officer (REQ001)
stakeholders.specific_need: Phase gates and reversibility procedures documented in case file so that trial authorization and regulatory decision points are auditable

conflicts: CRITICAL CONFLICT: Programme Manager values reversibility (risk reduction, adaptive management); Applicant wants forward momentum (cost, schedule urgency). Applicant fears that reversibility is an excuse to block trial progress indefinitely. Conflict: scale defines reversibility as documented exit criteria and re-entry procedure (not indefinite hold); applicant must know the conditions for phase reversal upfront.

Phase granularity: Programme Manager may want fine-grained phases (>= 4: ground-test, VLOS-single, VLOS-swarm, BVLOS-swarm) to manage risk; Applicant wants coarser phases (2: ground + flight) to reduce overhead. Conflict: scale requires >= 4 phases but allows flexibility in definition; typical phases are ground-system-test, VLOS-single, VLOS-swarm, BVLOS-swarm.

Data immutability: Programme Manager wants audit-locked data (write-once) so gates can be re-evaluated; Applicant prefers mutable records (easier to update if errors found). Conflict: forensic integrity requires immutable logs (CAA standard); applicant must implement write-once-read-many (WORM) data storage or cryptographic audit trail.
stakeholder_mappings.reasoning: Programme Manager has regulatory authority over trial schedule and gate progression and must balance risk management (reversibility) with cost/schedule efficiency. Safety-Case Reviewer depends on gates to structure hazard closure. Applicant wants schedule certainty but must accept reversibility as risk-management mechanism. DeFOSPAM Observation-11 flagged that phase gate criteria are undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes gates with clear progression criteria, reversibility procedures, and immutable data. PhasedGateReversibility is a top conflict hotspot (risk management vs cost/schedule).

stakeholders.name: CAA Airspace Regulator (AAA) / Community Liaison
stakeholders.specific_need: CAP 1616 stakeholder engagement requirements met: >= 5 stakeholder groups engaged (residents, landowners, environmental, aviation, media), prior notification >= 30 days, documented feedback collection and response, residual concerns logged. Engagement evidence recorded in case file.

stakeholders.name: Local Communities / Non-Segregated Airspace Users
stakeholders.specific_need: Prior notification of trial operations, opportunity to voice concerns (survey, workshop, open forum), acknowledgment of feedback, and transparency on how concerns are addressed. Acceptance of trial depends on stakeholder engagement quality.
stakeholders.target_override: Maximum engagement (workshops, site visits, media briefings) to build public acceptance

stakeholders.name: Press / Media
stakeholders.specific_need: Media access to trial information, pre-flight briefings, and incident reporting so that press narrative is informed and not speculative. Opportunity to report on trial and regulate public perception.

stakeholders.name: Environmental Groups / Third Parties
stakeholders.specific_need: Engagement on environmental impacts (noise, species disturbance at dusk/night, battery fire risk) so that environmental concerns are documented and mitigations are transparent

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Engagement scope and process defined upfront (stakeholder list, notification timeline, feedback mechanism, response template) so that cost and schedule are bounded. Wants minimal engagement (cost-driven).
stakeholders.tolerable_override: Lower engagement scope if trial location is remote (no residential areas within 5km); can reduce to >= 3 stakeholder groups
stakeholders.target_override: Engagement handled by CAA (applicant cost-neutral); applicant provides technical input only

stakeholders.name: CAA Case Officer (REQ001)
stakeholders.specific_need: Engagement evidence documented in case file so that trial authorization is defensible against public challenge or media scrutiny

conflicts: CRITICAL CONFLICT: Local Communities and Media want maximum engagement and transparency; Applicant wants minimal engagement (cost, schedule overhead). CAA Airspace Regulator enforces CAP 1616 minimum (>= 30 days prior notification, >= 5 stakeholder groups, documented feedback). Conflict: engagement is non-negotiable regulatory requirement; applicant cannot opt out. CAA often funds or manages engagement to ensure quality.

Environmental scope: Environmental Groups want detailed environmental assessment (noise levels, species impact, battery fire risk); Applicant wants minimal assessment (within regulatory compliance). Conflict: REQ019 (environmental impact) is absent from original requirements but implied by CAP 1616; applicant must conduct Environmental Assessment or justify exemption.

Feedback response: Communities expect substantive response to concerns (mitigation, acceptance, or documented rejection); Applicant prefers 'acknowledge and continue.' Conflict: CAP 1616 requires documented response (acceptance, mitigation, or justified rejection); applicant cannot ignore feedback.
stakeholder_mappings.reasoning: Airspace Regulator has authority to enforce CAP 1616 engagement requirements and must defend trial authorization to public, local communities, and media. Local Communities and Media have power (public objection, media scrutiny) to delay or block trial. Applicant must invest in engagement (cost, schedule) as prerequisite to trial approval. DeFOSPAM Flo-10 and Observation-12 flagged that REQ017 has no user story and engagement outcomes are undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes CAP 1616 compliance with stakeholder identification, prior notification, feedback collection, and documented response. PublicEngagementCoverage is generally low-technical but high-social-risk conflict.

qc_findings.finding_title: Status critically far below tolerable constraint
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (15% of submissions with swarm-aware SORA items) is dangerously below Isaac's Tolerable (>= 3 items per aircraft = 80% adoption target). This 65-point gap reflects widespread non-adoption of JARUS SORA v2.5 swarm guidance. Current operators have not integrated swarm-specific failure modes into their risk assessment packages, leaving coordinated-failure risks unanalyzed. This is a regulatory integrity issue: authorizations based on incomplete swarm risk evidence cannot be defensibly granted.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Immediate action: CAA publishes exemplary swarm SORA evidence packages (draw from AMLAS case studies); conduct assessor training on JARUS SORA v2.5 swarm-risk taxonomy; Ray's METER-003 enforces minimum evidence requirements (>= 3 distinct items per aircraft addressing loss-of-separation, emergent failure, coordination mechanism). Medium-term: JARUS SORA v2.5 adoption curve acceleration via EUROCAE WG6 guidance publication; monthly CAA assessor feedback loop: what swarm-aware evidence impressed assessors becomes public guidance.
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 15% vs Ideal 80%; Isaac Constraint: >= 3 items tolerable level; Ray METER-003: Swarm-Specific Risk Evidence Inventory Audit; Raj Background: SwarmAwareSORAEvidence critical priority rank 2

qc_findings.finding_title: Largest gap in analysis: status alarmingly below tolerable
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (28% of safety-critical ML decisions reconstructable) is dangerously close to Isaac's Survival threshold (70%) and far below Tolerable (80%). The 62-point gap from Status to Ideal (90%) is the single-largest quality risk in this requirement set. This reflects widespread lack of ML explainability instrumentation: operators rely on high-performance neural networks (opaque) without LIME/SHAP/saliency-map explanations. Post-incident investigation will be blocked if AAIB cannot reconstruct ML decisions. This is an investigability crisis.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Immediate action: AMLAS guidance mandates explainability by model type (decision trees 100%, SVMs feature importance + boundary, neural networks LIME/SHAP >= 80% coverage). Ray's METER-011 tests reconstruction by independent reviewer (>= 80% target). Recommend decision-tree or SVM models for safety-critical decisions; reserve neural networks for non-critical optimization. Freeze model development post-authorization; all ML components require re-certification if updated. Short-term: industry adoption of LIME/SHAP tooling and AAIB acceptance of proxy explanations.
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 28% vs Tolerable 80% vs Ideal 90%; Isaac Survival: 70% (system near unassurable threshold); Ray METER-011: ML Decision Reconstruction Forensic Test; Raj Background: MLForensicExplainability critical priority rank 8; Tom Target: Wish/Goal/Stretch targets unreachable without Status improvement

qc_findings.finding_title: Status below tolerable; workload bounds significantly deficient
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (composite score 52/100) is 18 points below Isaac's Tolerable (70). Largest deficit is WorkloadBounds: Status 35/100 vs Tolerable 100. Swarm operations generate multiple concurrent events (engine failure, C2 loss, emergent-behavior anomaly) that exceed human crew capacity to monitor and respond. Scale requires SituationalAwareness >= 2Hz nominal / >= 10Hz off-nominal; AuthorityPreservation >= 80% reversible / 100% irreversible; TakeoverLatency >= 10s; WorkloadBounds <= 3 concurrent alarms, <= 6 min active control/hour. Current HMI designs do not meet these thresholds for multi-aircraft operations.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: HMI redesign critical: (1) Auto-triage alerts—display only 1-2 most critical concurrently; (2) Progressive disclosure—alert details on demand, not auto-display; (3) Swarm-level abstraction—show formation state, not per-aircraft state; (4) Pre-positioned crew takeover—system proposes action, crew approves in 0.5s cycle. Ray's METER-013 validates workload in simulation before authorization. NASA-TLX workload measurement required. Mandatory crew training on multi-event prioritization.
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 52 vs Tolerable 70; Isaac Constraint: composite 70 required; Ray METER-012, METER-013: Crew State-Update Frequency, Crew Workload & Takeover Latency Simulation Test; Raj Background: MeaningfulHumanControl critical priority rank 9, major workload risk

qc_findings.finding_title: Status significantly below tolerable; inter-rater clarity insufficient
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (55% clarity) is 30 points below Isaac's Tolerable (85%). Two independent safety-case reviewers do not agree consistently on what constitutes 'clear' ConOps. Current ConOps documents define nominal behavior but omit explicit worst-credible boundaries for each operational phase. This ambiguity cascades: crew training is ad-hoc, geofence/TDA margins are conservative (to close gaps), and safety case hazard analysis is incomplete. Scale requires inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa >= 0.8) and explicit worst-credible definitions per phase.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Publish ConOps template with mandatory sections: (1) Nominal behavior quantified (e.g., formation tolerance +/- 10m, heading variance <= 5°), (2) Worst-credible boundary explicit (mean + 3-sigma or absolute physics limit), (3) Recovery procedure for each off-nominal phase. Ray's METER-004 (Inter-Rater Clarity Consensus Test) becomes standard gate: dual independent review with structured questionnaire, kappa >= 0.8 requirement. Exemplar swarm ConOps library published (AMLAS case studies). ASTM F3642 clarity guidance adoption mandatory.
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 55% vs Tolerable 85%; Isaac Constraint: 85% required; Ray METER-004: Inter-Rater Clarity Consensus Test; Alan Stakeholders: Safety-Case Reviewer, Flight-Ops Inspector, Airspace Regulator dependent on ConOps clarity; Tom Targets: Wish 80%, Goal 90%, Stretch 95% (all unreachable without status improvement)

qc_findings.finding_title: Status significantly below critical regulatory minimum
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (88% encounter resolution success) is 11.5 points below Isaac's Tolerable (99.5%) and 11.9 points below Isaac's Regulatory minimum (99.9% per DO-365 MOPS). This gap reflects inadequate non-cooperative encounter detection: civil radars struggle with small-RCS swarms. Scale requires >= 99.5% encounter resolution success (>= 500ft horizontal recovery, zero secondary conflicts, >= 95% decision consistency across repeat encounters). Current systems do not meet this standard, meaning BVLOS trials in Class G airspace with civil traffic cannot be authorized.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Algorithm development required: (1) Invest in non-cooperative radar detection algorithm maturity—current physics limits are 70-90% detection; target 99%+ via sensor fusion (radar + ADS-B + visual). (2) Ray's METER-008 (DO-365 Encounter Resolution Test Matrix) runs >= 200 scenarios; analyze failures and iterate. (3) Extend testing to swarm-formation-aware encounters: does DAA logic respond consistently if formation is tight vs loose? (4) Define swarm DAA protocol: lead triggers resolution, followers execute coordinated maneuver. High development investment required; current status makes BVLOS authorization impossible.
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 88% vs Tolerable 99.5% vs Regulatory 99.9%; Isaac Constraint: 99.5% tolerable, 99.9% regulatory; Ray METER-008: DO-365 Encounter Resolution Test Matrix; Raj Background: DAAPerformance critical priority rank 6, 11% gap to tolerable

qc_findings.finding_title: Status alarmingly low; REQ017 missing user story indicates regulatory deprioritization
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (2 stakeholder groups engaged) is 3 groups below Isaac's Tolerable (5 groups minimum per CAP 1616). REQ017 has NO USER STORY in the requirements specification, indicating explicit regulatory deprioritization of public engagement. However, CAP 1616 airspace change process and GDPR data-protection law implicitly mandate community consultation. Swarm operations will generate significant public concern (noise, privacy, safety); current engagement practices are inadequate. Gap reflects social-license risk: community opposition triggered by poor engagement can block trial approval.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Elevate public engagement to mandatory authorization criterion (not optional). Publish public-engagement template: (1) Identify 5+ stakeholder groups (residents within 5km, landowners, environmental, aviation, media); (2) Issue prior notification >= 30 days before first flight; (3) Conduct structured feedback (survey, workshop, open forum); (4) Document response to all feedback (acceptance, mitigation, or rejection with rationale). Ray's METER-019 (CAP 1616 Consultation Audit) becomes standard gate. Proactive media strategy required: early notification, safety messaging, site visits during trial, incident-response protocol publication. Establish baseline social-license metrics (community satisfaction, misinformation detection).
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 2 groups vs Tolerable 5 vs Ideal 6; Isaac Constraint: 5 groups tolerable, 6 regulatory (populated areas); Ray METER-019: CAP 1616 Consultation Audit & Community Engagement Report; Raj Background: PublicEngagementCoverage priority rank 15, significant social-license risk; DeFOSPAM Observation-12: REQ017 absent user story

qc_findings.finding_title: Novel domain with nascent practice; status far below required test coverage
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (3 emergent behaviors identified) is far below Tolerable (95% of test flight hours within bounds). This is not a simple gap—it reflects the fundamental immaturity of swarm assurance. The scale conflates two metrics: behavior identification (3 behaviors present) vs test coverage (95% of hours in bounds). Current status measures only inventory; lacks test evidence. No standard taxonomy of emergent swarm behaviors exists (EUROCAE WG6 draft 2026). Without explicit inventory of expected behaviors and quantified boundaries, trial cannot assess whether observed swarm dynamics are safe or anomalous.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Urgent: EUROCAE WG6 swarm-behavior taxonomy development (target 2026 publication). Interim: AMLAS case studies establish accepted behaviors. Ray's METER-009 (Emergent Behavior Boundary Documentation Audit) requires operator to identify >= 5 distinct behaviors (formation, coordination latency, flocking, decision deadlock, split/rejoin) with quantified boundaries. Ray's METER-010 (Emergent Behavior Flight Test Coverage Validator) accumulates evidence: >= 95% of flight hours in bounds, out-of-bounds triggers investigation. Define decision policy: if behavior exceeds bounds in > 5% of flights, trial pauses pending investigation. Establish baseline compliance monitoring.
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 3 behaviors vs Tolerable 5 vs Ideal 8; Isaac Constraint: 95% test coverage within bounds; Ray METER-009, METER-010: Emergent Behavior audits; Raj Background: SwarmEmergentBoundedness critical priority rank 7, largest novel-domain gap; Tom Targets: Wish 5, Goal 6, Stretch 7 (all achievable if Tolerable is clarified)

qc_findings.finding_title: 20% ambiguous responsibility assignments for novel domains
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (80% coverage) vs Tolerable (100% gap-free) indicates 20% ambiguous responsibility assignments, particularly for swarm-specific domains. The scale requires binary outcome: either 100% gap-free (exactly one owner per aspect, no overlaps) or not. Current matrices lack clear apportionment for: swarm-aware SORA assessment (CAA vs MAA?), emergent-behavior assurance (who signs off?), ML explainability (CAA + AAIB but not MAA?). This creates regulatory risk: operators don't know who to answer to; delayed authorization cycles result.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: CAA/MAA joint task force publishes apportionment guidance for swarm-specific domains within 90 days: (1) Swarm-aware SORA assessment = CAA (primary); (2) Emergent-behavior assurance = CAA + joint autonomy reviewer; (3) ML explainability = CAA + AAIB (investigative interest), not MAA unless classified. Ray's METER-017 (Regulatory Responsibility Traceability Matrix Audit) becomes mandatory pre-case-opening gate: verifies 100% coverage, zero overlaps, both CAA/MAA signatures present. For classified trials (MOD involvement), establish classified-apportionment process (sanitized summary for CAA, classified annex retained by MAA, joint panel review in secure facility).
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 80% vs Tolerable 100% (binary); Isaac Constraint: 100% required; Ray METER-017: Regulatory Responsibility Traceability Matrix Audit; Raj Background: CAAMAAApportionmentGapFreeness priority rank 13, governance issue not technical

qc_findings.finding_title: Nascent governance model; status far below achievable targets
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (2.5 well-defined gates) is far below Tolerable (>= 4 gates) and Ideal (>= 6 gates with reversibility). This is a governance/process defect, not a technical one. Most trials use forward-only gates (pass/fail) rather than reversible gates (exit criteria, re-entry procedures, data preservation). Scale requires all gates to be reversible by design: if Phase 3 discovers emergent-behavior anomaly, trial pauses (not cancels), emerges investigation, re-entry criteria defined. Current practice risks: unexpected findings trigger program cancellation instead of learning cycles.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Publish phased-trial template: Phase 1 (ground), Phase 2 (VLOS single), Phase 3 (VLOS swarm), Phase 4 (BVLOS nominal), Phase 5 (BVLOS swarm). Ray's METER-018 validates reversibility: each gate has (1) clear criterion (e.g., 10 successful flights, zero emergent-behavior exceedances), (2) exit criterion (e.g., 1 out-of-bounds event detected), (3) re-entry criteria (e.g., 5 additional flights with new design before re-try). Mandate write-once evidence storage or comprehensive audit-logging: all phase data preserved indefinitely. Policy clarity: if behavior exceeds bounds in > 5% of flights, trial pauses (reversible), not cancels (terminal).
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 2.5 gates vs Tolerable >=4 vs Ideal >=6; Ray METER-018: Phased Gate Criteria & Reversibility Procedure Specification; Raj Background: PhasedGateReversibility priority rank 14, programme management enabler

qc_findings.finding_title: Metric conflation: survival time vs link availability; definition ambiguity
qc_findings.defect_description: Alexa's Scale specifies time-based metric (>= 30 seconds autonomous survival). Isaac's Constraint specifies availability metric (>= 99% link availability). These measure different things: survival time = duration after link loss before unsafe state; availability = percentage of mission time link is operational. Scale and Constraint are not directly comparable. Raj's Background notes this conflation as a risk. Brook's Status (45-second survival) meets Survival threshold (30s) but doesn't clarify link availability percentage. Ambiguity creates validation gap: which metric dominates authorization decision?
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Clarify metrics: (1) Survival time = autonomous safe-state duration after complete C2 loss (>= 30s minimum per scale); (2) Link availability = percentage of mission time C2 link is operational (>= 99% per Isaac). Ray's METER-007 measures both independently: autonomous survival time AND link availability percentage. Update scale or constraint to eliminate ambiguity. Recommend: primary metric is 99% availability (crew reliance); secondary metric is 30s autonomous survival (failover assurance). Both must pass for authorization.
qc_findings.cross_references: Alexa Scale: >= 30 seconds autonomous survival (time-based); Isaac Constraint: >= 99% link availability (percentage-based); Ray METER-007: Link Loss Survival Hold-Safe Validation (measures both); Brook benchmarks: Status 45 seconds (only survival, not availability); Raj Background: C2LinkResilience medium risk on metric conflation

qc_findings.finding_title: Swarm-operations endorsement nascent; target 1.5 competencies below tolerable
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (3.5 documented competencies per crew member) is 1.5 competencies below Isaac's Tolerable (5). Current crew licensing requires: CAA UAS operator cert, aircraft type rating, operational endorsement. Swarm-operations endorsement is nascent and not yet widely deployed (emerging in 2026). Scale requires 5: certification (UAS operator + swarm endorsement + type rating), role-specific training (pass/fail documented), emergency-procedure simulation (>= 90% success), minimum flight hours (>= 50 pilot, >= 20 sensor op). Gap reflects training curriculum lag.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: CAA/JARUS publish swarm-operations endorsement training curriculum (timeline 2026). Pilot training providers offer endorsement courses. Interim: grandfather early pilots under alternative pathways (AMLAS trial participation = swarm-ops endorsement equivalent). Standard emergency procedures required: link-loss recovery, geofence-breach response, DAA encounter handling, swarm-member incapacitation. Ray's METER-016 validates >= 90% simulation proficiency before authorization. Allow simulator hours toward experience requirement (10 hours simulator = 10 hours operational). Gap is achievable through training standardization.
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 3.5 competencies vs Tolerable 5 vs Record 6; Isaac Constraint: 5 required; Ray METER-016: Crew Emergency Procedure Simulation Validation; Raj Background: CrewCompetencyEvidence priority rank 12, training-dependent improvement

qc_findings.finding_title: Status at baseline; 28-point gap to tolerable indicates persistent rework cycles
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (72% first-submission pack completeness) is 28 points below Isaac's Tolerable (100%). This means ~28% of submissions are rejected on first Readiness Check and must be reworked. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-8 identified ambiguity: 'complete' has two meanings (REQ001: application intake completeness vs REQ012: forensic data logging completeness). Alexa distinguished these with different scale parameters. However, 28% rework rate indicates operators lack clear definition of what constitutes a 'complete' pack before submission. This cascades: downstream reviews (SORA, safety case, crew training) cannot commence until pack passes gate.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Publish prescriptive document checklist (per operation type: PDRA-01 VLOS, BVLOS Swarm SAIL-V, etc.) in CAA guidance before intake opens. Implement Ray's METER-001 (Artefact Detector Quick Gate): automated file presence/absence detection, flagging gaps within 10 minutes of submission. Mandatory documents: ConOps (>= 5 pages, explicit worst-credible boundaries), SORA (>= 3 pages, swarm-aware risk evidence), Safety Case (>= 10 pages, hazard coverage), Training Plan, Personnel List, Environmental Assessment. Offer pre-submission consultation to reduce rejection rate. Target: 90% first-submission success within 9 months (Tom Wish), 100% within 12 months (Tom Goal).
qc_findings.cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 72% vs Tolerable 100%; Isaac Constraint: binary gate (100% pass or rework); Ray METER-001: Artefact Detector Quick Gate; Ray METER-002: Paragraph-Level Coverage Audit (Thorough Gate); Raj Background: PackCompleteness critical priority rank 1, foundational gate; Tom Targets: Wish 90%, Goal 100%

qc_findings.finding_title: Status exceeds tolerable; minimal gap but swarm multiplication risk unaddressed
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (99.6% containment) is ABOVE Isaac's Tolerable (99.5%), suggesting technology maturity. However, the Scale measures single-aircraft containment via Monte Carlo. Swarm operations multiply risk: probability that AT LEAST ONE aircraft breaches geofence increases combinatorially (5 aircraft: ~5 x single-breach probability). Scale does not explicitly address multi-aircraft correlation. Raj's Background notes this: 'Swarm ops increase breach probability combinatorially.' Status (99.6%) may mask aggregated risk when formalized as swarm.
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Extend scale to explicitly measure multi-aircraft containment: test all N aircraft simultaneously during Monte Carlo; measure probability that none breach. For 5-aircraft swarm: each aircraft at 99.6% means swarm at ~97.8% containment (5 independent failures). Tolerable constraint may need elevation (99.95% single-aircraft to achieve 99% swarm). Ray's METER-006 (Live Fly-Away Breach Test) must include full-swarm dynamics: do formation interactions (e.g., wake effects, RF interference) degrade individual geofence performance? Continuous post-flight monitoring (METER-010 pattern) tracks real-world performance; if trend moves below Tolerable, trial pauses.
qc_findings.cross_references: Alexa Scale: Monte Carlo 99.9% success across single/dual failures; Brook benchmarks: Status 99.6% vs Tolerable 99.5% vs Survival 99.0% vs Regulatory 99.9%; Isaac Constraint: multiple levels per populated area; Ray METER-005, METER-006: Monte Carlo + Live Fly-Away Test; Raj Background: GeofenceContainment notes swarm multiplication risk

qc_findings.finding_title: Status slightly below tolerable; multi-aircraft coordination gap
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (99.1% effectiveness) is 0.8 points below Isaac's Tolerable (99.9%). This is a modest gap suggesting technology maturity (98% baseline in FY 2024 improved to 99.1%). However, Scale measures single-aircraft termination via FMEA-derived fault injection. Swarms add complexity: if one aircraft terminates while others continue, mid-air collision risk during synchronized descent. Scale does not specify multi-aircraft termination protocol or test coverage. Raj's Background notes this: 'Swarm termination is coordinated: if one aircraft terminates while others continue, mid-air collision risk during descent.'
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Define swarm termination protocol: (1) Trigger fires on one aircraft (independent termination system); (2) Broadcast termination command to swarm via inter-vehicle link; (3) All aircraft execute synchronized descent with separation maintained (not free-fall). Ray's METER-014 (FMEA-Derived Fault-Injection Test) must include multi-aircraft scenarios: terminate one aircraft while others continue; verify no mid-air collisions. Expand fault modes to include inter-vehicle link loss during termination (formation breakup risk). Test >= 50 failure scenarios including multi-aircraft termination sequences. Continuous post-flight monitoring tracks termination reliability; if trend moves below 99%, trial pauses.
qc_findings.cross_references: Alexa Scale: >= 99.9% single-aircraft effectiveness; Brook benchmarks: Status 99.1% vs Tolerable 99.9% vs Survival 99.0%; Isaac Constraint: 99.9% required for first flight; Ray METER-014: Independent Termination Fault-Injection Test; Raj Background: IndependentFlightTermination notes multi-aircraft coordination gap

qc_findings.finding_title: Status below tolerable; swarm data-volume management unaddressed
qc_findings.defect_description: Brook's Status (88% incident data completeness) is 12 points below Isaac's Tolerable (100%). Scale requires complete recorded evidence (all state variables >= 10Hz, data integrity no gaps > 100ms, forensic provenance verified, retention through MOR investigation). Status gap (12%) indicates ~12% of incidents have logging gaps or missing provenance certification. For swarms, data volume multiplies: 5-aircraft swarm logging ~100+ state variables at >= 10Hz generates gigabytes per hour. Storage, transmission, and forensic provenance management become challenging. Scale does not explicitly address swarm-specific logging schema (per-aircraft state, inter-vehicle link state, formation geometry).
qc_findings.fix_recommendation: Define swarm logging schema: (1) Full-resolution for safety-critical variables (position XYZ, velocity, autonomy mode, engine status, crew inputs); (2) Aggregated/compressed for non-critical (sensor calibration, motor RPM, diagnostics). Ray's METER-015 (Data Recording Completeness & Integrity Audit) expands to swarm: pre-flight checks all logging systems (per-aircraft + formation-level metrics), post-flight validates data integrity. Redundant logging systems reduce gap probability. Standardize forensic provenance (SHA-256 hashing, atomic-clock timestamps, digitally signed metadata). EUROCAE WG6 may develop guidance (check 2026 timeline). Compression post-flight for archive; real-time transmission via high-bandwidth link (not feasible for swarm-scale data).
qc_findings.cross_references: Alexa Scale: Complete recorded evidence all variables >= 10Hz, no gaps > 100ms; Brook benchmarks: Status 88% vs Tolerable 100%; Isaac Constraint: 100% required (binary: either fully logged or investigation compromised); Ray METER-015: Data Recording Completeness & Integrity Audit

top_priority_fixes: MLForensicExplainability (62% gap from Status to Ideal): Single-largest quality risk. Immediate action: mandate explainability approach by model type (decision trees 100%, SVMs feature importance + boundary, neural networks LIME/SHAP >= 80% coverage). Ray's METER-011 becomes standard gate. Freeze model development post-authorization. Industry adoption of explainability tooling required; AAIB acceptance of proxy explanations essential.

SwarmAwareSORAEvidence (65-point gap from Status to Tolerable): Regulatory integrity crisis. CAA publishes exemplary swarm SORA evidence packages (AMLAS case studies); conduct assessor training on JARUS SORA v2.5 swarm-risk taxonomy. Ray's METER-003 enforces minimum evidence (>= 3 distinct items per aircraft addressing loss-of-separation, emergent failure, coordination mechanism). Monthly CAA feedback loop accelerates adoption.

MeaningfulHumanControl (18-point gap from Status to Tolerable, 65-point to Ideal): Swarm workload overwhelms crews. HMI redesign critical: auto-triage alerts (1-2 concurrent max), progressive disclosure, swarm-level abstraction, pre-positioned crew takeover. Ray's METER-013 validates workload in simulation. NASA-TLX workload measurement mandatory. Crew training on multi-event prioritization required.

ConOpsClarity (30-point gap from Status to Tolerable): Inter-rater clarity deficient. Publish ConOps template with mandatory sections: nominal behavior quantified, worst-credible boundary explicit, recovery procedure per phase. Ray's METER-004 (kappa >= 0.8) becomes standard gate. Exemplar swarm ConOps library (AMLAS) + ASTM F3642 adoption.

DAAPerformance (11.9-point gap from Status to Regulatory minimum): BVLOS authorization impossible without 99.9% encounter resolution success. Algorithm development required: invest in non-cooperative radar detection (current 70-90%, target 99%+), sensor fusion. Ray's METER-008 runs >= 200 scenarios; analyze failures and iterate. Define swarm DAA protocol (coordinated resolution across formation).
quality_assessment.assessment_narrative: The vibe requirements specification achieves 63% overall completeness, below the 85% threshold for 'ready' exit recommendation. Quality posture is 'major_rework' (0.93 defects per spec; defect density moderate but concentrated in critical safety domains). The specification demonstrates strong architectural foundation: Alexa's 15 scales are well-operationalized with Planguage rigor, Ray's 19 meters span simulation-to-flight validation, Brook's benchmarks establish baselines and trends, Alan's stakeholder mappings identify ownership. However, execution maturity lags specification: the critical gap is REALIZATION—current Status values (Brook benchmarks) for 8 value tags are BELOW the Tolerable constraints Isaac defined. This indicates the specification is ambitious but not achievable with current industry practice. The single largest quality risk is MLForensicExplainability: Status (28%) is dangerously close to the Survival threshold (70%), creating regulatory integrity risk—AAIB will refuse to investigate black-box ML decisions post-incident, blocking authorization on AI-safety grounds. The second-largest risk is SwarmAwareSORAEvidence: Status (15% adoption) is far below Tolerable (80%), indicating swarm-specific risk assessment is not yet mainstream in the civil UAS regulatory ecosystem. These two defects alone threaten authorization viability for ML-based autonomous swarms. Moderately severe gaps exist in ConOpsClarity (30-point deficit), DAAPerformance (11.9-point regulatory shortfall), MeaningfulHumanControl (18-point workload deficit), and PublicEngagementCoverage (social-license risk). The specification is well-structured but describes aspirational targets that exceed current industry capability. The exit recommendation is 'major_rework' because: (a) completion deficits across 8 value tags require upstream engineering development (not just regulatory process), (b) social-license risk (PublicEngagementCoverage deprioritization) is unaddressed in original requirements, (c) novel domain maturity (SwarmEmergentBoundedness, PhasedGateReversibility) lacks industry practice baseline. Critical path to authorization is: (1) ML explainability tooling adoption (3-6 months), (2) swarm SORA taxonomy standardization (12-18 months), (3) ConOps clarity guidance and HMI design maturity (6-9 months), (4) public engagement elevation to mandatory criterion (immediate policy change). Without these upstream improvements, first swarm authorizations are blocked or stalled on safety/regulatory grounds.
